chunks.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography
chunks.content:
Term: Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)
Definition: Cryptographic algorithms designed to be secure against attacks by both classical and quantum computers.
Technical Note: PQC algorithms are based on mathematical problems believed to be hard for quantum computers, such as lattice problems, hash functions, and error-correcting codes.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Quantum Computing
chunks.content:
Term: Quantum Computing
Definition: A type of computing that uses quantum-mechanical phenomena like superposition and entanglement to perform calculations exponentially faster than classical computers for certain problems.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Q-Day
chunks.content:
Term: Q-Day
Definition: The hypothetical future date when a quantum computer becomes powerful enough to break current public-key cryptography (RSA, ECC).
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later
chunks.content:
Term: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL)
Definition: An attack strategy where adversaries collect encrypted data today with the intention of decrypting it once quantum computers become available.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Encryption
chunks.content:
Term: Encryption
Definition: The process of converting readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using an algorithm and a key, so only authorized parties can read it.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Digital Signature
chunks.content:
Term: Digital Signature
Definition: A mathematical scheme that proves the authenticity and integrity of a digital message or document, analogous to a handwritten signature.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Public Key
chunks.content:
Term: Public Key
Definition: A cryptographic key that can be shared openly. Used to encrypt data or verify digital signatures.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Private Key
chunks.content:
Term: Private Key
Definition: A cryptographic key kept secret by its owner. Used to decrypt data or create digital signatures.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Key Pair
chunks.content:
Term: Key Pair
Definition: A mathematically linked public key and private key used together for encryption/decryption or signing/verification.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Hybrid Cryptography
chunks.content:
Term: Hybrid Cryptography
Definition: Using both classical and post-quantum algorithms together during the transition period, providing security even if one algorithm is broken.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Crypto Agility
chunks.content:
Term: Crypto Agility
Definition: The ability of a system to quickly switch between cryptographic algorithms without major redesign, critical for adapting to PQC.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Qubit
chunks.content:
Term: Qubit
Definition: The basic unit of quantum information, analogous to a classical bit but capable of existing in superposition of 0 and 1 simultaneously.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Superposition
chunks.content:
Term: Superposition
Definition: A quantum property where a qubit can represent both 0 and 1 at the same time, enabling quantum computers to explore many solutions simultaneously.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Diffie-Hellman
chunks.content:
Term: Diffie-Hellman (DH)
Definition: The original public-key key exchange protocol, allowing two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel. Quantum-vulnerable.
Technical Note: Broken by quantum computers via Shor's algorithm. Being replaced by Key Encapsulation Mechanisms (KEMs) such as ML-KEM.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: RSA
chunks.content:
Term: RSA
Definition: A widely used classical public-key algorithm based on the difficulty of factoring large numbers. Vulnerable to quantum attacks via Shor's algorithm.
Technical Note: Common key sizes: 2048, 3072, 4096 bits. Being replaced by ML-DSA for signatures and ML-KEM for key exchange.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: DSA
chunks.content:
Term: DSA (DSA)
Definition: Digital Signature Algorithm, a classical FIPS 186 signature scheme based on discrete logarithms. Deprecated by NIST IR 8547 alongside RSA and ECDSA due to quantum vulnerability.
Technical Note: Broken by Shor's algorithm. Common key sizes: 1024–3072 bits. No longer approved for new systems after 2030 per NIST IR 8547.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Elliptic Curve Cryptography
chunks.content:
Term: Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)
Definition: A classical public-key algorithm using the mathematics of elliptic curves. More efficient than RSA but equally vulnerable to quantum attacks.
Technical Note: Common curves: P-256, P-384, P-521, secp256k1 (Bitcoin), Curve25519.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ML-KEM
chunks.content:
Term: ML-KEM
Definition: Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism, the NIST-standardized PQC algorithm for key exchange (formerly Kyber). Defined in FIPS 203.
Technical Note: Variants: ML-KEM-512 (128-bit security), ML-KEM-768 (192-bit), ML-KEM-1024 (256-bit).
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ML-DSA
chunks.content:
Term: ML-DSA
Definition: Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm, the NIST-standardized PQC algorithm for digital signatures (formerly Dilithium). Defined in FIPS 204.
Technical Note: Variants: ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87 corresponding to security levels 2, 3, 5.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: SLH-DSA
chunks.content:
Term: SLH-DSA
Definition: Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm, a conservative NIST-standardized hash-based PQC signature scheme (formerly SPHINCS+). Defined in FIPS 205.
Technical Note: 12 variants: combinations of SHA-256/SHAKE, 128/192/256-bit security, and fast/small trade-offs.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: FN-DSA
chunks.content:
Term: FN-DSA
Definition: FFT over NTRU-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm (formerly Falcon), a compact PQC signature scheme. Defined in FIPS 206, published October 2024.
Technical Note: Variants: FN-DSA-512 (Level 1) and FN-DSA-1024 (Level 5). Known for very small signatures but requires careful floating-point implementation.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: FrodoKEM
chunks.content:
Term: FrodoKEM
Definition: A conservative lattice-based KEM algorithm based on the "plain" Learning With Errors problem, offering strong security guarantees at the cost of larger key sizes.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: HQC
chunks.content:
Term: HQC
Definition: Hamming Quasi-Cyclic, a code-based KEM selected by NIST as an additional standard. Uses error-correcting codes for security.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Classic McEliece
chunks.content:
Term: Classic McEliece
Definition: A code-based KEM with very large public keys but extremely conservative security assumptions. One of the oldest post-quantum proposals.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: XMSS
chunks.content:
Term: XMSS (XMSS)
Definition: eXtended Merkle Signature Scheme, a stateful hash-based digital signature algorithm standardized in RFC 8391 and NIST SP 800-208.
Technical Note: Like LMS, XMSS is stateful and each key can only sign a limited number of messages. Required by CNSA 2.0 for firmware and software signing.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: LMS/HSS
chunks.content:
Term: LMS/HSS (LMS)
Definition: Leighton-Micali Signature / Hierarchical Signature System. A stateful hash-based signature scheme for firmware and software signing.
Technical Note: Standardized in RFC 8554. "Stateful" means each key can only sign a limited number of messages.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: AES
chunks.content:
Term: AES (AES)
Definition: Advanced Encryption Standard, a symmetric encryption algorithm. Relatively safe from quantum attacks (just double the key size: AES-256).
Technical Note: Quantum attacks via Grover's algorithm halve the effective security level, so AES-128 becomes ~64-bit. AES-256 remains secure.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Shor's Algorithm
chunks.content:
Term: Shor's Algorithm
Definition: A quantum algorithm that can efficiently factor large numbers and compute discrete logarithms, breaking RSA and ECC.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Grover's Algorithm
chunks.content:
Term: Grover's Algorithm
Definition: A quantum algorithm that speeds up brute-force searches, effectively halving the security level of symmetric ciphers and hash functions.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ECDSA
chunks.content:
Term: ECDSA (ECDSA)
Definition: Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm, a classical signature scheme used in TLS, Bitcoin, and many other systems. Quantum-vulnerable.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ECDH
chunks.content:
Term: ECDH (ECDH)
Definition: Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman, a classical key agreement protocol. Quantum-vulnerable and being replaced by ML-KEM.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: X25519
chunks.content:
Term: X25519
Definition: An elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman key exchange function using Curve25519. Widely used in TLS 1.3 and the basis for hybrid PQC key exchange.
Technical Note: Combined with ML-KEM-768 to form the X25519MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange, now deployed by major browsers and cloud providers.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: X448
chunks.content:
Term: X448
Definition: An elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman key exchange function using Curve448, providing higher security than X25519. Quantum-vulnerable.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Ed25519
chunks.content:
Term: Ed25519
Definition: A high-performance digital signature algorithm using the Edwards curve Curve25519. Used in SSH, TLS, and Solana.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Ed448
chunks.content:
Term: Ed448
Definition: An EdDSA signature algorithm using the Edwards curve Curve448, providing 224-bit security. Quantum-vulnerable.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: SHA-256
chunks.content:
Term: SHA-256
Definition: Secure Hash Algorithm producing a 256-bit digest. Used extensively in Bitcoin and TLS. Quantum-safe at current key sizes.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: HKDF
chunks.content:
Term: HKDF (HKDF)
Definition: HMAC-based Key Derivation Function, used to derive cryptographic keys from a shared secret. Essential in TLS 1.3.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: TLS
chunks.content:
Term: TLS (TLS)
Definition: Transport Layer Security, the protocol that secures HTTPS web traffic. TLS 1.3 is the current version.
Technical Note: PQC integration uses hybrid key exchange (e.g., X25519 + ML-KEM-768) to maintain backward compatibility.
Category: protocol | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: X.509
chunks.content:
Term: X.509
Definition: The standard format for public key certificates used in TLS, email signing, and code signing.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Public Key Infrastructure
chunks.content:
Term: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
Definition: The system of Certificate Authorities, digital certificates, and registration authorities that manages public keys and enables secure communication.
Category: protocol | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Certificate Signing Request
chunks.content:
Term: Certificate Signing Request (CSR)
Definition: A message sent to a Certificate Authority to request a digital certificate. Contains the public key and identity information.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Certificate Authority
chunks.content:
Term: Certificate Authority (CA)
Definition: A trusted organization that issues digital certificates, verifying the identity of certificate holders.
Category: protocol | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Key Encapsulation Mechanism
chunks.content:
Term: Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM)
Definition: A method to securely establish a shared secret key between two parties. The PQC replacement for traditional key exchange.
Technical Note: Unlike Diffie-Hellman, KEMs work via encapsulation (encrypt a random secret with a public key) and decapsulation (decrypt with the private key).
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: 5G-AKA
chunks.content:
Term: 5G-AKA
Definition: 5G Authentication and Key Agreement protocol, the primary authentication mechanism in 5G networks.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: SUCI
chunks.content:
Term: SUCI (SUCI)
Definition: Subscription Concealed Identifier, a privacy-protecting identifier used in 5G that encrypts the subscriber's permanent identity.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: OpenID4VCI
chunks.content:
Term: OpenID4VCI
Definition: OpenID for Verifiable Credential Issuance, a protocol for issuing digital credentials to wallets.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: OpenID4VP
chunks.content:
Term: OpenID4VP
Definition: OpenID for Verifiable Presentations, a protocol for presenting digital credentials from a wallet to a verifier.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: SSH
chunks.content:
Term: SSH (SSH)
Definition: Secure Shell, a protocol for encrypted remote login and command execution. PQC integration adds ML-KEM hybrid key exchange.
Category: protocol | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: IPsec
chunks.content:
Term: IPsec (IPsec)
Definition: Internet Protocol Security, a protocol suite for encrypting and authenticating IP traffic. Used in VPNs and site-to-site connections.
Technical Note: PQC integration via IKEv2 multiple key exchange (RFC 9370) adds ML-KEM alongside classical DH.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: IKEv2
chunks.content:
Term: IKEv2 (IKEv2)
Definition: Internet Key Exchange version 2, the protocol used to establish security associations in IPsec VPNs. PQC migration adds ML-KEM key exchange.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: EMV
chunks.content:
Term: EMV (EMV)
Definition: Europay/Mastercard/Visa, the global standard for chip-based payment card authentication. Uses RSA and ECC signatures that are quantum-vulnerable.
Technical Note: Over 14 billion EMV cards are in circulation globally. Offline authentication relies on RSA/ECC signatures vulnerable to quantum forgery.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: S/MIME
chunks.content:
Term: S/MIME (S/MIME)
Definition: Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions, a standard for email encryption and digital signing. PQC integration via ML-KEM and ML-DSA.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CMS
chunks.content:
Term: CMS (CMS)
Definition: Cryptographic Message Syntax, a standard for digitally signed, encrypted, or authenticated data. Foundation for S/MIME and code signing.
Technical Note: Multiple IETF RFCs define PQC integration: RFC 9629 (KEM in CMS), RFC 9882 (ML-DSA in CMS), RFC 9814 (SLH-DSA in CMS).
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: OpenPGP
chunks.content:
Term: OpenPGP
Definition: An open standard for email encryption and signing based on PGP. PQC integration adds composite ML-KEM+ECC encryption and ML-DSA+ECC signatures.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Signal Protocol
chunks.content:
Term: Signal Protocol
Definition: The encryption protocol used by Signal, WhatsApp, and other messengers. Updated with PQXDH to add ML-KEM-1024 for post-quantum security.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: PQXDH
chunks.content:
Term: PQXDH (PQXDH)
Definition: Post-Quantum Extended Diffie-Hellman, the post-quantum key agreement protocol used by Signal (and optionally WhatsApp). Combines X25519 with ML-KEM-1024 to establish session keys.
Technical Note: Deployed by Signal in September 2023. The shared secret is derived from both the classical X25519 exchange and an ML-KEM-1024 encapsulation, ensuring security if either algorithm is broken.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIDO2 / WebAuthn
chunks.content:
Term: FIDO2 / WebAuthn
Definition: Web authentication standards enabling passwordless login using public-key cryptography. Currently relies on quantum-vulnerable ECDSA/EdDSA.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: RFC 8446
chunks.content:
Term: RFC 8446
Definition: The IETF standard defining TLS version 1.3, published August 2018. TLS 1.3 removed legacy cipher suites, mandated forward secrecy, and reduced the handshake to 1 round-trip.
Technical Note: TLS 1.3 is the foundation for PQC key exchange integration. RFC 8446 defines the "key_share" extension that hybrid PQC groups (e.g., X25519MLKEM768) plug into without protocol changes.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIPS 203
chunks.content:
Term: FIPS 203
Definition: The NIST standard defining ML-KEM (Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism), published August 2024.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIPS 204
chunks.content:
Term: FIPS 204
Definition: The NIST standard defining ML-DSA (Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm), published August 2024.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIPS 205
chunks.content:
Term: FIPS 205
Definition: The NIST standard defining SLH-DSA (Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm), published August 2024.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIPS 140-3
chunks.content:
Term: FIPS 140-3
Definition: The U.S. government standard for validating cryptographic modules. Hardware and software must pass FIPS 140-3 testing to be used in federal systems.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CNSA 2.0
chunks.content:
Term: CNSA 2.0
Definition: NSA's Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0, mandating PQC adoption timelines for national security systems.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Common Criteria
chunks.content:
Term: Common Criteria
Definition: An international standard (ISO/IEC 15408) for evaluating the security properties of IT products, used globally for government procurement.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: eIDAS 2.0
chunks.content:
Term: eIDAS 2.0
Definition: The EU regulation establishing a framework for electronic identification and trust services, including the European Digital Identity Wallet.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NIS2 Directive
chunks.content:
Term: NIS2 Directive
Definition: The EU Network and Information Security Directive 2 (Directive 2022/2555), requiring operators of essential services and digital service providers to implement strong cryptographic controls. Transposed into national law across EU member states by October 2024.
Technical Note: NIS2 mandates risk-based security measures including encryption and cryptographic key management. ENISA guidance links NIS2 obligations directly to PQC migration timelines, making it a key compliance driver for EU organisations.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ACVP
chunks.content:
Term: ACVP (ACVP)
Definition: Automated Cryptographic Validation Protocol, used by NIST to test that cryptographic implementations produce correct results.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: PKCS#12
chunks.content:
Term: PKCS#12
Definition: A file format for storing a private key together with its X.509 certificate, commonly used for importing/exporting credentials.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIPS 206
chunks.content:
Term: FIPS 206
Definition: The NIST standard defining FN-DSA (FFT over NTRU-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm, formerly Falcon), published October 2024.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: FIPS 186-5
chunks.content:
Term: FIPS 186-5
Definition: The NIST Digital Signature Standard defining RSA, ECDSA, and EdDSA. Being deprecated in favor of PQC signature algorithms per NIST IR 8547.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NIST IR 8547
chunks.content:
Term: NIST IR 8547
Definition: NIST Internal Report establishing the PQC transition timeline: deprecation of classical asymmetric crypto by 2030, disallowment by 2035.
Technical Note: Covers RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, EdDSA, DH, and DSA. The most authoritative U.S. government PQC migration deadline document.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NIST SP 800-131A
chunks.content:
Term: NIST SP 800-131A
Definition: NIST Special Publication providing guidance on transitioning the use of cryptographic algorithms and key lengths. Defines which algorithms are approved, deprecated, or disallowed for federal use.
Technical Note: Revision 3 (2019) deprecated SHA-1 and 2TDEA. A future revision is expected to align with NIST IR 8547 timelines, formally disallowing RSA, ECDSA, and DH after 2035.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NIST SP 800-208
chunks.content:
Term: NIST SP 800-208
Definition: NIST recommendation for stateful hash-based signature schemes LMS and XMSS, used for firmware signing and secure boot.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: RFC 9629
chunks.content:
Term: RFC 9629
Definition: IETF standard defining how to use Key Encapsulation Mechanisms (KEMs) in the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) for encrypted messaging.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: PCI DSS
chunks.content:
Term: PCI DSS (PCI DSS)
Definition: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard, requiring strong cryptography for cardholder data protection. Quantum threatens its RSA/ECC foundations.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: HIPAA
chunks.content:
Term: HIPAA (HIPAA)
Definition: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, the U.S. law requiring encryption of protected health information. Patient data faces HNDL risk.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CMVP
chunks.content:
Term: CMVP (CMVP)
Definition: Cryptographic Module Validation Program, the NIST/CSE program that tests and certifies cryptographic modules for government use under FIPS 140-3.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: IEC 62443
chunks.content:
Term: IEC 62443
Definition: International standard series for industrial automation and control systems (IACS) cybersecurity. Critical for IoT and energy sector PQC migration.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: NIST
chunks.content:
Term: NIST (NIST)
Definition: National Institute of Standards and Technology, the U.S. agency leading PQC standardization through its Post-Quantum Cryptography project.
Category: organization | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: ETSI
chunks.content:
Term: ETSI (ETSI)
Definition: European Telecommunications Standards Institute, producing PQC migration guides and standards for European industry.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: IETF
chunks.content:
Term: IETF (IETF)
Definition: Internet Engineering Task Force, developing PQC integration into internet protocols like TLS, SSH, and IKEv2.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ANSSI
chunks.content:
Term: ANSSI (ANSSI)
Definition: French National Cybersecurity Agency, publishing PQC recommendations and cryptographic certifications for France and Europe.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: BSI
chunks.content:
Term: BSI (BSI)
Definition: German Federal Office for Information Security, providing PQC migration guidelines and recommendations for German government systems.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: 3GPP
chunks.content:
Term: 3GPP (3GPP)
Definition: The partnership project that develops specifications for mobile telecommunications including 5G security architecture.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NSA
chunks.content:
Term: NSA (NSA)
Definition: National Security Agency, the U.S. intelligence agency that publishes CNSA 2.0 and mandates PQC adoption timelines for national security systems.
Category: organization | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: CISA
chunks.content:
Term: CISA (CISA)
Definition: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, the U.S. agency coordinating PQC migration for critical infrastructure and publishing product category lists.
Category: organization | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: ENISA
chunks.content:
Term: ENISA (ENISA)
Definition: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity, publishing PQC guidelines and coordinating quantum-safe transitions across EU member states.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: GSMA
chunks.content:
Term: GSMA (GSMA)
Definition: Global System for Mobile Communications Association, leading the Post-Quantum Telco Network Taskforce for PQC integration in mobile networks.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CA/Browser Forum
chunks.content:
Term: CA/Browser Forum
Definition: An industry body of Certificate Authorities and browser vendors that governs Web PKI certificate standards, including PQC certificate adoption rules.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NCSC
chunks.content:
Term: NCSC (NCSC)
Definition: UK National Cyber Security Centre, publishing PQC migration guidance and a 2035 deadline for full transition to quantum-safe cryptography.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: OSCCA
chunks.content:
Term: OSCCA (OSCCA)
Definition: China's Office of State Commercial Cryptography Administration, managing China's independent cryptographic standards including SM2 and PQC competitions.
Category: organization | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: ISO/IEC JTC 1
chunks.content:
Term: ISO/IEC JTC 1
Definition: Joint Technical Committee 1 of ISO and IEC, the international standards body for information technology including cryptographic standards (SC 27).
Category: organization | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Lattice-Based Cryptography
chunks.content:
Term: Lattice-Based Cryptography
Definition: A family of PQC algorithms based on the hardness of problems involving mathematical lattices. The basis for ML-KEM and ML-DSA.
Technical Note: Key problems: Learning With Errors (LWE), Module-LWE, Ring-LWE. Offer good performance and key sizes.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Hash-Based Signatures
chunks.content:
Term: Hash-Based Signatures
Definition: Digital signature schemes whose security relies only on the properties of hash functions. Very conservative security but can be stateful.
Technical Note: Stateful: LMS/XMSS (must track which keys are used). Stateless: SLH-DSA/SPHINCS+ (larger signatures).
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Code-Based Cryptography
chunks.content:
Term: Code-Based Cryptography
Definition: PQC algorithms based on the hardness of decoding random linear error-correcting codes. Used by Classic McEliece and HQC.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Security Level
chunks.content:
Term: Security Level
Definition: NIST defines 5 security levels for PQC algorithms. Level 1 ≈ AES-128, Level 3 ≈ AES-192, Level 5 ≈ AES-256.
Technical Note: Level 1: comparable to breaking AES-128. Level 5: comparable to breaking AES-256. Most deployments use Level 3.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Key Exchange
chunks.content:
Term: Key Exchange
Definition: The process by which two parties establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel. In PQC, replaced by Key Encapsulation Mechanisms.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Quantum Advantage
chunks.content:
Term: Quantum Advantage
Definition: The point at which a quantum computer can solve a specific problem faster than any classical computer. Also called "quantum supremacy."
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: WebAssembly
chunks.content:
Term: WebAssembly (WASM)
Definition: A binary instruction format that enables near-native performance in web browsers. Used by PQC Today to run OpenSSL and liboqs in-browser.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: HSM
chunks.content:
Term: HSM (HSM)
Definition: Hardware Security Module, a dedicated physical device for managing and protecting cryptographic keys. Must be updated for PQC support.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Entanglement
chunks.content:
Term: Entanglement
Definition: A quantum phenomenon where two qubits become correlated so measuring one instantly determines the state of the other, regardless of distance.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: HD Wallet
chunks.content:
Term: HD Wallet (HD)
Definition: Hierarchical Deterministic Wallet, a cryptocurrency wallet that generates all key pairs from a single seed phrase using BIP32/39/44 standards.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: BIP39
chunks.content:
Term: BIP39
Definition: Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 39 defines mnemonic seed phrases (12 or 24 words) used to back up and restore cryptocurrency wallets.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: EUDI Wallet
chunks.content:
Term: EUDI Wallet
Definition: European Union Digital Identity Wallet, a mobile wallet for storing and presenting digital credentials under the eIDAS 2.0 framework.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Qualified Electronic Signature
chunks.content:
Term: Qualified Electronic Signature (QES)
Definition: A digital signature with the legal equivalence of a handwritten signature under EU law, requiring a qualified certificate and a secure creation device.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: MILENAGE
chunks.content:
Term: MILENAGE
Definition: The set of cryptographic functions (f1–f5) used in 5G authentication. Based on AES and generates authentication vectors.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Encapsulation / Decapsulation
chunks.content:
Term: Encapsulation / Decapsulation
Definition: The two operations of a Key Encapsulation Mechanism. Encapsulation creates a shared secret using a public key; decapsulation recovers it using the private key.
Technical Note: Unlike Diffie-Hellman key exchange, KEMs are asymmetric: the sender encapsulates, the receiver decapsulates. This is the core operation of ML-KEM.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Learning With Errors
chunks.content:
Term: Learning With Errors (LWE)
Definition: A mathematical problem believed to be hard for quantum computers, forming the security foundation of ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and FrodoKEM.
Technical Note: Variants include Ring-LWE (structured, faster) and Module-LWE (used in ML-KEM/ML-DSA, balancing security and performance).
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: NTRU Lattice
chunks.content:
Term: NTRU Lattice (NTRU)
Definition: A lattice-based cryptographic family using polynomial rings, providing the mathematical basis for FN-DSA (Falcon). One of the earliest PQC proposals (1996).
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Hybrid Key Exchange
chunks.content:
Term: Hybrid Key Exchange
Definition: Combining a classical key exchange (e.g., X25519) with a PQC KEM (e.g., ML-KEM-768) in a single handshake, ensuring security even if one algorithm is broken.
Technical Note: Deployed today as X25519MLKEM768 in Chrome, Firefox, and major cloud providers. IETF draft-ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem defines the TLS 1.3 integration.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Composite Certificate
chunks.content:
Term: Composite Certificate
Definition: An X.509 certificate encoding both a classical and a PQC key/signature under a single composite OID. Both signatures must verify. Requires all relying parties to support the composite OID — legacy-incompatible.
Technical Note: Defined in IETF drafts: composite ML-KEM (draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-kem) and composite ML-DSA (draft-ietf-lamps-pq-composite-sigs). OpenSSL 3.6 does not yet support composite OIDs natively.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Related Certificate
chunks.content:
Term: Related Certificate (RFC 9763)
Definition: A binding mechanism (RFC 9763) that links two independent X.509 certificates — one classical, one PQC — to the same end entity via a SHA-256 hash. Also known as the NSA "catalyst" approach. Legacy systems only process the classical cert; PQC-aware verifiers check the binding.
Technical Note: The RelatedCertificate extension (OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.35) carries a hash of the related cert and a pointer. Each certificate is individually valid, enabling backward compatibility — unlike composite certificates which require composite OID support.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Chameleon Certificate
chunks.content:
Term: Chameleon Certificate
Definition: A single X.509 certificate that encodes a classical "delta" variant inside a DeltaCertificateDescriptor extension (OID 2.16.840.1.114027.80.6.1). PQC-aware verifiers reconstruct the classical certificate from the delta; legacy systems see a standard classical cert.
Technical Note: Defined in draft-bonnell-lamps-chameleon-certs (DigiCert, Entrust). More bandwidth-efficient than related certs (RFC 9763) because only one certificate is transmitted. However, as a draft standard, adoption is still emerging.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: TBSCertificate
chunks.content:
Term: TBSCertificate
Definition: "To Be Signed" certificate — the core unsigned structure inside an X.509 certificate containing version, serial number, issuer/subject DNs, validity period, SubjectPublicKeyInfo, and extensions. The CA signs this structure to produce the final certificate.
Technical Note: In DER encoding, TBSCertificate is a SEQUENCE. The outer Certificate SEQUENCE wraps TBSCertificate + signatureAlgorithm + signatureValue. PQC algorithms like ML-DSA and SLH-DSA are specified in the outer signatureAlgorithm AlgorithmIdentifier.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: AlgorithmIdentifier
chunks.content:
Term: AlgorithmIdentifier
Definition: An ASN.1 SEQUENCE containing an OID that identifies a cryptographic algorithm, plus optional parameters. Appears in X.509 certificates to specify signature algorithms (signatureAlgorithm field) and public key algorithms (SubjectPublicKeyInfo.algorithm).
Technical Note: PQC algorithms defined in FIPS 203/204/205 use absent parameters (no NULL) per their respective X.509 profile RFCs (RFC 9881, RFC 9909). The OID for ML-DSA-65 is 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.18; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s is 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.20.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Hybrid Certificate
chunks.content:
Term: Hybrid Certificate
Definition: An X.509 certificate using both classical and PQC algorithms during the transition period, maintaining interoperability with legacy systems.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: SCADA
chunks.content:
Term: SCADA (SCADA)
Definition: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition, industrial control systems used in power grids, water treatment, and manufacturing. Quantum-vulnerable via legacy crypto.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: V2X Communication
chunks.content:
Term: V2X Communication (V2X)
Definition: Vehicle-to-Everything communication, enabling cars to exchange safety data with infrastructure and other vehicles. Uses ECDSA P-256 certificates that are quantum-vulnerable.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: CBDC
chunks.content:
Term: CBDC (CBDC)
Definition: Central Bank Digital Currency, a digital form of fiat money issued by central banks. Requires quantum-safe cryptography for long-term trust.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: CBOM
chunks.content:
Term: CBOM (CBOM)
Definition: Cryptographic Bill of Materials, a comprehensive inventory of all cryptographic algorithms, keys, and certificates used in an organization or product.
Technical Note: Essential first step in PQC migration. Enables organizations to identify quantum-vulnerable crypto and prioritize replacements.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: DRM
chunks.content:
Term: DRM (DRM)
Definition: Digital Rights Management, technology protecting copyrighted content through encryption. Systems like Widevine, PlayReady, and FairPlay face quantum threats to their key hierarchies.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: KMS
chunks.content:
Term: KMS (KMS)
Definition: Key Management System, software for generating, storing, rotating, and auditing cryptographic keys. Must be upgraded to support PQC key sizes and algorithms.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: TPM
chunks.content:
Term: TPM (TPM)
Definition: Trusted Platform Module, a hardware chip providing secure key storage, platform integrity measurement, and cryptographic operations. PQC support being added via TCG specifications.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Code Signing
chunks.content:
Term: Code Signing
Definition: Digitally signing software executables and scripts to verify their authenticity and integrity. PQC migration replaces RSA/ECDSA signatures with ML-DSA or SLH-DSA.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Secure Boot
chunks.content:
Term: Secure Boot
Definition: A hardware-enforced chain of trust that verifies each component in the boot process is signed by a trusted authority. Requires PQC-capable signature verification.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: UEFI
chunks.content:
Term: UEFI (UEFI)
Definition: Unified Extensible Firmware Interface — the modern firmware interface between hardware and OS. UEFI Secure Boot enforces signature verification on bootloaders and drivers before execution.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: QKD
chunks.content:
Term: QKD (QKD)
Definition: Quantum Key Distribution, a method of distributing encryption keys using quantum physics rather than mathematical hardness. Distinct from PQC and requires specialized hardware.
Technical Note: QKD (e.g., BB84 protocol) uses photons to detect eavesdropping. Unlike PQC, it requires dedicated fiber or satellite links and cannot run on standard networks.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: BB84
chunks.content:
Term: BB84
Definition: The first quantum key distribution protocol, proposed by Bennett and Brassard in 1984. Uses four quantum states in two conjugate bases to establish a shared secret key.
Technical Note: Alice encodes random bits in rectilinear (+) or diagonal (x) bases. Bob measures in a randomly chosen basis. After basis reconciliation over a classical channel, only matching-basis bits form the sifted key.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: QBER
chunks.content:
Term: QBER (QBER)
Definition: Quantum Bit Error Rate, the fraction of sifted key bits where Alice and Bob disagree despite using the same measurement basis. A QBER above ~11% indicates eavesdropping.
Technical Note: In BB84, an eavesdropper measuring in the wrong basis introduces a 25% error rate on intercepted qubits. The theoretical QBER threshold for secure key generation is approximately 11%.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Sifted Key
chunks.content:
Term: Sifted Key
Definition: The subset of raw key bits where both parties used the same measurement basis during QKD. Typically about half the transmitted bits survive sifting.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Privacy Amplification
chunks.content:
Term: Privacy Amplification
Definition: A post-processing step in QKD that uses universal hash functions to distill a shorter, provably secret key from a partially compromised key by removing any information an eavesdropper may have obtained.
Technical Note: The output key length depends on the estimated information leakage. Uses hash functions (e.g., SHA-256) to compress the corrected key, reducing Eve's knowledge to negligible levels.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: No-Cloning Theorem
chunks.content:
Term: No-Cloning Theorem
Definition: A fundamental quantum mechanics result proving it is impossible to create an identical copy of an unknown quantum state, providing the theoretical basis for QKD security.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Trusted Node
chunks.content:
Term: Trusted Node
Definition: A physically secured relay point in a QKD network where keys are decrypted and re-encrypted to extend range beyond single-link limits (typically ~100 km for fiber).
Technical Note: Trusted nodes are a practical necessity but a security concern: compromising any node reveals all keys routed through it. This is a major architectural limitation of current QKD networks.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: EuroQCI
chunks.content:
Term: EuroQCI (EuroQCI)
Definition: The European Quantum Communication Infrastructure initiative, a pan-EU program to deploy a quantum communication network across all 27 member states combining terrestrial fiber and satellite links.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Cryptographic Inventory
chunks.content:
Term: Cryptographic Inventory
Definition: A comprehensive catalog of all cryptographic algorithms, protocols, keys, and certificates in an organization, essential for planning PQC migration.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: OTA Updates
chunks.content:
Term: OTA Updates (OTA)
Definition: Over-the-Air updates, the process of remotely delivering firmware or software to devices. Signature verification must be quantum-safe to prevent forgery.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: EHR
chunks.content:
Term: EHR (EHR)
Definition: Electronic Health Records, digital patient medical records that require long-term confidentiality. Prime targets for Harvest Now, Decrypt Later attacks.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: SBOM
chunks.content:
Term: SBOM (SBOM)
Definition: Software Bill of Materials, a formal inventory of all components in a software product. Increasingly required alongside CBOM for security compliance.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ADS-B
chunks.content:
Term: ADS-B (ADS-B)
Definition: Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast, the primary aircraft surveillance technology. Currently lacks cryptographic authentication, making it a quantum-era concern.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: TEE
chunks.content:
Term: TEE (TEE)
Definition: Trusted Execution Environment, an isolated processing environment on a processor that protects code and data. Must be updated for PQC algorithm support.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: DUKPT
chunks.content:
Term: DUKPT (DUKPT)
Definition: Derived Unique Key Per Transaction, a key management scheme for payment terminals that derives a unique encryption key for each transaction. Currently uses Triple-DES.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Keccak-256
chunks.content:
Term: Keccak-256
Definition: The hash function used by Ethereum, based on the SHA-3 algorithm family. Produces 256-bit digests.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: secp256k1
chunks.content:
Term: secp256k1
Definition: The elliptic curve used by Bitcoin for digital signatures. Quantum-vulnerable and a key concern for cryptocurrency PQC migration.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Kyber
chunks.content:
Term: Kyber
Definition: The original name (CRYSTALS-Kyber) for the lattice-based KEM standardized by NIST as ML-KEM in FIPS 203. Often still referenced in older documents and libraries.
Technical Note: Kyber was selected from the NIST PQC competition Round 3. It was renamed to ML-KEM for the final standard.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Dilithium
chunks.content:
Term: Dilithium
Definition: The original name (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) for the lattice-based signature scheme standardized by NIST as ML-DSA in FIPS 204.
Technical Note: Dilithium was selected from the NIST PQC competition Round 3. It was renamed to ML-DSA for the final standard.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Falcon
chunks.content:
Term: Falcon
Definition: The original name for the NTRU-lattice-based signature scheme standardized by NIST as FN-DSA in the draft FIPS 206. Known for compact signatures.
Technical Note: Falcon uses fast Fourier transforms over NTRU lattices. Renamed to FN-DSA for the final standard.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: SPHINCS+
chunks.content:
Term: SPHINCS+
Definition: The original name for the stateless hash-based signature scheme standardized by NIST as SLH-DSA in FIPS 205. Very conservative security assumptions.
Technical Note: SPHINCS+ relies only on the security of its underlying hash function, making it a strong fallback if lattice assumptions are broken.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: BIKE
chunks.content:
Term: BIKE (BIKE)
Definition: Bit Flipping Key Encapsulation, a code-based KEM that was a NIST PQC Round 4 candidate. Uses quasi-cyclic moderate-density parity-check codes.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: SHA-3
chunks.content:
Term: SHA-3
Definition: The third generation Secure Hash Algorithm standard (FIPS 202), based on the Keccak sponge construction. Includes SHA3-256, SHA3-512, and SHAKE variants.
Technical Note: SHA-3 provides an independent alternative to SHA-2 with different mathematical foundations. Used in SLH-DSA parameter sets.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: SHAKE
chunks.content:
Term: SHAKE
Definition: Extendable-Output Functions from the SHA-3 family (SHAKE128, SHAKE256). Unlike fixed-length hashes, SHAKE can produce output of any desired length.
Technical Note: Used in SLH-DSA-SHAKE parameter sets and as a key derivation mechanism in several PQC algorithms.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: EdDSA
chunks.content:
Term: EdDSA
Definition: Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm, the family of signature schemes including Ed25519 and Ed448. Offers high performance and resistance to side-channel attacks.
Technical Note: EdDSA is deterministic (no random nonce), reducing implementation risks. Quantum-vulnerable via Shor's algorithm.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ChaCha20-Poly1305
chunks.content:
Term: ChaCha20-Poly1305
Definition: A modern authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD) cipher combining the ChaCha20 stream cipher with the Poly1305 authenticator. Quantum-safe at current key sizes.
Technical Note: Widely used in TLS 1.3, WireGuard, and SSH as an alternative to AES-GCM. Efficient in software without hardware AES acceleration.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Triple-DES
chunks.content:
Term: Triple-DES (3DES)
Definition: A legacy symmetric cipher that applies DES three times to each block. Being phased out in favor of AES due to its small 64-bit block size.
Technical Note: Still found in legacy payment systems (DUKPT) and some government systems. NIST deprecated 3DES after December 31, 2023.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: P-256
chunks.content:
Term: P-256
Definition: A NIST standard elliptic curve (also called secp256r1 or prime256v1) widely used in TLS, FIDO2, and government systems. Quantum-vulnerable.
Technical Note: Part of the Suite B / CNSA 1.0 algorithms. Combined with ML-KEM-768 in the SecP256r1MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: P-384
chunks.content:
Term: P-384
Definition: A NIST standard elliptic curve (secp384r1) offering 192-bit security. Used in high-security TLS configurations and government systems. Quantum-vulnerable.
Technical Note: Combined with ML-KEM-1024 in the SecP384r1MLKEM1024 hybrid key exchange for NIST Level 5 security.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Ascon
chunks.content:
Term: Ascon
Definition: A lightweight authenticated encryption cipher selected by NIST as the standard for constrained environments (IoT, embedded devices). Very low resource requirements.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: RIPEMD-160
chunks.content:
Term: RIPEMD-160
Definition: A 160-bit cryptographic hash function used in Bitcoin address generation (combined with SHA-256). Quantum-safe at current output sizes.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: SM2
chunks.content:
Term: SM2
Definition: China's national standard elliptic curve public-key algorithm for digital signatures and key exchange, managed by OSCCA. Quantum-vulnerable.
Technical Note: Combined with ML-KEM-768 in the curveSM2MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange for Chinese PQC migration.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: PBKDF2
chunks.content:
Term: PBKDF2
Definition: Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2, used to derive cryptographic keys from passwords. Applies a pseudorandom function (typically HMAC-SHA) iteratively.
Technical Note: Used in BIP39 to derive seed bytes from mnemonic phrases (2048 iterations of HMAC-SHA512).
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: QUIC
chunks.content:
Term: QUIC
Definition: A modern transport protocol built on UDP, providing encrypted connections with lower latency than TCP+TLS. The foundation of HTTP/3.
Technical Note: QUIC integrates TLS 1.3 directly, so PQC migration via hybrid key exchange (X25519MLKEM768) applies to QUIC connections as well.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: WireGuard
chunks.content:
Term: WireGuard
Definition: A modern, high-performance VPN protocol using state-of-the-art cryptography (ChaCha20, Curve25519). PQC integration is being developed.
Technical Note: PQC migration involves adding ML-KEM hybrid key exchange to the Noise protocol framework used by WireGuard.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: DTLS
chunks.content:
Term: DTLS (DTLS)
Definition: Datagram Transport Layer Security, the UDP equivalent of TLS. Used in IoT (CoAP), WebRTC, and VPN protocols. DTLS 1.3 adds PQC support.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: mTLS
chunks.content:
Term: mTLS
Definition: Mutual TLS, a TLS mode where both client and server present certificates for authentication. Common in microservices, zero-trust architectures, and API security.
Technical Note: PQC migration for mTLS requires updating both client and server certificates to PQC or hybrid algorithms.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: OCSP
chunks.content:
Term: OCSP (OCSP)
Definition: Online Certificate Status Protocol, used to check whether an X.509 certificate has been revoked. An alternative to Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs).
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: DNSSEC
chunks.content:
Term: DNSSEC (DNSSEC)
Definition: DNS Security Extensions, a suite of protocols that add cryptographic authentication to DNS responses. Uses digital signatures to prevent DNS spoofing.
Technical Note: PQC migration is challenging due to DNS packet size limits. Larger PQC signatures may require protocol changes or new transport mechanisms.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ACME
chunks.content:
Term: ACME (ACME)
Definition: Automatic Certificate Management Environment, the protocol used by Let's Encrypt and other CAs to automate certificate issuance and renewal.
Technical Note: ACME will need updates to support PQC certificate types as the Web PKI transitions to quantum-safe algorithms.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: PQ3
chunks.content:
Term: PQ3
Definition: Apple's post-quantum secure messaging protocol for iMessage, providing Level 3 security with ML-KEM key exchange and periodic re-keying.
Technical Note: PQ3 uses a ratcheting design that regularly re-establishes quantum-safe shared secrets, protecting past and future messages.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: MACsec
chunks.content:
Term: MACsec (MACsec)
Definition: IEEE 802.1AE Media Access Control Security, providing Layer 2 encryption for Ethernet frames. Used to secure LAN traffic between switches and endpoints.
Technical Note: MACsec relies on AES-GCM (quantum-safe) for encryption but uses ECDH/ECDSA for key agreement, requiring PQC migration for the key exchange.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: KMIP
chunks.content:
Term: KMIP (KMIP)
Definition: Key Management Interoperability Protocol, an OASIS standard for communication between key management systems and cryptographic clients.
Technical Note: KMIP 2.1+ includes support for post-quantum key types and algorithms.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: CMP
chunks.content:
Term: CMP (CMP)
Definition: Certificate Management Protocol (RFC 4210), used for managing X.509 certificates including enrollment, updates, and revocation. PQC support via RFC 9810.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: JWT
chunks.content:
Term: JWT (JWT)
Definition: JSON Web Token, a compact token format for securely transmitting claims between parties. Widely used in API authentication and OAuth 2.0.
Technical Note: JWTs typically use RSA or ECDSA signatures which are quantum-vulnerable. PQC JWT support (ML-DSA) is being standardized via JOSE working group.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: JSON Web Signature
chunks.content:
Term: JSON Web Signature (JWS)
Definition: A standard (RFC 7515) for digitally signing JSON payloads. JWS is the mechanism that makes JWTs tamper-proof — the signature covers the JOSE header and payload.
Technical Note: PQC migration requires new JWS algorithm identifiers (e.g., ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65) registered via the IETF JOSE PQC draft. Signature sizes grow from 64 bytes (ES256) to ~2,420 bytes (ML-DSA-44).
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: JSON Web Encryption
chunks.content:
Term: JSON Web Encryption (JWE)
Definition: A standard (RFC 7516) for encrypting JSON payloads. JWE provides confidentiality for JWT claims using a two-layer encryption model: a Key Encryption Key (KEK) wraps a Content Encryption Key (CEK).
Technical Note: PQC migration replaces ECDH-ES key agreement with ML-KEM encapsulation. The KEM ciphertext replaces the encrypted key in JWE compact serialization.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: JOSE
chunks.content:
Term: JOSE
Definition: JSON Object Signing and Encryption, the IETF framework encompassing JWS, JWE, JWK, and JWA specifications. JOSE defines how to sign, encrypt, and represent cryptographic keys in JSON format.
Technical Note: The IETF JOSE working group is standardizing PQC algorithm identifiers (ML-DSA, ML-KEM, SLH-DSA) for use in JOSE headers. The framework was designed for algorithm agility — only the "alg" header value changes.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: OpenID Connect
chunks.content:
Term: OpenID Connect (OIDC)
Definition: An identity layer built on OAuth 2.0 that provides authentication via ID tokens (JWTs). Widely used for single sign-on across web and mobile applications.
Technical Note: OIDC ID tokens are signed JWTs. PQC migration requires authorization servers to sign ID tokens with ML-DSA and clients to verify PQC signatures. JWKS endpoints must serve PQC public keys.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession
chunks.content:
Term: Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession (DPoP)
Definition: An OAuth 2.0 extension (RFC 9449) that binds access tokens to a specific client by requiring a proof-of-possession JWT with each API request, preventing token theft and replay attacks.
Technical Note: Each DPoP proof is a JWS signed with the client's key. With PQC, every API request includes a ~4 KB ML-DSA signature in addition to the access token, potentially exceeding HTTP header size limits.
Category: protocol | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: JSON Web Key Set
chunks.content:
Term: JSON Web Key Set (JWKS)
Definition: A JSON document (RFC 7517) containing a set of public keys used to verify JWTs. Authorization servers publish JWKS endpoints so resource servers can fetch signing keys.
Technical Note: ML-DSA public keys are ~2 KB each (vs 32 bytes for EC). JWKS payloads grow significantly during PQC migration, especially with key rotation maintaining multiple active keys.
Category: protocol | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Root CA
chunks.content:
Term: Root CA
Definition: The top-level Certificate Authority in a PKI trust hierarchy. A Root CA issues a self-signed certificate that serves as the trust anchor — all certificates in the chain ultimately derive their trust from it.
Technical Note: Root CAs are installed in OS and browser trust stores. PQC migration for Root CAs is highest priority since a compromised root breaks the entire chain. CNSA 2.0 requires Root CAs to use ML-DSA-87 by 2030.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Sigstore
chunks.content:
Term: Sigstore
Definition: An open-source project (Linux Foundation) for keyless code signing. Developers authenticate with their existing identity (GitHub, Google) and receive short-lived certificates, eliminating long-term key management.
Technical Note: Sigstore centralizes crypto in two services: Fulcio (CA) and Rekor (transparency log). Upgrading these to ML-DSA automatically provides PQC protection to all downstream ecosystems (npm, PyPI, Kubernetes).
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Fulcio
chunks.content:
Term: Fulcio
Definition: The Certificate Authority component of Sigstore. Fulcio issues short-lived (~20 minute) X.509 certificates that bind an OIDC identity to an ephemeral signing key.
Technical Note: Fulcio is the primary PQC upgrade point in the Sigstore ecosystem. Migrating Fulcio to ML-DSA certificates automatically protects all downstream consumers without developer intervention.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Rekor
chunks.content:
Term: Rekor
Definition: The transparency log component of Sigstore. Rekor is an immutable, append-only log that records every signing event — signature, certificate, and artifact hash — for public auditability.
Technical Note: PQC migration increases Rekor entry sizes significantly (~200 bytes for ECDSA vs ~3,600 bytes for ML-DSA-65). Storage overhead is the primary cost of PQC migration in Sigstore.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Keyless Signing
chunks.content:
Term: Keyless Signing
Definition: A code signing approach where no long-term private keys are managed by the developer. Identity-based trust (via OIDC) replaces key-based trust, with ephemeral keys generated per-signing event.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Time Stamping Authority
chunks.content:
Term: Time Stamping Authority (TSA)
Definition: A trusted service (RFC 3161) that proves a digital signature was created at a specific time. Essential for code signing, where signatures must remain valid after certificate expiration.
Technical Note: PQC migration requires TSAs to issue timestamps signed with quantum-safe algorithms. A quantum-vulnerable timestamp means an attacker could backdate forged signatures.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Extended Key Usage
chunks.content:
Term: Extended Key Usage (EKU)
Definition: An X.509 certificate extension that restricts the purposes for which a certificate can be used — for example, Code Signing (OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.3), Server Authentication, or Client Authentication.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Software Supply Chain
chunks.content:
Term: Software Supply Chain
Definition: The entire pipeline from source code to deployed software, including dependencies, build systems, artifact registries, signing infrastructure, and distribution channels. Every link in this chain is a potential attack surface.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: SLSA
chunks.content:
Term: SLSA (SLSA)
Definition: Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts — a security framework (originally from Google) that defines increasing levels of supply chain integrity guarantees, from basic build provenance to fully hermetic, reproducible builds.
Technical Note: SLSA levels depend on cryptographic attestations signed during the build process. If those signatures use quantum-vulnerable algorithms (ECDSA, RSA), the entire SLSA guarantee collapses once quantum computers can forge attestations.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: in-toto
chunks.content:
Term: in-toto
Definition: An open-source framework for securing the integrity of software supply chains by defining and cryptographically verifying each step of the build and release process. Each step produces a signed attestation (a "link") forming a verifiable chain.
Technical Note: in-toto link metadata is signed with the functionary's private key. PQC migration requires upgrading all functionary keys to ML-DSA to prevent quantum forgery of supply chain attestations.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Build Provenance
chunks.content:
Term: Build Provenance
Definition: A cryptographically signed attestation documenting how a software artifact was built — including the source repository, build system, entry point, and build parameters. Answers the question "who built this and how?"
Technical Note: Build provenance is a core requirement of SLSA Level 1+. Without quantum-safe signatures on provenance attestations, an adversary with a quantum computer could forge provenance for malicious artifacts.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NIST SP 800-227
chunks.content:
Term: NIST SP 800-227
Definition: NIST Special Publication providing recommendations for Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM) implementation, including guidance on parameter selection and secure use of ML-KEM.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: NIST IR 8545
chunks.content:
Term: NIST IR 8545
Definition: NIST Internal Report documenting the selection of HQC as an additional KEM standard, providing a code-based alternative to lattice-based ML-KEM.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: RFC 9370
chunks.content:
Term: RFC 9370
Definition: IETF standard defining multiple key exchanges in IKEv2, enabling hybrid classical + PQC key exchange for IPsec VPNs.
Technical Note: Allows adding ML-KEM alongside classical Diffie-Hellman in a single IKEv2 handshake without breaking backward compatibility.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: RFC 9881
chunks.content:
Term: RFC 9881
Definition: IETF standard defining how to use ML-DSA digital signatures in X.509 certificates and Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs).
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: RFC 9882
chunks.content:
Term: RFC 9882
Definition: IETF standard defining the use of ML-DSA signatures in the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS), enabling PQC-signed messages and documents.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: ISO/IEC 18013-5
chunks.content:
Term: ISO/IEC 18013-5
Definition: International standard for mobile driving licences (mDL), defining the data model, security mechanisms, and device engagement protocols for digital identity documents.
Category: standard | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: BSI TR-02102
chunks.content:
Term: BSI TR-02102
Definition: German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Technical Guideline series on cryptographic recommendations, covering TLS, IPsec, SSH, and general algorithms.
Technical Note: TR-02102-1 covers general recommendations; TR-02102-2 through TR-02102-4 provide protocol-specific PQC migration guidance.
Category: standard | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: PQCA
chunks.content:
Term: PQCA (PQCA)
Definition: Post-Quantum Cryptographic Alliance, a Linux Foundation project bringing together industry and academia to advance PQC adoption through open-source software.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: OQS
chunks.content:
Term: OQS (OQS)
Definition: Open Quantum Safe, an open-source project providing C library (liboqs) implementations of post-quantum algorithms and integrations with OpenSSL, BoringSSL, and other libraries.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ASD
chunks.content:
Term: ASD (ASD)
Definition: Australian Signals Directorate, Australia's national cybersecurity agency. Published aggressive PQC migration timelines with a 2030 deadline for full transition — five years ahead of the US.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CCCS
chunks.content:
Term: CCCS (CCCS)
Definition: Canadian Centre for Cyber Security, Canada's technical authority on cybersecurity. Published the ITSM.40.001 PQC migration roadmap for the Government of Canada.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CRYPTREC
chunks.content:
Term: CRYPTREC (CRYPTREC)
Definition: Cryptography Research and Evaluation Committees, Japan's organization for evaluating and recommending cryptographic algorithms for government use.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: KpqC
chunks.content:
Term: KpqC (KpqC)
Definition: Korean Post-Quantum Cryptography project, South Korea's national initiative to develop and standardize its own PQC algorithms including HAETAE, AIMer, SMAUG-T, and NTRU+.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: ITU-T
chunks.content:
Term: ITU-T (ITU-T)
Definition: International Telecommunication Union — Telecommunication Standardization Sector. Study Group 17 (SG17) is developing PQC standards for telecommunications infrastructure.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: NCCoE
chunks.content:
Term: NCCoE (NCCoE)
Definition: NIST National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence, collaborating with industry to develop practical PQC migration guidance and reference architectures.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: BIS
chunks.content:
Term: BIS (BIS)
Definition: Bank for International Settlements, the international financial institution that published the quantum-readiness roadmap (Paper 158) for the global financial system.
Category: organization | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: CACR
chunks.content:
Term: CACR (CACR)
Definition: Chinese Association for Cryptologic Research, the organization that ran China's independent PQC competition and is shaping the NGCC (Next-Generation Commercial Cryptographic) standards program.
Category: organization | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: CRQC
chunks.content:
Term: CRQC (CRQC)
Definition: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer, a hypothetical future quantum computer powerful enough to break RSA, ECC, and other classical public-key cryptography.
Technical Note: Estimates vary, but a CRQC would need thousands of stable logical qubits. Most timelines estimate 2030-2040 for CRQC capability.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Forward Secrecy
chunks.content:
Term: Forward Secrecy
Definition: A property of key exchange protocols where compromise of long-term keys does not compromise past session keys. Each session uses ephemeral keys that are discarded.
Technical Note: Also called Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS). Critical for TLS 1.3 and a reason hybrid PQC key exchange is preferred over static key transport.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Side-Channel Attack
chunks.content:
Term: Side-Channel Attack
Definition: An attack that exploits physical characteristics of a cryptographic implementation (timing, power consumption, electromagnetic emissions) rather than mathematical weaknesses.
Technical Note: PQC algorithms like FN-DSA require careful floating-point implementation to resist side-channel attacks. Constant-time implementations are essential.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Certificate Chain
chunks.content:
Term: Certificate Chain
Definition: A linked sequence of X.509 certificates from a leaf (end-entity) certificate up through intermediates to a trusted root CA, establishing a chain of trust.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Root of Trust
chunks.content:
Term: Root of Trust
Definition: A foundational component (hardware or software) that is inherently trusted and used as the starting point for a chain of trust in a system.
Technical Note: PQC migration requires updating roots of trust (TPM, secure boot firmware, root CA certificates) to quantum-safe algorithms.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Key Rotation
chunks.content:
Term: Key Rotation
Definition: The practice of periodically replacing cryptographic keys with new ones, limiting the exposure window if a key is compromised.
Technical Note: PQC migration involves key rotation from classical to hybrid or pure PQC keys across all cryptographic infrastructure.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Non-Repudiation
chunks.content:
Term: Non-Repudiation
Definition: A property of digital signatures that prevents a signer from denying they signed a message. Essential for legal documents, contracts, and financial transactions.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: SD-JWT
chunks.content:
Term: SD-JWT (SD-JWT)
Definition: Selective Disclosure JSON Web Token, a credential format that allows holders to reveal only specific claims from a signed token without exposing all data.
Technical Note: Used in the EUDI Wallet ecosystem alongside mdoc format. Each claim is individually salted and hashed for privacy.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: mDL
chunks.content:
Term: mDL (mDL)
Definition: Mobile Driver's License, a digital credential stored on a smartphone conforming to ISO/IEC 18013-5. Uses mdoc format with cryptographic device binding.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: mdoc
chunks.content:
Term: mdoc
Definition: Mobile Document, a CBOR-based verifiable credential format defined in ISO/IEC 18013-5. Uses Mobile Security Objects (MSO) for issuer-signed data integrity.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: CBOR
chunks.content:
Term: CBOR (CBOR)
Definition: Concise Binary Object Representation, a compact binary data serialization format (RFC 8949). Used in mdoc credentials, IoT protocols, and WebAuthn attestations.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Verifiable Credential
chunks.content:
Term: Verifiable Credential (VC)
Definition: A tamper-proof digital credential that can be cryptographically verified. Issued by a trusted authority and held in a digital wallet for presentation to verifiers.
Technical Note: W3C Verifiable Credentials and ISO mdoc are the two major formats. PQC migration requires updating the signature algorithms used by issuers.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Selective Disclosure
chunks.content:
Term: Selective Disclosure
Definition: The ability to reveal only specific attributes from a digital credential without exposing all contained data. A key privacy feature of SD-JWT and mdoc formats.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: BIP32
chunks.content:
Term: BIP32
Definition: Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 32, defining Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) wallets. Enables deriving an entire tree of key pairs from a single master seed.
Technical Note: Uses HMAC-SHA512 for key derivation. Supports hardened and normal derivation paths (e.g., m/44'/0'/0'/0/0).
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: UTXO
chunks.content:
Term: UTXO (UTXO)
Definition: Unspent Transaction Output, Bitcoin's model for tracking coin ownership. Each transaction consumes existing UTXOs and creates new ones.
Technical Note: PQC migration for Bitcoin must handle UTXOs locked by quantum-vulnerable ECDSA scripts, potentially requiring a network-wide migration.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: SUPI
chunks.content:
Term: SUPI (SUPI)
Definition: Subscription Permanent Identifier, a 5G subscriber's permanent identity (usually based on IMSI). Protected by encrypting it as SUCI before transmission.
Technical Note: SUCI conceals the SUPI using ECIES (Profile A/B) or ML-KEM (Profile C) to prevent IMSI-catching attacks.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Fully Homomorphic Encryption
chunks.content:
Term: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)
Definition: An encryption scheme that allows computations to be performed on ciphertext, producing encrypted results that match operations on the plaintext.
Technical Note: FHE is lattice-based and believed to be quantum-safe. Used for privacy-preserving cloud computing, but remains computationally expensive.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Zero-Knowledge Proof
chunks.content:
Term: Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP)
Definition: A cryptographic method where one party proves knowledge of a value without revealing the value itself. Used in privacy-preserving authentication and blockchain.
Technical Note: PQC-compatible ZKP constructions (e.g., lattice-based, hash-based) are active areas of research for quantum-safe privacy systems.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: DER
chunks.content:
Term: DER (DER)
Definition: Distinguished Encoding Rules, a binary encoding format for ASN.1 data structures. The standard encoding for X.509 certificates, keys, and ECDSA signatures.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: PEM
chunks.content:
Term: PEM (PEM)
Definition: Privacy Enhanced Mail, a Base64-encoded format for storing cryptographic objects (certificates, keys, CSRs). Recognized by "-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----" headers.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Derivation Path
chunks.content:
Term: Derivation Path
Definition: A hierarchical path notation (e.g., m/44'/0'/0'/0/0) used in HD wallets to deterministically derive child keys from a master key following BIP32/BIP44 standards.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Quantum Error Correction
chunks.content:
Term: Quantum Error Correction
Definition: Techniques to protect quantum information from decoherence and errors during computation. Essential for building practical, large-scale quantum computers.
Technical Note: Current quantum computers lack sufficient error correction for cryptanalysis. Advances in error correction directly affect CRQC timelines.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Interoperability
chunks.content:
Term: Interoperability
Definition: The ability of different cryptographic implementations, libraries, and systems to work together correctly. Critical during PQC migration when classical and quantum-safe systems must coexist.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: Hybrid Mode
chunks.content:
Term: Hybrid Mode
Definition: An operational mode where both classical and post-quantum cryptographic algorithms run in parallel, ensuring security even if one algorithm is broken.
Technical Note: Recommended by NIST, BSI, and ANSSI as the preferred transition strategy. Applied to key exchange, signatures, and certificates.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: QTSP
chunks.content:
Term: QTSP (QTSP)
Definition: Qualified Trust Service Provider, an organization authorized under eIDAS 2.0 to issue qualified electronic signatures, seals, timestamps, and certificates.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Attestation
chunks.content:
Term: Attestation
Definition: A cryptographically signed statement vouching for the authenticity or properties of a device, key, or credential. Used in FIDO2, TPM, and digital identity systems.
Category: concept | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Key Binding
chunks.content:
Term: Key Binding
Definition: Cryptographic proof that a credential is bound to a specific holder's key, preventing unauthorized transfer or replay of digital credentials.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Crypto Discovery
chunks.content:
Term: Crypto Discovery
Definition: The automated process of scanning an organization's systems to identify all cryptographic algorithms, keys, certificates, and protocols in use. The first step in PQC migration.
Technical Note: Tools like SandboxAQ AQtive Guard and IBM Guardium automate crypto discovery to build a Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM).
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: RLP
chunks.content:
Term: RLP (RLP)
Definition: Recursive Length Prefix, Ethereum's serialization encoding for transactions, blocks, and account state. Used to encode transaction data before signing with ECDSA.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Base58Check
chunks.content:
Term: Base58Check
Definition: A Bitcoin-specific encoding format that converts binary data to a human-readable alphanumeric string with a built-in checksum for error detection.
Technical Note: Omits confusable characters (0, O, I, l). Used for Bitcoin addresses, WIF private keys, and extended public/private keys.
Category: concept | Complexity: advanced

chunks.title: Harvest Now, Forge Later
chunks.content:
Term: Harvest Now, Forge Later (HNFL)
Definition: An attack strategy where adversaries record digitally signed data today, planning to forge signatures once quantum computers can break the signing algorithm.
Technical Note: While HNDL targets confidentiality (encrypted data), HNFL targets authenticity and integrity — firmware updates, certificates, and legal documents signed with RSA/ECDSA become forgeable.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: X25519MLKEM768
chunks.content:
Term: X25519MLKEM768
Definition: A hybrid key exchange algorithm combining X25519 (classical ECDH) with ML-KEM-768 (post-quantum KEM). Both shared secrets are combined via HKDF so the result is secure if either algorithm holds.
Technical Note: Deployed in Chrome, Firefox, and Cloudflare TLS 1.3. Uses concatenated shared secrets fed through HKDF-SHA256 for the final key.
Category: algorithm | Complexity: intermediate

chunks.title: Cipher Suite
chunks.content:
Term: Cipher Suite
Definition: A named combination of cryptographic algorithms used together in a TLS connection — specifying the key exchange, authentication, bulk encryption, and hash algorithms.
Technical Note: TLS 1.3 cipher suites (e.g., TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384) are simpler than TLS 1.2, separating key exchange from the cipher suite. PQC adds new key exchange groups like X25519MLKEM768.
Category: concept | Complexity: beginner

chunks.title: TLS Handshake
chunks.content:
Term: TLS Handshake
Definition: The initial negotiation between client and server that establishes a secure TLS connection — agreeing on cipher suites, exchanging keys, and authenticating the server.
