— an attacker observing two signatures from the same OTS key can forge arbitrary signatures. The key MUST be revoked immediately.
In stateful hash-based signatures, the private key state includes a
tracking the next unused OTS leaf. If this counter is lost (via VM snapshot restore, backup restoration, or crash without flush), the signer may unknowingly reuse a one-time key.
VM is snapshotted at counter=100. Signing continues to counter=150. VM is restored to snapshot. Counter resets to 100. Leaves 100–149 will be reused. An attacker with both signatures from any reused leaf can forge.
System signs at counter=50 but crashes before persisting counter=51 to disk. On restart, counter reads 50. Leaf 50 will be reused on next signature.
Private key (including counter) is copied to a second server for redundancy. Both servers sign independently, reusing all leaves. Every signature from the clone is potentially forgeable.
Always use hardware monotonic counters (TPM/HSM), write-ahead logging, and never duplicate stateful signing keys.

chunks.title: TLS Basics — TLSExercises
chunks.content:
Module: TLS Basics
Component: TLSExercises

Run these scenarios in order to compare classical, PQC, and hybrid TLS configurations. Each exercise pre-configures the simulator — click "Load & Run" to apply the settings and switch to the Simulate tab. Use the Comparison Table to track overhead across runs.
Take the Protocol Integration quiz to test what you've learned.
Run a standard TLS 1.3 handshake with RSA-2048 certificates and X25519 key exchange — the baseline configuration used by most websites today.
Observe the baseline handshake size (~4 KB) and the negotiated cipher suite. Note the RSA signature in the CertificateVerify message.
Switch to ML-DSA-87 post-quantum certificates while keeping classical X25519 key exchange. See how PQC signatures affect handshake overhead.
Compare handshake bytes vs Scenario 1. ML-DSA-87 signatures are significantly larger than RSA, increasing the Certificate and CertificateVerify messages.
Use X25519MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange with RSA certificates. This is the combination already deployed in Chrome and Firefox for quantum-safe key agreement.
Notice the increased handshake size from the larger ML-KEM key share (~1.2 KB). The Key Exchange badge in results will show "Hybrid".
4. Full PQC (ML-DSA-87 + ML-KEM-1024)
Maximum post-quantum configuration: ML-DSA-87 certificates with ML-KEM-1024 key exchange. See the full cost of a quantum-safe TLS connection.
This will have the largest handshake overhead. Compare the total bytes with Scenario 1 to quantify the PQC migration cost.

chunks.title: TLS Basics — TLSExercises
chunks.content:
Module: TLS Basics

Enable server-side client certificate verification. Both parties authenticate with certificates, commonly used in API security and zero-trust architectures.
Watch for the CertificateRequest message from the server, and the client Certificate + CertificateVerify responses. Both cert verification statuses should show "Verified".

chunks.title: TLS Basics — TLSIntroduction
chunks.content:
Module: TLS Basics
Component: TLSIntroduction

1.3 (RFC 8446) is the latest version of the Transport Layer Security protocol, securing virtually all HTTPS traffic on the internet. It was a major overhaul from TLS 1.2, removing insecure legacy features and introducing mandatory
, a faster 1-RTT handshake, and a dramatically simplified cipher suite list.
The handshake establishes a secure connection in a single round trip. The client sends its supported parameters and a key share; the server responds with its choice and key share. After the ServerHello, all remaining handshake messages are encrypted using handshake traffic keys derived via
TLS 1.3 cipher suites only specify the symmetric encryption algorithm and hash — key exchange and authentication are negotiated separately. This is why there are only 5 suites compared to hundreds in TLS 1.2.
AES-128 in CCM mode + SHA-256 — designed for constrained environments (IoT)
AES-128 in CCM mode with 8-byte auth tag + SHA-256 — for highly constrained devices
TLS 1.3 uses ephemeral key exchange for every connection, ensuring forward secrecy. With post-quantum cryptography (PQC), three approaches are available:
. Fast and small (~32 byte keys). Vulnerable to quantum computers.
ML-KEM-512/768/1024. Quantum-resistant. Larger keys (~1.2 KB for ML-KEM-768) increase handshake size.
combines both. Already deployed in Chrome and Firefox. Secure even if one algorithm is broken.

chunks.title: TLS Basics — TLSIntroduction
chunks.content:
Module: TLS Basics
Component: TLSIntroduction

TLS 1.3 derives all session keys through a structured key schedule using HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function). The shared secret from key exchange feeds into a chain of Extract and Expand operations:
These exact secrets are visible in the Simulate tab after running a handshake.
Quantum computers threaten classical key exchange (ECDH) through
attack means adversaries can record encrypted traffic today and decrypt it once quantum computers are available. PQC integration into TLS addresses this by using
The trade-off: PQC algorithms have larger keys and ciphertexts, increasing handshake overhead. For example, ML-KEM-768 public keys are ~1,184 bytes vs 32 bytes for X25519. The hybrid approach (e.g., X25519MLKEM768) provides quantum resistance while maintaining classical security as a fallback.
PQC signatures are significantly larger than classical ones — an ML-DSA certificate chain can add several kilobytes to every TLS handshake. For the web, where billions of connections happen daily, this overhead is a serious scalability concern. Google's
Traditional TLS uses a chain of X.509 certificates, each containing a public key and a signature from the issuing CA. With PQC signatures (~2.4 KB for ML-DSA-65), a typical 2-certificate chain adds ~5 KB+ to the handshake — a substantial bandwidth increase.

chunks.title: VPN & SSH PQC — VPNSSHExercises
chunks.content:
Module: VPN & SSH PQC
Component: VPNSSHExercises

Work through these scenarios to compare VPN and SSH protocol behavior with classical, hybrid, and post-quantum key exchange. Each exercise pre-configures the Simulator — click "Load & Run" to begin.
Take the PQC quiz to test what you've learned about VPN and SSH protocols.
1. Classical IKEv2 Handshake (ECP-256)
Step through a standard IKEv2 handshake using ECDH Group 19 (secp256r1). Observe the IKE_SA_INIT and IKE_AUTH phases, payload structure, and the compact message sizes with classical DH.
The total handshake is approximately 1,400 bytes across 2 round trips. The KE payload carries a 64-byte ECP-256 public key. This baseline is quantum-vulnerable to Shor's algorithm.
2. Hybrid IKEv2 with ML-KEM (AKE)
Run a hybrid IKEv2 handshake per draft-ietf-ipsecme-ikev2-mlkem. Notice the Additional Key Exchange (AKE) payload in IKE_INTERMEDIATE that carries the ML-KEM-768 encapsulation key.
The handshake grows to approximately 3,768 bytes and adds a third round trip for IKE_INTERMEDIATE. The ML-KEM encapsulation key (1,184 B) and ciphertext (1,088 B) dominate the size increase.
Observe the classical SSH key exchange using X25519. Step through SSH_MSG_KEXINIT negotiation, ECDH_INIT, and ECDH_REPLY messages. Note the compact 32-byte public keys.
X25519 produces 32-byte public keys and a 32-byte shared secret. The total handshake is under 1 KB. This is the most widely deployed SSH KEX but is quantum-vulnerable.
4. SSH with ML-KEM-768 Hybrid (OpenSSH 9.9)

chunks.title: VPN & SSH PQC — VPNSSHExercises
chunks.content:
Module: VPN & SSH PQC
Component: VPNSSHExercises

Switch to mlkem768x25519-sha256, the NIST-standard hybrid KEX added in OpenSSH 9.9. Compare the message sizes with the classical X25519 exchange.
The public key grows from 32 bytes to 1,216 bytes (X25519 + ML-KEM concatenated). The total handshake is approximately 3,296 bytes — a 3.3x increase. The shared secret doubles to 64 bytes.
5. Cross-Protocol Size Comparison
View the comprehensive comparison table across IKEv2, SSH, WireGuard, and TLS 1.3. Filter by protocol and mode to understand the relative impact of PQC on each protocol.
WireGuard has the largest relative size increase (22x) due to its ultra-compact classical handshake. IKEv2 and SSH handle PQC sizes well through TCP or fragmentation support. TLS 1.3 maintains 1-RTT even with hybrid KEX.

chunks.title: VPN & SSH PQC — VPNSSHIntroduction
chunks.content:
Module: VPN & SSH PQC
Component: VPNSSHIntroduction

(Internet Key Exchange version 2, RFC 7296) is the key negotiation protocol for
VPNs. It establishes Security Associations (SAs) through a two-phase handshake:
public values. Both parties derive the same shared secret (SKEYSEED) from their DH exchange, nonces, and SPIs, then derive encryption and integrity keys for the IKE SA.
framework (RFC 9370). This enables hybrid key exchange without modifying the core IKEv2 state machine.
“The initiator sends an ML-KEM encapsulation key in an Additional Key Exchange payload during IKE_INTERMEDIATE. The responder encapsulates against this key and returns the ciphertext. The resulting shared secret is combined with the classical DH secret using the IKEv2 key hierarchy.”
ECP-256 or MODP-2048 in KE payload during IKE_SA_INIT. Quantum-vulnerable.
ML-KEM-768 encapsulation key (1,184 B) in IKE_INTERMEDIATE. Adds one round trip.
SKEYSEED derived from both DH and KEM shared secrets via PRF. Secure if either holds.
OpenSSH has been a pioneer in deploying post-quantum key exchange. Since version 8.5 (March 2021), it has shipped with
— a hybrid of Streamlined NTRU Prime and X25519. In OpenSSH 9.9 (September 2024), NIST-standard
SSH hybrid KEX works by concatenating the classical and PQC public keys in SSH_MSG_KEX_ECDH_INIT, and concatenating the classical share and KEM ciphertext in SSH_MSG_KEX_ECDH_REPLY. The shared secret is derived from both components.

chunks.title: VPN & SSH PQC — VPNSSHIntroduction
chunks.content:
Module: VPN & SSH PQC
Component: VPNSSHIntroduction

, ChaCha20-Poly1305, BLAKE2s. This minimal design makes WireGuard fast and auditable, but also means it has
WireGuard's fixed X25519 is quantum-vulnerable. Adding PQC requires modifying the protocol or layering a separate negotiation on top.
runs a separate PQC key exchange (ML-KEM + Classic McEliece) and feeds the resulting PSK into WireGuard's pre-shared key slot.
The Rosenpass approach preserves WireGuard's simplicity while adding quantum resistance. The PSK is rotated periodically, and WireGuard's security holds even if Rosenpass is compromised (defense in depth).
Post-quantum key exchange significantly increases handshake sizes across all protocols. ML-KEM-768 adds approximately
for the ciphertext. For protocols like WireGuard that use UDP, this can cause IP fragmentation issues.
WireGuard sees the largest relative increase (22x) because its classical handshake is extremely compact. IKEv2 handles fragmentation explicitly (RFC 7383) over UDP, while SSH handles larger payloads natively because it relies on TCP transport.
While PQC Key Exchange (ML-KEM) secures connections against future decryption,
secures against active attackers. Both IKEv2 and SSH uniquely require multiple layers of authentication that must be migrated.

chunks.content:
Module: VPN & SSH PQC
Component: VPNSSHIntroduction

SSH requires PQC host keys (server identity) and user keys (UserAuth). While OpenSSH 9.9 supports ML-KEM, standardizing ML-DSA keys for host and user authentication is still an active effort.
(the VPN tunnel) already uses symmetric algorithms like AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305. These algorithms are naturally quantum-resistant, requiring only standard key lengths (256-bit) to be secure against Grover's algorithm.

chunks.title: VPN & SSH PQC — sshConstants (data)
chunks.content:
Module: VPN & SSH PQC
Data: sshConstants

Classical Curve25519 ECDH key exchange (RFC 8731). The default in OpenSSH since 6.5. Fast and widely deployed, but vulnerable to quantum computers.
Hybrid NTRU Prime + X25519 key exchange. Available since OpenSSH 8.5, enabled by default in 9.0+. Uses the Streamlined NTRU Prime lattice-based KEM combined with X25519.
Hybrid ML-KEM-768 + X25519 key exchange. Added in OpenSSH 9.9 (September 2024). Uses the NIST-standardized ML-KEM (FIPS 203) combined with X25519 for quantum resistance with NIST compliance.
Client and server exchange SSH-2.0 version strings.
Client sends supported KEX algorithms, host key types, ciphers, MACs, and compression.
Server sends its supported algorithms. Both sides select the best match.
Client sends its ephemeral public key (or hybrid public key: classical + PQC concatenated).
Server sends its host key, ephemeral public share (or ciphertext), and exchange hash signature.
Both sides switch to the negotiated encryption keys. Handshake complete.

chunks.title: Vendor & Supply Chain Risk — Introduction
chunks.content:
Module: Vendor & Supply Chain Risk
Component: Introduction

Your organization's quantum readiness is only as strong as your weakest vendor. Third-party software, hardware, and cloud services form the backbone of enterprise cryptographic infrastructure. If a critical vendor lacks
“Supply chain risks are among the most significant challenges for PQC migration. Organizations must evaluate vendors' cryptographic capabilities and ensure contractual commitments to post-quantum readiness.”
targets. Every unpatched vendor extends your exposure window.
A single vendor dependency on classical-only crypto can block your entire PQC migration program.
A vendor scorecard provides a structured, repeatable method for evaluating how well-prepared each vendor is for the post-quantum transition. Scores are weighted across dimensions that map to real migration risk factors.
• PQC algorithm support (ML-KEM/FIPS 203, ML-DSA/FIPS 204, SLH-DSA/FIPS 205)
(Cryptographic Bill of Materials) extends the SBOM concept to track every cryptographic algorithm, key, certificate, and protocol used by a software product. Demanding CBOMs from vendors is the single most impactful step toward supply chain quantum readiness.
Algorithm inventory, key sizes, protocol versions, certificate types, and quantum vulnerability status per component.
CycloneDX 1.6+ includes a crypto extension for machine-readable CBOM data. The CycloneDX community (cyclonedx.org) maintains the specification, originally an OWASP project.

chunks.title: PQC Software Category Cross-Reference & Augmentation Analysis
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: PQC Software Category Cross-Reference & Augmentation Analysis

**Analysis Date:** December 16, 2025
**Data Sources:** PQC CSV Database + Cryptographic Software Categories

---

chunks.title: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This analysis cross-references 30 cryptographic software categories against the Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) threat landscape, migration timeline, standards library, and industry leaders database. The integration reveals critical gaps and provides actionable intelligence for PQC migration planning.

### Key Findings:

1. **Critical Readiness Gap**: Only 51% of tracked software (25/49 products) has ANY PQC support
2. **High-Priority Categories**: 15 of 30 categories (50%) require immediate action (2025-Q4 timeline)
3. **Migration Bottleneck**: 8 "Critical" priority categories represent foundational infrastructure affecting ALL industries
4. **Leadership Alignment**: 100 tracked PQC leaders map to only 12 software categories, indicating concentration risk

---

chunks.title: SECTION 3: THREAT-TO-SOFTWARE MAPPING
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: SECTION 3: THREAT-TO-SOFTWARE MAPPING

### High-Impact Threat Categories

| Threat Domain               | Affected Software Categories       | Critical Products at Risk          |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| **Financial Services HNDL** | CSC-001, CSC-005, CSC-004, CSC-029 | OpenSSL, payment HSMs, banking PKI |
| **Government/Defense**      | CSC-004, CSC-026, CSC-014          | AD CS, OpenSSH, secure boot        |
| **Healthcare EHR**          | CSC-007, CSC-008, CSC-006          | Database TDE, email encryption     |
| **Critical Infrastructure** | CSC-005, CSC-014, CSC-010, CSC-026 | SCADA TLS, industrial SSH, OT VPNs |
| **Cloud Services**          | CSC-003, CSC-028, CSC-002          | Cloud KMS, CASBs, cloud HSMs       |
| **IoT/Embedded**            | CSC-001, CSC-026, CSC-014          | wolfSSL, mbed TLS, embedded SSH    |

### Industry-Specific Urgency

**Finance & Banking** (CRITICAL)

- Affected Categories: 8 critical, 5 high
- Key Software: OpenSSL, Thales Luna HSM, Oracle TDE, payment terminals
- Timeline: Immediate (2025-Q4)
- Primary Threats: HNDL attacks, payment fraud, long-term data exposure

**Government & Defense** (CRITICAL)

- Affected Categories: 6 critical, 4 high
- Key Software: Microsoft AD CS, OpenSSH, secure boot, classified messaging
- Timeline: CNSA 2.0 compliance by 2026-Q1
- Primary Threats: Nation-state attacks, classified data exposure

**Healthcare** (HIGH)

- Affected Categories: 5 critical, 6 high
- Key Software: EHR database encryption, HIPAA email, endpoint encryption
- Timeline: 2026-Q1
- Primary Threats: Patient data HNDL, long-term health record exposure

**Critical Infrastructure** (CRITICAL)

- Affected Categories: 7 critical, 5 high
- Key Software: SCADA TLS, industrial SSH, OT VPNs, grid controllers
- Timeline: 2026-Q1
- Primary Threats: Grid control systems, OT communications, real-time attacks

Section: SECTION 4: STANDARDS-TO-SOFTWARE MAPPING

### FIPS 203 (ML-KEM) Implementation Status

**Software with Full Support:**

- OpenSSL 3.5.4, Bouncy Castle Java 1.83, liboqs, GnuTLS (hybrid)
- wolfSSL 5.8.2, Bouncy Castle C# .NET

**Affected Categories:**

- CSC-005 (TLS/SSL) - Critical for secure communications
- CSC-003 (Key Management) - Essential for key encapsulation
- CSC-010 (VPN/IPsec) - Required for VPN modernization
- CSC-014 (SSH) - Deployed in OpenSSH 10.2 by default

**Industry Documents Requiring ML-KEM:**

- IETF TLS 1.3 Hybrid KEM (draft-03)
- IETF LAMPS Composite KEM (draft-11)
- CISA PQC Product Category List

### FIPS 204 (ML-DSA) Implementation Status

- CSC-004 (PKI) - Certificate signing

**Affected Categories:**

- CSC-004 (PKI) - Long-term trust

**Use Cases:**

- Long-term archival signatures
- Backup to ML-DSA
- Stateless signature requirements

chunks.title: SECTION 5: TIMELINE PRESSURE ANALYSIS
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: SECTION 5: TIMELINE PRESSURE ANALYSIS

### Regulatory Deadlines Impacting Software Categories

**2025-12-31: CNSA 1.0 Compliance (NSA)**

- Affected: CSC-004 (PKI), CSC-002 (HSM), CSC-001 (Crypto Libraries)
- Impact: All National Security Systems
- Status: In Progress

**2025-Q4: CISA PQC Product Category List**

- Affected: Multiple categories across data management, networking, endpoints
- Impact: Federal procurement requirements
- Status: List published, approved products pending

**2026-06-30: CNSA 2.0 Compliance Window**

- Affected: All cryptographic software categories
- Impact: 6-month window for systems not CNSA 1.0 compliant
- Status: Planning phase

**2026: EU/Germany PQC Initiatives**

- Germany quantum-secure ID cards (BSI)
- PKI Consortium global coordination
- Impact: International standards pressure

**2026-2027: HSM PQC Architecture Availability**

- Current Gap: No full PQC HSM architectures
- FIPS 140-3 certification lag: 2+ years
- Impact: Root of trust vulnerability window

---

chunks.title: SECTION 6: STRATEGIC RECOMMENDATIONS
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: SECTION 6: STRATEGIC RECOMMENDATIONS

### Immediate Actions (2025-Q4)

1. **Cryptographic Libraries (CSC-001)**
   - Deploy OpenSSL 3.5.4 or Bouncy Castle 1.83 with full PQC
   - Establish crypto inventory of all library dependencies
   - Begin hybrid mode deployment (classical + PQC)

2. **TLS/SSL Infrastructure (CSC-005)**
   - Enable hybrid TLS 1.3 with X25519MLKEM768
   - Update web servers, load balancers, API gateways
   - Test performance impact in production-like environments

3. **PKI Systems (CSC-004)**
   - Deploy composite certificate capability
   - Plan phased migration of certificate hierarchies
   - Test interoperability with legacy systems

4. **HSM Software (CSC-002)**
   - Engage HSM vendors on PQC roadmaps
   - Plan 2026-2027 HSM replacement cycle
   - Deploy hybrid HSM architecture if available

5. **Key Management (CSC-003)**
   - Audit all key lifecycle processes
   - Implement ML-KEM for key encapsulation
   - Update KMS to support dual-algorithm operation

### Planning Phase (2026-Q1)

6. **SSH Infrastructure (CSC-014)**
   - Deploy OpenSSH 10.2 with mlkem768x25519 default
   - Update jump hosts and bastion servers
   - Test with privileged access management systems

7. **VPN/IPsec (CSC-010)**
   - Deploy strongSwan with experimental ML-KEM
   - Plan site-to-site VPN migration
   - Test remote access VPN performance

8. **Secure Boot (CSC-026)**
   - Implement ML-DSA firmware signing
   - Update TPM 2.0 configurations
   - Plan UEFI Secure Boot PQC enablement

9. **Code Signing (CSC-017)**
   - Deploy ML-DSA for software signing
   - Update CI/CD pipelines
   - Test container image signing with PQC

10. **Database Encryption (CSC-007)**
    - Evaluate TDE with PQC key wrapping
    - Test column-level encryption performance
    - Plan data warehouse migration

### Strategic Initiatives (2026-Q2+)

11. **Cryptographic Agility (CSC-025)**
    - Implement crypto abstraction layers
    - Deploy algorithm negotiation frameworks
    - Establish governance for algorithm transitions

12.

chunks.title: SECTION 7: RISK MITIGATION STRATEGIES
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: SECTION 7: RISK MITIGATION STRATEGIES

### Vendor Lock-in Risks

**Issue:** Concentration of PQC support in few vendors (OpenSSL, Bouncy Castle)

**Mitigation:**

1. Support multiple cryptographic library options
2. Implement crypto abstraction layers (CSC-025)
3. Maintain fallback to alternative implementations
4. Participate in open-source PQC projects

### Performance Impact Risks

**Issue:** PQC algorithms have larger keys and slower operations

**Mitigation:**

1. Deploy hybrid mode initially (classical + PQC)
2. Conduct thorough performance testing
3. Plan infrastructure scaling (network, compute)
4. Optimize hot paths with hardware acceleration

### Interoperability Risks

**Issue:** Mixed PQC deployment creates compatibility issues

**Mitigation:**

1. Maintain backward compatibility during transition
2. Deploy hybrid algorithms (X25519MLKEM768)
3. Implement graceful fallback mechanisms
4. Coordinate with partners on migration timeline

### HSM Availability Risks

**Issue:** 2+ year gap until PQC-ready HSMs widely available

**Mitigation:**

1. Plan HSM replacement cycles now for 2026-2027
2. Consider software-based key protection as interim
3. Deploy HSM pooling/clustering for flexibility
4. Evaluate quantum-safe HSM alternatives (Crypto4A)

### Compliance Pressure Risks

**Issue:** Regulatory deadlines compressing migration timeline

**Mitigation:**

1. Prioritize systems with regulatory requirements
2. Establish executive sponsorship for PQC program
3. Allocate dedicated resources and budget
4. Seek guidance/waivers where needed (e.g., NSS)

---

chunks.title: SECTION 8: INVESTMENT PRIORITIES
chunks.content:
Document: PQC_Software_Category_Strategic_Analysis
Section: SECTION 8: INVESTMENT PRIORITIES

**High-Priority Investments (60% of budget):**

1. Cryptographic library upgrades and licensing
2. HSM replacement planning and procurement
3. TLS/SSL infrastructure modernization
4. PKI system upgrades and testing
5. Professional services for migration planning

**Medium-Priority Investments (30% of budget):**

1. Performance testing and optimization
2. Monitoring and discovery tools

4. Interoperability testing
5. Third-party assessments

**Strategic Investments (10% of budget):**

1. Cryptographic agility frameworks
2. Research and development
3. Industry collaboration
4. Standards participation
5. Innovation initiatives

### ROI Considerations

**Cost Avoidance:**

- Prevent harvest-now-decrypt-later exposure
- Avoid regulatory non-compliance penalties
- Reduce incident response costs
- Minimize emergency migration expenses

**Business Enablement:**

- Support long-term digital trust
- Enable quantum-safe product offerings
- Maintain customer confidence
- Ensure business continuity

**Competitive Advantage:**

- Early mover advantage in quantum-safe services
- Differentiation in regulated industries
- Enhanced security posture
- Future-proof architecture

chunks.title: SECTION 10: NEXT STEPS
chunks.content:

### Phase 1: Assessment (2025-Q4)

- [ ] Complete cryptographic inventory
- [ ] Identify all software dependencies
- [ ] Map current state to software categories
- [ ] Prioritize systems by business impact

**Week 3-4:**

- [ ] Assess PQC readiness of current software
- [ ] Identify vendor PQC roadmaps
- [ ] Evaluate hybrid deployment options
- [ ] Create detailed migration plan

### Phase 2: Pilot Deployment (2026-Q1)

**Month 1:**

- [ ] Deploy PQC in non-production environments
- [ ] Conduct performance testing
- [ ] Validate interoperability
- [ ] Train operations teams

**Month 2:**

- [ ] Pilot production deployment (low-risk systems)
- [ ] Monitor and measure performance
- [ ] Collect feedback and refine approach
- [ ] Document lessons learned

**Month 3:**

- [ ] Expand to medium-risk production systems
- [ ] Establish operational procedures
- [ ] Update runbooks and documentation
- [ ] Prepare for full-scale rollout

### Phase 3: Production Rollout (2026-Q2+)

**Continuous Activities:**

- [ ] Phased migration by priority
- [ ] Regular assessment and monitoring
- [ ] Vendor engagement and coordination
- [ ] Standards tracking and compliance
- [ ] Team training and capability building

### Appendix C: Glossary

- **ML-DSA**: Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm (FIPS 204)
- **SLH-DSA**: Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm (FIPS 205)

chunks.title: Security Audit Report
chunks.content:
Document: security_audit_report_12022025
Section: Security Audit Report

**Date:** 2025-12-02
**Auditor:** Antigravity

chunks.title: Executive Summary
chunks.content:
Document: security_audit_report_12022025
Section: Executive Summary

A comprehensive security audit was conducted on the PQC Timeline Application codebase. The audit included automated dependency scanning and manual static code analysis. **No high or critical vulnerabilities were found.** The codebase follows security best practices for the identified scope.

chunks.title: 1. Dependency Analysis
chunks.content:
Document: security_audit_report_12022025
Section: 1. Dependency Analysis

- **Tool**: `npm audit`
- **Status**: ✅ **Passed**
- **Findings**: 0 vulnerabilities found in project dependencies.

chunks.title: 2. Static Code Analysis
chunks.content:
Document: security_audit_report_12022025
Section: 2. Static Code Analysis

### Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)

- **Check**: Searched for `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` and unescaped user input.
- **Status**: ✅ **Passed**
- **Findings**: No instances of `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` were found. React's default escaping is effectively used.

### Code Injection

- **Check**: Searched for `eval()`, `new Function()`, and other execution sinks.
- **Status**: ✅ **Passed**
- **Findings**: No dangerous execution functions were found.

### External Links (Tabnabbing)

- **Check**: Verified that all `target="_blank"` links include `rel="noopener noreferrer"`.
- **Status**: ✅ **Passed**
- **Findings**: All external links in the following components are correctly secured:
  - `src/components/Timeline/GanttDetailPopover.tsx`
  - `src/components/Leaders/LeadersGrid.tsx`
  - `src/components/Library/LibraryDetailPopover.tsx`
  - `src/components/Library/LibraryTreeTable.tsx`

### Data Storage

- **Check**: Analyzed usage of `localStorage` and `sessionStorage`.
- **Status**: ℹ️ **Note**
- **Findings**:
  - `src/components/Playground/contexts/SettingsProvider.tsx` uses `sessionStorage` to persist playground execution modes and enabled algorithms.
  - **Risk**: Low. This data is non-sensitive user preference data.
  - **Recommendation**: Ensure no sensitive PII or cryptographic keys are ever stored in `sessionStorage` in the future.

### Hardcoded Secrets

- **Check**: Searched for patterns like "API_KEY", "SECRET", "TOKEN", "PASSWORD".
- **Status**: ✅ **Passed**
- **Findings**:
  - "Secret" keywords found in `src/wasm/` files refer to variable names for cryptographic operations (e.g., `secretKey`), which is expected behavior for this application.
  - "Secret" keywords in CSV files refer to public URLs or threat descriptions (e.g., "AWS Secrets Manager").
  - No actual hardcoded credentials were identified.

chunks.title: Recommendations
chunks.content:
Document: security_audit_report_12022025
Section: Recommendations

1.  **Continuous Monitoring**: Integrate `npm audit` into the CI/CD pipeline to catch new dependency vulnerabilities early.
2.  **CSP**: Implement a Content Security Policy (CSP) header in the deployment configuration to further mitigate XSS risks.
3.  **Secret Management**: Continue to ensure that any future backend integration uses environment variables for secrets, not hardcoded values.

chunks.title: 3GPP TS 33.310: Network Domain Security – Authentication Framework (NDS/AF)
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: 3GPP TS 33.310: Network Domain Security – Authentication Framework (NDS/AF)

> **Official Documentation:** [3GPP TS 33.310 (Latest Version - V19.4.0)](https://www.3gpp.org/DynaReport/33310.htm)
>
> **ETSI Publication:** [ETSI TS 133 310](https://www.etsi.org/deliver/etsi_ts/133300_133399/133310/)
>
> **Technical Content:** [Tech-Invite TS 33.310](https://www.tech-invite.com/3m33/tinv-3gpp-33-310.html)

---

chunks.title: Overview
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Overview

3GPP TS 33.310 specifies a **scalable entity authentication framework** for 3GPP network nodes using X.509 certificates. It defines certificate profiles, trust models, and protocols for securing inter-operator and intra-operator communications via IPsec (NDS/IP) and TLS.

---

chunks.title: Scope
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Scope

| Domain                     | Description                                                                |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **NDS/IP**                 | IPsec-based security for Za (inter-SEG) and Zb (intra-domain) interfaces   |
| **TLS**                    | TLS-based authentication for inter-operator communications (IMS, GBA, SBA) |
| **Base Station Enrolment** | Certificate provisioning for eNodeB/gNodeB via CMPv2                       |
| **5GC SBA**                | Service Based Architecture certificates for NFs, SCP, SEPP                 |

---

chunks.title: Certificate Profiles Defined
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Certificate Profiles Defined

### End Entity Certificates

| Profile                      | Use Case                                             |
| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| **SEG Certificate**          | Security Gateway authentication (IPsec Za interface) |
| **NE Certificate**           | Network Element authentication (Zb interface)        |
| **TLS Client Certificate**   | TLS client authentication                            |
| **TLS Server Certificate**   | TLS server authentication                            |
| **Base Station Certificate** | eNodeB/gNodeB backhaul security                      |

### 5GC SBA Certificates (Release 15+)

| Profile               | Use Case                                          |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| **NF Certificate**    | Network Function producer/consumer authentication |
| **SCP Certificate**   | Service Communication Proxy                       |
| **SEPP Intra-domain** | Security Edge Protection Proxy (within PLMN)      |
| **SEPP Inter-domain** | SEPP for inter-PLMN/inter-SNPN roaming            |

### CA Certificates

| Profile                | Use Case                                              |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| **Interconnection CA** | Issues cross-certificates to other operators' SEG CAs |
| **SEG CA**             | Issues certificates to SEGs within operator domain    |
| **TLS CA**             | Issues certificates to TLS entities                   |
| **NE CA**              | Issues certificates to Network Elements               |

---

chunks.title: Key Technical Requirements
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Key Technical Requirements

### Common Certificate Rules (Clause 6.1.1)

- **Version:** X.509 v3
- **Compliance:** RFC 5280 with 3GPP-specific amendments (3GPP overrides RFC 5280 on conflicts)
- **Serial Number:** Unique within CA
- **Signature Algorithm:**
  - RSA (2048-bit minimum) or ECDSA (P-256 recommended for 5GC SBA)
  - SHA-256 or stronger hash algorithms

### SEG Certificate Profile (Clause 6.1.3)

| Field/Extension         | Requirement                                      |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| `subject`               | Distinguished Name identifying the SEG           |
| `issuer`                | SEG CA subject name                              |
| `keyUsage`              | `digitalSignature`, `keyEncipherment` (critical) |
| `extKeyUsage`           | `id-kp-ipsecIKE` (OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.17)        |
| `subjectAltName`        | IP address or FQDN of the SEG                    |
| `crlDistributionPoints` | Mandatory – points to operator's CRL             |

### TLS Entity Certificate Profile (Clause 6.1.3a)

| Field/Extension  | Requirement                                  |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| `keyUsage`       | `digitalSignature` (critical)                |
| `extKeyUsage`    | `id-kp-serverAuth` and/or `id-kp-clientAuth` |
| `subjectAltName` | DNS name (mandatory for TLS per RFC 6125)    |

### 5GC SBA Certificate Profile (Clause 6.1.3c)

| Field/Extension  | Requirement                                                |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `subjectAltName` | FQDN in format: `<nf-type>.<mnc>.mcc<MCC>.3gppnetwork.org` |
| `signature`      | ECDSA recommended (RSA optional)                           |
| `extKeyUsage`    | `id-kp-serverAuth`, `id-kp-clientAuth`                     |

**SEPP Inter-PLMN FQDN Format:**

```
sepp<SEPP-id>.5gc.mnc<MNC>.mcc<MCC>.3gppnetwork.org
```

### CA Certificate Profile (Clause 6

chunks.title: Trust Models
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Trust Models

| Model                  | Description                                     |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| **Simple Trust Model** | Direct cross-certification between operator CAs |
| **Bridge CA Model**    | Centralized Bridge CA for multi-operator trust  |
| **Hierarchical**       | Root CA → Intermediate CA → End Entity          |

---

chunks.title: Certificate Lifecycle Management
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Certificate Lifecycle Management

### Supported Protocols

| Protocol             | Use Case                                          |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| **CMPv2 (RFC 4210)** | Preferred for SEG, NE, and base station enrolment |
| **PKCS#10**          | Manual certificate requests (fallback)            |
| **ACME**             | 5GC NF certificate management (Release 19+)       |

### Revocation

- **CRL:** Mandatory; accessed via LDAP or HTTP
- **OCSP:** Optional; accessed via HTTP
- CRL/OCSP must be accessible to interconnecting operators

---

chunks.title: Key Interfaces
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Key Interfaces

| Interface | Description                       | Security                            |
| --------- | --------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| **Za**    | Between SEGs (inter-operator)     | IPsec with IKEv2 (mandatory)        |
| **Zb**    | Between NE and SEG (intra-domain) | IPsec (optional)                    |
| **N32**   | Between SEPPs (5GC roaming)       | TLS 1.2+ with mutual authentication |

---

chunks.title: Recent Updates (Release 18/19)
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Recent Updates (Release 18/19)

| Feature             | Description                                             |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Annex I**         | Guidance for 5GC certificate management procedures      |
| **Annex J**         | ACME-based certificate management for 5GC NFs           |
| **SEPP Inter-SNPN** | Certificate profiles for Standalone Non-Public Networks |

---

chunks.title: Related Specifications
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Related Specifications

| Specification | Description                                 |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| **TS 33.210** | NDS/IP – IP network layer security          |
| **TS 33.501** | 5G System security architecture             |
| **TS 33.203** | Access security for IP-based services (IMS) |
| **TS 33.220** | Generic Bootstrapping Architecture (GBA)    |
| **TS 23.003** | Numbering, addressing, and identification   |
| **RFC 5280**  | X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile       |
| **RFC 4210**  | CMPv2 – Certificate Management Protocol     |

---

chunks.title: Implementation Considerations
chunks.content:
Document: 3GPP_TS_33.310_NDS_AF_Certificate_Overview
Section: Implementation Considerations

1. **Cross-Certification:** Essential for inter-operator IPsec/TLS connectivity
2. **Repository Access:** SEGs use LDAP; TLS entities may use HTTP
3. **5GC SBA:** ECDSA preferred over RSA for performance
4. **Base Station Enrolment:** Vendor certificate → Operator certificate swap via CMPv2
5. **FQDN Naming:** Follow 3GPP naming conventions for SBA certificates

---

_Document generated for reference purposes. Always consult the official 3GPP/ETSI publications for authoritative and current guidance._

chunks.title: CA/Browser Forum TLS Baseline Requirements Overview
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: CA/Browser Forum TLS Baseline Requirements Overview

> **Official Documentation:** [CA/Browser Forum TLS BR v2.1.9 (Latest)](https://cabforum.org/working-groups/server/baseline-requirements/requirements/)
>
> **Document Repository:** [https://cabforum.org/working-groups/server/baseline-requirements/documents/](https://cabforum.org/working-groups/server/baseline-requirements/documents/)
>
> **GitHub Repository:** [https://github.com/cabforum/servercert](https://github.com/cabforum/servercert)

---

chunks.title: Overview
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Overview

The **Baseline Requirements for the Issuance and Management of Publicly-Trusted TLS Server Certificates** establish minimum standards that Certification Authorities (CAs) must follow when issuing TLS/SSL certificates trusted by browsers and operating systems.

---

chunks.title: Certificate Types Covered
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Certificate Types Covered

| Type                             | Description                                         |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| **DV (Domain Validation)**       | Validates domain control only                       |
| **OV (Organization Validation)** | Validates domain + organization identity            |
| **EV (Extended Validation)**     | Highest validation level with verified legal entity |

---

chunks.title: Key Technical Requirements
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Key Technical Requirements

### Certificate Profile

- **Version:** X.509 v3
- **Serial Number:** Minimum 64 bits of entropy from a CSPRNG
- **Validity Period:** Maximum 398 days (reducing to 47 days by 2029 per SC-081v3)
- **Key Size Minimums:**
  - RSA: 2048 bits (3072+ recommended)
  - ECDSA: P-256, P-384, or P-521

### Required Extensions

| Extension               | Requirement                                                  |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `basicConstraints`      | CA:FALSE for subscriber certificates                         |
| `keyUsage`              | `digitalSignature`, optionally `keyEncipherment`             |
| `extKeyUsage`           | `id-kp-serverAuth` (required), `id-kp-clientAuth` (optional) |
| `subjectAltName`        | MUST contain all FQDNs; CN is deprecated for domain names    |
| `certificatePolicies`   | MUST include CA/B Forum Reserved Policy OID                  |
| `authorityInfoAccess`   | OCSP responder URL required                                  |
| `crlDistributionPoints` | CRL URL required                                             |

### CA/Browser Forum Policy OIDs

| OID              | Certificate Type            |
| ---------------- | --------------------------- |
| `2.23.140.1.2.1` | Domain Validated (DV)       |
| `2.23.140.1.2.2` | Organization Validated (OV) |
| `2.23.140.1.1`   | Extended Validation (EV)    |

---

chunks.title: Domain Validation Methods (Section 3.2.2.4)
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Domain Validation Methods (Section 3.2.2.4)

Approved methods include:

1. **Email to Domain Contact** – Validation via WHOIS/DNS admin contact
2. **HTTP Practical Demonstration** – File placed at `/.well-known/pki-validation/`
3. **DNS Change** – TXT or CNAME record with random value
4. **ACME Challenge** – Automated validation per RFC 8555
5. **Phone Confirmation** – Call to verified domain contact

**Note:** Wildcard certificates have restricted validation methods.

---

chunks.title: Revocation Requirements
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Revocation Requirements

- **CRL:** CAs MUST publish CRLs; update at least every 7 days
- **OCSP:** MUST be available; responses valid for max 10 days
- **Revocation Timeline:**
  - Within 24 hours for key compromise
  - Within 5 days for other issues
- **Reason Codes:** Required for Subordinate CA certificate revocations

---

chunks.title: CA Security Requirements
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: CA Security Requirements

- HSM required for Root CA private keys
- Annual compliance audits (WebTrust or ETSI)
- Network security and segmentation
- Multi-person control for CA signing operations
- Incident response and vulnerability management

---

chunks.title: Recent Key Changes (2024-2025)
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Recent Key Changes (2024-2025)

| Ballot       | Change                                                  |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| **SC-081v3** | Certificate lifespan reduction to 47 days by 2029       |
| **SC-085**   | DNSSEC validation required for CAA lookups (March 2026) |
| **SC-062**   | Major restructuring to RFC 3647 format (v2.0.0)         |

---

chunks.title: Compliance & Auditing
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Compliance & Auditing

Acceptable audit schemes:

- **WebTrust for CAs** – SSL Baseline with Network Security
- **ETSI EN 319 411-1** – Policy requirements for TSPs

---

chunks.title: Related Standards
chunks.content:
Document: CAB_Forum_TLS_Baseline_Requirements_Overview
Section: Related Standards

- **CA/B Forum EV Guidelines** – Extended Validation specific requirements
- **RFC 5280** – Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile
- **RFC 6960** – OCSP
- **RFC 8555** – ACME Protocol
- **ETSI EN 319 411-1/411-2** – European TSP policy requirements

---

_Document generated for reference purposes. Always consult the official CA/Browser Forum publications for authoritative and current guidance._

chunks.title: ETSI EN 319 412-2: Natural Person Certificate Requirements
chunks.content:
Document: ETSI_EN_319_412-2_Certificate_Overview
Section: ETSI EN 319 412-2: Natural Person Certificate Requirements

> **Official Documentation:** [ETSI EN 319 412-2 V2.2.1 (Latest Published Version)](https://www.etsi.org/deliver/etsi_en/319400_319499/31941202/02.02.01_60/en_31941202v020201p.pdf)
>
> **ETSI Standards Portal:** [https://www.etsi.org/standards](https://www.etsi.org/standards)

---

chunks.title: Overview
chunks.content:
Document: ETSI_EN_319_412-2_Certificate_Overview
Section: Overview

ETSI EN 319 412-2 specifies requirements for **Natural Person Certificates** within the European trust services framework under eIDAS regulation.

---

chunks.title: Certificate Profiles Defined
chunks.content:
Document: ETSI_EN_319_412-2_Certificate_Overview
Section: Certificate Profiles Defined

| Profile        | Description                                                               |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **NCP**        | Normalized Certificate Policy – Standard assurance level                  |
| **NCP+**       | Certificates where private key resides in a QSCD                          |
| **QCP-n**      | Qualified certificates for natural persons (electronic signatures)        |
| **QCP-n-qscd** | Qualified certificates with keys on a Qualified Signature Creation Device |

---

chunks.title: Key Technical Requirements
chunks.content:
Document: ETSI_EN_319_412-2_Certificate_Overview
Section: Key Technical Requirements

### Subject Field (Mandatory)

- `commonName` (CN) – Natural person's name
- `countryName` (C) – Country of the CA or subject
- `serialNumber` – Unique identifier when names aren't unique (often national ID)

### Certificate Extensions

- `keyUsage` – Typically `nonRepudiation` (contentCommitment) for qualified signatures
- `certificatePolicies` – Must reference applicable OID from EN 319 411-1/411-2
- `qcStatements` – Critical for qualified certificates, indicating QC compliance and QSCD usage

### QC Statements OIDs (per EN 319 412-5)

| OID                        | Purpose                            |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| `id-etsi-qcs-QcCompliance` | Indicates a qualified certificate  |
| `id-etsi-qcs-QcSSCD`       | Indicates private key is on a QSCD |

---

chunks.title: Practical Recommendations
chunks.content:
Document: ETSI_EN_319_412-2_Certificate_Overview
Section: Practical Recommendations

For enterprise deployments requiring legal validity under eIDAS:

1. Use **QCP-n-qscd** profile for qualified electronic signatures with highest legal standing
2. Ensure your TSP (Trust Service Provider) is listed on the **EU Trusted List**
3. Implement certificate lifecycle management aligned with **EN 319 411-1/411-2**

---

chunks.title: Related Standards
chunks.content:
Document: ETSI_EN_319_412-2_Certificate_Overview
Section: Related Standards

- **EN 319 411-1/411-2** – Policy and security requirements for TSPs
- **EN 319 412-5** – QC Statements and certificate extensions
- **eIDAS Regulation (EU) 910/2014** – Legal framework for electronic identification and trust services

---

_Document generated for reference purposes. Always consult the official ETSI publications for authoritative guidance._

chunks.title: X.509 Certificate Profile Review Report
chunks.content:
Document: X509_Profile_Review_Report
Section: X.509 Certificate Profile Review Report

**Date:** December 3, 2025  
**Standards Validated:** CA/B Forum TLS BR 2.1.x, ETSI EN 319 412-2, 3GPP TS 33.310, RFC 9310  
**OpenSSL Target:** v3.5.4

---

chunks.title: Executive Summary
chunks.content:
Document: X509_Profile_Review_Report
Section: Executive Summary

I reviewed 11 X.509 certificate and CSR profile CSV files against their respective standards and OpenSSL 3.5.4 compatibility. **4 issues were identified and corrected.**

| Status                     | Count  |
| -------------------------- | ------ |
| ✅ Profiles with no issues | 9      |
| 🔧 Profiles corrected      | 2      |
| **Total files**            | **11** |

---

chunks.title: Issues Found and Corrections Made
chunks.content:
Document: X509_Profile_Review_Report
Section: Issues Found and Corrections Made

### 1. Cert-GeneralIT_CABF_TLSBR_2025.csv

**Issue:** Missing mandatory extensions for CA/B Forum TLS Baseline Requirements

| Missing Extension      | OID       | Requirement                                               |
| ---------------------- | --------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| extendedKeyUsage       | 2.5.29.37 | **MUST** include `serverAuth` for TLS server certificates |
| subjectKeyIdentifier   | 2.5.29.14 | **SHOULD** be included per RFC 5280                       |
| certificatePolicies    | 2.5.29.32 | **MUST** include at least one policy OID per TLS BR       |
| authorityKeyIdentifier | 2.5.29.35 | **MUST** be included for non-self-signed certificates     |

**Correction:** Added all four missing extensions with proper OIDs and validation rules.

---

### 2. Cert-Telecom_3GPP_TS33310_2025.csv

**Issue:** Incorrect OID for `nftypes` extension

| Field    | Before (Incorrect) | After (Correct)             |
| -------- | ------------------ | --------------------------- |
| OID      | 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.26 | 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.34          |
| Standard | Draft/Pre-RFC      | **RFC 9310** (January 2023) |

**Correction:** Updated to RFC 9310 standardized OID `1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.34` (id-pe-nftype).

---

chunks.title: PQC Algorithm OID Verification
chunks.content:
Document: X509_Profile_Review_Report
Section: PQC Algorithm OID Verification

All Post-Quantum Cryptography OIDs are correct per NIST FIPS 203/204/205:

### ML-DSA (FIPS 204) - Digital Signatures

| Algorithm | OID                     | Security Level    | Status     |
| --------- | ----------------------- | ----------------- | ---------- |
| ML-DSA-44 | 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.17 | Level 2 (128-bit) | ✅ Correct |
| ML-DSA-65 | 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.18 | Level 3 (192-bit) | ✅ Correct |
| ML-DSA-87 | 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.19 | Level 5 (256-bit) | ✅ Correct |

### ML-KEM (FIPS 203) - Key Encapsulation

| Algorithm   | OID                    | Security Level | Status     |
| ----------- | ---------------------- | -------------- | ---------- |
| ML-KEM-512  | 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.4.1 | Level 1        | ✅ Correct |
| ML-KEM-768  | 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.4.2 | Level 3        | ✅ Correct |
| ML-KEM-1024 | 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.4.3 | Level 5        | ✅ Correct |

---

chunks.title: OpenSSL 3.5.4 Compatibility
chunks.content:
Document: X509_Profile_Review_Report
Section: OpenSSL 3.5.4 Compatibility

All profiles are fully compatible with OpenSSL 3.5.4:

| Feature                           | Support Status                    |
| --------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| ML-DSA (FIPS 204)                 | ✅ Native support since v3.5.0    |
| ML-KEM (FIPS 203)                 | ✅ Native support since v3.5.0    |
| SLH-DSA (FIPS 205)                | ✅ Native support since v3.5.0    |
| Classical algorithms (ECDSA, RSA) | ✅ Full support                   |
| All X.509 extension OIDs          | ✅ Full support                   |
| RFC 9310 NFTypes extension        | ✅ Supported via custom extension |

**OpenSSL 3.5 Command Examples:**

```bash
# Generate ML-DSA-65 key
openssl genpkey -algorithm ML-DSA-65 -out ml-dsa-65.key

# Generate ML-KEM-768 key
openssl genpkey -algorithm ML-KEM-768 -out ml-kem-768.key

# List available signature algorithms
openssl list -signature-algorithms

# List available KEM algorithms
openssl list -kem-algorithms
```

---

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: Which quantum algorithm poses the primary threat to RSA and elliptic-curve cryptography?
  A) Grover's Algorithm
  B) Shor's Algorithm
  C) Deutsch-Jozsa Algorithm
  D) Bernstein-Vazirani Algorithm
Answer: B
Explanation: Shor's Algorithm can factor large integers and compute discrete logarithms in polynomial time on a quantum computer, breaking RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, and other public-key algorithms. Grover's Algorithm only provides a quadratic speedup for symmetric key search, effectively halving the bit-security of symmetric ciphers.

Q: What does HNDL stand for in the context of quantum threats?
  A) Hash-Normalized Decryption Layer
  B) Harvest Now, Decrypt Later
  C) Hybrid Network Defense Lattice
  D) Hardware-Neutralized Data Lock
Answer: B
Explanation: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL) is a strategy where adversaries intercept and store encrypted data today, planning to decrypt it once a cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC) becomes available. Data with long-term sensitivity (10+ years) is especially at risk.

Q: True or False: Quantum computers threaten symmetric encryption algorithms like AES-256 just as severely as they threaten RSA.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. Grover's Algorithm reduces symmetric key security by half (e.g., AES-256 becomes ~128-bit equivalent), but this is manageable by doubling key sizes. Shor's Algorithm, in contrast, completely breaks public-key algorithms like RSA and ECC. Symmetric encryption remains relatively safe with sufficient key lengths.

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: What is a Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC)?
  A) Any quantum computer with more than 100 qubits
  B) A quantum computer capable of breaking current public-key cryptography
  C) A quantum computer that can only perform hash computations
  D) A classical-quantum hybrid used for key generation
Answer: B
Explanation: A CRQC is a quantum computer powerful enough to run Shor's Algorithm at scale, requiring thousands of stable logical qubits. Estimates for when a CRQC might exist range from 2030 to 2040, with significant uncertainty.

Q: What is the primary purpose of hybrid cryptography in the PQC transition?
  A) To reduce key sizes by combining two algorithms
  B) To maintain security even if one algorithm is broken
  C) To speed up encryption by parallelizing operations
  D) To comply with FIPS 140-3 requirements
Answer: B
Explanation: Hybrid cryptography combines a classical algorithm (e.g., X25519) with a PQC algorithm (e.g., ML-KEM-768). If either algorithm is later found to be insecure, the other still provides protection. This is a recommended transition strategy during the migration period.

Q: What is crypto agility?
  A) The speed at which a cryptographic algorithm runs
  B) The ability to quickly swap cryptographic algorithms without redesigning systems
  C) A metric for measuring quantum computer performance
  D) The process of compressing encrypted data
Answer: B
Explanation: Crypto agility is the ability of a system to switch cryptographic algorithms, parameters, or protocols with minimal disruption. It is critical for the PQC transition because it allows organizations to adopt new standards as they mature without requiring full system rebuilds.

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: True or False: Post-quantum cryptography requires quantum computers to operate.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. Post-quantum cryptographic algorithms are designed to run on classical (non-quantum) computers. They are called "post-quantum" because they are resistant to attacks from quantum computers, not because they require quantum hardware.

Q: Which mathematical problem forms the security foundation for lattice-based PQC algorithms like ML-KEM and ML-DSA?
  A) Integer Factorization Problem
  B) Discrete Logarithm Problem
  C) Learning With Errors (LWE)
  D) Hash Collision Resistance
Answer: C
Explanation: Lattice-based PQC algorithms rely on the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem and its structured variant, Module-LWE. These problems are believed to be hard for both classical and quantum computers. Integer factorization and discrete logarithms are the problems broken by quantum computers.

Q: Which of the following classical algorithms are vulnerable to quantum attacks via Shor's Algorithm? (Select all that apply)
  A) RSA
  B) AES-256
  C) ECDSA
  D) ECDH
Answer: A|C|D
Explanation: RSA, ECDSA, and ECDH are all broken by Shor's Algorithm because they rely on integer factorization or discrete logarithm problems. AES-256 is a symmetric cipher and is only weakened (to ~128-bit equivalent) by Grover's Algorithm, not broken.

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: What does Q-Day refer to?
  A) The day NIST publishes final PQC standards
  B) The day a quantum computer first breaks a widely used public-key algorithm
  C) The deadline for organizations to complete PQC migration
  D) The day quantum key distribution becomes commercially available
Answer: B
Explanation: Q-Day is the hypothetical future date when a cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC) first breaks a widely deployed public-key cryptographic algorithm like RSA or ECC. It is a critical planning milestone because HNDL attacks mean data must be protected well before Q-Day arrives.

Q: According to the Global Risk Institute survey, what is the estimated probability of a CRQC appearing within 10 years?
  A) Less than 1%
  B) 5-14%
  C) 19-34%
  D) Over 50%
Answer: C
Explanation: The Global Risk Institute survey estimates a 19-34% probability of a CRQC within 10 years and 5-14% within 5 years. This uncertainty drives the urgency of starting PQC migration now rather than waiting for certainty.

Q: What is the average organizational quantum-safe readiness score according to cross-industry assessments?
  A) 25 out of 100
  B) 50 out of 100
  C) 75 out of 100
  D) 90 out of 100
Answer: A
Explanation: The average organizational quantum-safe readiness score is only 25 out of 100. Additionally, 70% of organizations lack a complete cryptographic inventory, highlighting the urgent need for CBOM development and PQC migration planning.

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: True or False: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) is the same as post-quantum cryptography.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. QKD uses quantum physics to distribute encryption keys over optical channels and requires specialized hardware. PQC uses mathematical algorithms that run on classical computers. They are complementary but distinct approaches to quantum-safe security.

Q: What percentage of organizations lack a complete cryptographic inventory according to cross-industry data?
  A) 30%
  B) 50%
  C) 70%
  D) 90%
Answer: C
Explanation: 70% of organizations lack a complete cryptographic inventory (CBOM). Without knowing what cryptographic algorithms and protocols are deployed, organizations cannot effectively plan or execute PQC migration.

Q: In the context of HNDL attacks, which type of data has the longest sensitivity window?
  A) Marketing campaign data
  B) Government classified documents (25-75+ years)
  C) Quarterly financial reports
  D) Social media analytics
Answer: B
Explanation: TOP SECRET government data requires protection for 25-75+ years, making it the highest-priority target for HNDL attacks. Adversaries actively harvesting encrypted government communications today can store them for future decryption by quantum computers.

Q: What does the acronym PQC stand for?
  A) Pre-Quantum Computing
  B) Post-Quantum Cryptography
  C) Parallel Quantum Channels
  D) Public Quantum Certificates
Answer: B
Explanation: PQC stands for Post-Quantum Cryptography — the field of cryptographic algorithms designed to be secure against attacks from both classical and quantum computers. These algorithms run on existing classical hardware.

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: Which of the following are types of PQC algorithm families? (Select all that apply)
  A) Lattice-based
  B) Code-based
  C) Hash-based
  D) Blockchain-based
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: Lattice-based (ML-KEM, ML-DSA), code-based (HQC, Classic McEliece), and hash-based (SLH-DSA, XMSS, LMS) are the three main PQC algorithm families. Blockchain is not a cryptographic algorithm family.

Q: What is the Mosca Inequality and why is it important for PQC planning?
  A) A mathematical proof that quantum computers are impossible
  B) A framework for determining when to begin PQC migration based on data shelf life and migration time
  C) A benchmark for PQC algorithm performance
  D) A theorem about lattice security bounds
Answer: B
Explanation: The Mosca Inequality states: if the time your data needs protection (x) plus migration time (y) exceeds the time until a CRQC arrives (z), i.e., x + y > z, then migration must begin immediately. It is a key decision framework for PQC migration urgency.

Q: According to IBM's quantum roadmap, what milestone was achieved with the Kookaburra processor?
  A) First 100-qubit processor
  B) First modular 1,386-qubit processor
  C) First error-corrected quantum computer
  D) First commercial quantum advantage demonstration
Answer: B
Explanation: IBM's Kookaburra processor (2026) is the first modular quantum processor with 1,386 qubits, representing a significant step toward cryptographically relevant quantum computing. This milestone increases the urgency of PQC migration across all industries.

chunks.title: Quiz: PQC Fundamentals
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: PQC Fundamentals

Q: True or False: All public-key cryptographic algorithms currently in use (RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, EdDSA, DH, DSA) will be disallowed by NIST after 2035.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. NIST IR 8547 establishes that all classical public-key algorithms — RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, EdDSA, DH, and DSA — will be deprecated by 2030 and entirely disallowed by 2035 in systems subject to NIST guidelines.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: What was ML-KEM formerly known as during the NIST PQC competition?
  A) Falcon
  B) SPHINCS+
  C) CRYSTALS-Kyber
  D) NTRU
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-KEM (Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism) was originally known as CRYSTALS-Kyber during the NIST PQC standardization competition. It was standardized as FIPS 203 in August 2024.

Q: Which PQC algorithm family does SLH-DSA (formerly SPHINCS+) belong to?
  A) Lattice-based
  B) Code-based
  C) Hash-based
  D) Multivariate
Answer: C
Explanation: SLH-DSA (Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm) is hash-based. Its security relies solely on the properties of hash functions, making it one of the most conservative PQC signature schemes. It was standardized as FIPS 205.

Q: Which PQC algorithm was selected by NIST in March 2025 as a backup KEM to provide algorithmic diversity from ML-KEM?
  A) FrodoKEM
  B) Classic McEliece
  C) HQC
  D) BIKE
Answer: C
Explanation: HQC (Hamming Quasi-Cyclic) was selected in March 2025 as the 5th PQC standard. As a code-based KEM, it provides algorithmic diversity from the lattice-based ML-KEM, serving as a backup if lattice-based vulnerabilities are discovered.

Q: What is the key advantage of FN-DSA (formerly Falcon) over ML-DSA?
  A) Stronger security at the same parameter level
  B) Much more compact signatures
  C) Faster key generation
  D) Simpler implementation requirements
Answer: B
Explanation: FN-DSA produces extremely compact signatures (~0.65KB for FN-DSA-512 vs ~2.4KB for ML-DSA-44). This makes it ideal for certificates and constrained devices. However, it requires careful floating-point implementation to resist side-channel attacks.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: What is the approximate public key size of Classic McEliece-8192128, and why does this limit its deployment?
  A) ~1.3MB — too large for most network protocols and constrained devices
  B) ~64KB — manageable but requires protocol adjustments
  C) ~256 bytes — comparable to classical algorithms
  D) ~10KB — similar to FrodoKEM
Answer: A
Explanation: Classic McEliece-8192128 has public keys of approximately 1.3MB, making it impractical for most network protocols (TLS, SSH) and constrained environments. However, it is one of the oldest and most conservative PQC proposals (dating to 1978) with extremely fast decapsulation.

Q: True or False: ML-KEM is a digital signature algorithm.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. ML-KEM (FIPS 203) is a Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM) used for key exchange, not for digital signatures. ML-DSA (FIPS 204) is the corresponding lattice-based digital signature algorithm.

Q: What distinguishes FrodoKEM from ML-KEM in terms of security assumptions?
  A) FrodoKEM uses code-based security while ML-KEM uses lattice-based
  B) FrodoKEM uses "plain" LWE while ML-KEM uses structured Module-LWE
  C) FrodoKEM uses hash-based security while ML-KEM uses lattice-based
  D) They have identical security assumptions with different key sizes
Answer: B
Explanation: FrodoKEM is based on the "plain" Learning With Errors (LWE) problem, which makes fewer structural assumptions than ML-KEM's Module-LWE. This makes FrodoKEM more conservative but significantly slower (10-20x) and with much larger keys (9-21KB vs 0.8-1.6KB).

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: Which of the following are lattice-based PQC algorithms? (Select all that apply)
  A) ML-KEM
  B) HQC
  C) ML-DSA
  D) FN-DSA
Answer: A|C|D
Explanation: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and FN-DSA are all lattice-based. ML-KEM and ML-DSA use Module-LWE, while FN-DSA uses NTRU lattices. HQC is code-based, using Hamming quasi-cyclic codes for its security.

Q: What is the main trade-off between SLH-DSA "small" (s) and "fast" (f) variants?
  A) Small variants have larger keys but faster verification
  B) Small variants produce smaller signatures but are much slower to sign
  C) Fast variants use weaker hash functions for speed
  D) There is no meaningful difference — they are interchangeable
Answer: B
Explanation: SLH-DSA "small" (s) variants produce compact signatures but are 50-500x slower at signing. "Fast" (f) variants sign much faster (~5x relative) but produce larger signatures (~17KB for SHA2-128f). Verification speed is similar for both.

Q: What was ML-DSA formerly known as during the NIST PQC competition?
  A) CRYSTALS-Dilithium
  B) CRYSTALS-Kyber
  C) SPHINCS+
  D) Rainbow
Answer: A
Explanation: ML-DSA (Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm) was originally known as CRYSTALS-Dilithium. It is the primary PQC digital signature algorithm, standardized as FIPS 204 in August 2024.

Q: Which stateful hash-based signature schemes are recommended by NIST SP 800-208 for firmware and software signing?
  A) SLH-DSA and ML-DSA
  B) XMSS and LMS/HSS
  C) FN-DSA and HQC
  D) Ed25519 and ECDSA
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST SP 800-208 recommends XMSS (eXtended Merkle Signature Scheme, RFC 8391) and LMS/HSS (Leighton-Micali Signature / Hierarchical Signature System) for firmware and software signing. These are stateful schemes that require careful key state management.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: True or False: HQC is a lattice-based key encapsulation mechanism.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. HQC (Hamming Quasi-Cyclic) is a code-based KEM, not lattice-based. It was specifically selected to provide algorithmic diversity from the lattice-based ML-KEM, ensuring a fallback exists if lattice-based approaches are compromised.

Q: What is the public key size of ML-KEM-768?
  A) 32 bytes
  B) 800 bytes
  C) 1184 bytes
  D) 1568 bytes
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-KEM-768 has a public key size of approximately 1,184 bytes. This is significantly larger than X25519 (32 bytes) but still manageable for most applications. ML-KEM-512 is 800 bytes, and ML-KEM-1024 is 1,568 bytes.

Q: What NIST Security Level does ML-KEM-512 provide?
  A) Level 1 (AES-128 equivalent)
  B) Level 3 (AES-192 equivalent)
  C) Level 5 (AES-256 equivalent)
  D) Level 2 (AES-128 equivalent)
Answer: A
Explanation: ML-KEM-512 provides NIST Security Level 1, equivalent to the difficulty of breaking AES-128. Level 3 (AES-192) is ML-KEM-768, and Level 5 (AES-256) is ML-KEM-1024.

Q: What is the approximate signature size of FN-DSA-512 compared to ML-DSA-44?
  A) FN-DSA-512 is larger (~3.3KB vs ~2.4KB)
  B) FN-DSA-512 is about one-quarter the size (~666 bytes vs ~2420 bytes)
  C) They are the same size
  D) FN-DSA-512 does not produce signatures
Answer: B
Explanation: FN-DSA-512 produces signatures of approximately 666 bytes, compared to ML-DSA-44's 2,420 bytes — roughly one-quarter the size. This compactness makes FN-DSA ideal for bandwidth-constrained applications like certificates and IoT devices.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: What is the approximate stack RAM requirement for FrodoKEM-1344, and why does this matter?
  A) ~4KB — similar to ML-KEM
  B) ~180KB — too large for most IoT devices
  C) ~300 bytes — extremely efficient
  D) ~32KB — moderate for embedded systems
Answer: B
Explanation: FrodoKEM-1344 requires approximately 180KB of stack RAM, compared to ~6KB for ML-KEM-768. This makes FrodoKEM impractical for IoT and constrained devices despite its more conservative security assumptions based on plain LWE.

Q: True or False: All three ML-KEM variants (512/768/1024) produce the same 32-byte shared secret.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. All ML-KEM variants — ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, and ML-KEM-1024 — produce a 32-byte shared secret after decapsulation, regardless of security level. The variants differ in public key size, ciphertext size, and security strength.

Q: Which algorithm family does HQC belong to?
  A) Lattice-based
  B) Hash-based
  C) Code-based (error-correcting codes)
  D) Multivariate polynomial
Answer: C
Explanation: HQC (Hamming Quasi-Cyclic) belongs to the code-based algorithm family, using error-correcting codes for its security. It was selected by NIST in March 2025 specifically to provide algorithmic diversity from the lattice-based ML-KEM.

Q: How does SLH-DSA signing speed compare to RSA-2048 signing speed for the SHA2-128s (small) variant?
  A) About the same speed
  B) ~5x slower
  C) ~50-500x slower
  D) ~10x faster
Answer: C
Explanation: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s signing is approximately 500x slower than RSA-2048. The "small" (s) variants prioritize compact signatures (~7.8KB) at the cost of very slow signing. The "fast" (f) variants trade larger signatures for much better signing speed.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: Which of the following PQC algorithms are digital signature schemes? (Select all that apply)
  A) ML-DSA
  B) ML-KEM
  C) SLH-DSA
  D) FN-DSA
Answer: A|C|D
Explanation: ML-DSA (FIPS 204), SLH-DSA (FIPS 205), and FN-DSA (FIPS 206 draft) are all digital signature algorithms. ML-KEM (FIPS 203) is a Key Encapsulation Mechanism for key exchange, not signatures.

Q: What type of cryptographic operation does ML-KEM-1024 (CNSA 2.0 required) perform?
  A) Digital signature generation
  B) Key encapsulation for establishing shared secrets
  C) Password hashing
  D) Random number generation
Answer: B
Explanation: ML-KEM-1024 is a Key Encapsulation Mechanism at NIST Security Level 5 (AES-256 equivalent). It is the variant required by CNSA 2.0 for National Security Systems and replaces classical key exchange algorithms like ECDH P-521 and RSA-4096.

Q: What is the approximate public key size of SLH-DSA compared to ML-DSA?
  A) SLH-DSA keys are much larger (~1.3KB vs ~32 bytes)
  B) SLH-DSA keys are much smaller (~32 bytes vs ~1.3KB)
  C) They are approximately the same size
  D) SLH-DSA does not use public keys
Answer: B
Explanation: SLH-DSA has remarkably small public keys (~32 bytes for 128-bit variants), compared to ML-DSA-44's ~1,312 bytes. However, SLH-DSA compensates with much larger signatures (~7.8-16KB) and slower signing speeds.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: What type of mathematical problem does HQC (Hamming Quasi-Cyclic) rely on for its security?
  A) Lattice problems (shortest vector problem)
  B) Code-based problems (decoding random linear codes)
  C) Hash-based one-way functions
  D) Isogeny problems on elliptic curves
Answer: B
Explanation: HQC is a code-based KEM whose security relies on the hardness of decoding random linear codes over binary fields — specifically, the Hamming metric decoding problem. This is a fundamentally different hardness assumption from lattice-based schemes like ML-KEM, providing algorithmic diversity in the PQC portfolio. Code-based cryptography has been studied since McEliece's 1978 proposal, giving it one of the longest security track records in PQC.

Q: Why did NIST select HQC as a fourth PQC standard in March 2025, rather than relying solely on ML-KEM?
  A) HQC has smaller ciphertext sizes than ML-KEM
  B) HQC provides algorithmic diversity based on a different hardness assumption (code-based vs. lattice)
  C) HQC is faster than ML-KEM for all use cases
  D) HQC was submitted earlier in the NIST competition
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST selected HQC to serve as a backup KEM based on a completely different mathematical foundation from ML-KEM's lattice problem. If a breakthrough algorithm were discovered to efficiently solve lattice problems, ML-KEM would be broken — but HQC would remain secure. This redundancy mirrors the NIST strategy of standardizing both ML-DSA (lattice) and SLH-DSA (hash-based): defense-in-depth through algorithmic diversity.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: How does HQC's ciphertext size compare to ML-KEM-768?
  A) HQC produces smaller ciphertexts — approximately 800 bytes vs ML-KEM-768's 1,088 bytes
  B) HQC produces larger ciphertexts — approximately 4,522 bytes vs ML-KEM-768's 1,088 bytes
  C) They are equal — both were designed to the same NIST security Level 3 ciphertext target
  D) HQC ciphertext size varies dynamically based on message length
Answer: B
Explanation: HQC-128 ciphertext is approximately 4,522 bytes — roughly four times larger than ML-KEM-768's 1,088 bytes at equivalent security level. This size tradeoff is the primary deployment consideration: HQC is not a drop-in replacement for ML-KEM in bandwidth-constrained protocols like TLS. It is intended as a parallel alternative for systems where algorithmic diversity justifies the bandwidth cost.

Q: True or False: HQC is expected to receive its own FIPS number (distinct from FIPS 203) approximately in 2027.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. NIST announced HQC selection in March 2025 with a projected standardization timeline of 2026–2027. HQC will receive its own dedicated FIPS standard number, separate from FIPS 203 (ML-KEM). During the interim period, organizations can reference the HQC draft specification directly. The code-based design means it requires its own validation test vectors and CAVP testing protocol.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: Which statement best describes HQC's performance characteristics relative to ML-KEM-512?
  A) HQC-128 has faster key generation but slower encapsulation than ML-KEM-512
  B) HQC-128 has roughly comparable decapsulation speed but approximately 4× larger public keys and ciphertexts than ML-KEM-512
  C) HQC-128 is strictly faster in all operations due to quasi-cyclic structure
  D) HQC-128 requires hardware acceleration to achieve practical performance
Answer: B
Explanation: HQC uses quasi-cyclic codes which enable efficient polynomial multiplication, giving decapsulation performance roughly comparable to ML-KEM. However, HQC public keys (~3,002 bytes for HQC-128) and ciphertexts (~4,522 bytes) are significantly larger than ML-KEM-512 (800-byte public key, 768-byte ciphertext). For most internet protocols, ML-KEM is preferred; HQC is deployed when algorithmic diversity justifies the bandwidth overhead.

Q: What is the standardized name for the FALCON signature scheme in FIPS 206?
  A) ML-DSA
  B) FN-DSA (FALCON)
  C) SLH-DSA
  D) HQC-Sign
Answer: B
Explanation: FALCON was standardized as FN-DSA (FIPS 206) by NIST, published in 2024. FN-DSA stands for 'Module Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm over NTRU Lattices.' It produces the most compact signatures among NIST-standardized schemes: FN-DSA-512 generates ~666-byte signatures with an 897-byte public key — roughly one-quarter the size of ML-DSA-44 signatures (2,420 bytes). This compactness makes FN-DSA attractive for IoT and bandwidth-sensitive deployments.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: What is the primary implementation challenge that makes FN-DSA (FALCON) harder to deploy safely than ML-DSA?
  A) FN-DSA requires a larger key size than ML-DSA for equivalent security
  B) FN-DSA's Gaussian sampling during signing requires high-precision floating-point arithmetic that is vulnerable to timing side-channel attacks
  C) FN-DSA is not standardized by NIST and lacks FIPS validation
  D) FN-DSA signatures are not compatible with X.509 certificates
Answer: B
Explanation: FN-DSA signing requires sampling from a discrete Gaussian distribution over NTRU lattices, which traditionally uses floating-point arithmetic. This creates timing side-channel vulnerabilities: if signing time leaks information about the secret key, attackers can reconstruct keys. Safe implementations require constant-time integer arithmetic (avoiding floating-point entirely) or hardware-assisted sampling. ML-DSA avoids this issue by using rejection sampling from a simpler uniform distribution. This is why ML-DSA is preferred for general-purpose signing despite FN-DSA's smaller signatures.

chunks.title: Quiz: Algorithm Families
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Algorithm Families

Q: For a constrained IoT firmware signing use case where bandwidth is critical, what is the tradeoff between selecting FN-DSA-512 vs ML-DSA-44?
  A) FN-DSA-512 is always the correct choice — smaller signatures always win in IoT
  B) ML-DSA-44 is always correct — its simpler signing algorithm outweighs FN-DSA's compactness
  C) FN-DSA-512 offers ~3.6× smaller signatures but requires careful constant-time implementation; ML-DSA-44 is easier to implement safely but produces larger firmware manifests
