  D) Both have identical signature sizes; the only difference is key generation speed
Answer: C
Explanation: FN-DSA-512 signatures are ~666 bytes vs ML-DSA-44's 2,420 bytes — a 3.6× reduction that meaningfully affects OTA update bandwidth on narrowband IoT links. However, FN-DSA requires careful constant-time Gaussian sampling to prevent timing side channels; a naive implementation is dangerous. ML-DSA-44 uses simpler rejection sampling, making it easier to implement correctly with standard toolchains. For high-volume firmware updates or NB-IoT deployments, FN-DSA's compactness can justify the extra implementation care. For general enterprise PKI, ML-DSA is preferred.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: Which FIPS standard defines ML-KEM?
  A) FIPS 203
  B) FIPS 204
  C) FIPS 205
  D) FIPS 206
Answer: A
Explanation: FIPS 203 defines ML-KEM (Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism). FIPS 204 is ML-DSA, FIPS 205 is SLH-DSA, and FIPS 206 (draft) is FN-DSA.

Q: When were FIPS 203, 204, and 205 published?
  A) January 2023
  B) August 2024
  C) March 2025
  D) January 2026
Answer: B
Explanation: FIPS 203 (ML-KEM), FIPS 204 (ML-DSA), and FIPS 205 (SLH-DSA) were all published by NIST in August 2024, marking a historic milestone in post-quantum cryptography standardization.

Q: NIST Security Level 3 is comparable to breaking which symmetric cipher?
  A) AES-128
  B) AES-192
  C) AES-256
  D) 3DES
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST Security Level 3 provides security comparable to breaking AES-192. Level 1 corresponds to AES-128, and Level 5 corresponds to AES-256. Level 3 is the recommended minimum for most general-use applications.

Q: According to NIST IR 8547, by which year will classical public-key algorithms like RSA and ECDSA be deprecated?
  A) 2027
  B) 2030
  C) 2033
  D) 2035
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST IR 8547 establishes a two-phase timeline: RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, EdDSA, DH, and DSA will be deprecated by 2030, and entirely disallowed by 2035.

Q: What is the current status of FIPS 206 (FN-DSA)?
  A) Published and finalized
  B) Draft standard, expected finalization in late 2026 or 2027
  C) Withdrawn from consideration
  D) Replaced by a different algorithm
Answer: B
Explanation: FIPS 206 (FN-DSA, formerly Falcon) is currently in draft status. The initial public draft was released in November 2024 and is expected to be finalized in late 2026 or early 2027. It provides compact signatures valuable for certificates and constrained devices.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: True or False: NIST Security Level 5 provides the highest level of post-quantum security defined by NIST.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. NIST Security Level 5 is the highest, comparable to breaking AES-256. It is typically recommended for government, defense, and scenarios requiring maximum long-term security. Level 3 (AES-192 equivalent) is generally recommended for most applications.

Q: What does NIST SP 800-227 provide guidance on?
  A) Digital signature algorithm selection
  B) Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM) recommendations
  C) Hash function security requirements
  D) Random number generation standards
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST SP 800-227, published in September 2025, provides recommendations for Key Encapsulation Mechanisms (KEMs), including guidance on ML-KEM parameter selection and hybrid key exchange approaches.

Q: Which ML-KEM variant does NIST recommend for general-use applications?
  A) ML-KEM-512 (Level 1)
  B) ML-KEM-768 (Level 3)
  C) ML-KEM-1024 (Level 5)
  D) All variants are equally recommended
Answer: B
Explanation: ML-KEM-768 (NIST Security Level 3) is recommended for general-use applications. It provides a balance of security and performance. ML-KEM-1024 (Level 5) is reserved for government/defense use cases requiring the highest security.

Q: Which PQC algorithms were standardized by NIST in the first batch (August 2024)? (Select all that apply)
  A) ML-KEM (FIPS 203)
  B) ML-DSA (FIPS 204)
  C) SLH-DSA (FIPS 205)
  D) FN-DSA (FIPS 206)
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: ML-KEM (FIPS 203), ML-DSA (FIPS 204), and SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) were all published in August 2024. FN-DSA (FIPS 206) remains a draft standard expected for finalization in late 2026 or early 2027.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: According to NIST IR 8547, what is the difference between "deprecated" and "disallowed" for legacy algorithms?
  A) They mean the same thing — the algorithm cannot be used
  B) Deprecated means discouraged but still permitted; disallowed means prohibited entirely
  C) Deprecated applies to software; disallowed applies to hardware
  D) Deprecated is a warning; disallowed means fines are imposed
Answer: B
Explanation: Deprecated (2030) means the algorithm is strongly discouraged for new deployments but may still be used for legacy compatibility. Disallowed (2035) means the algorithm must not be used under any circumstances in systems subject to NIST guidelines.

Q: How many PQC algorithms has NIST standardized or selected as of early 2026?
  A) 2
  B) 3
  C) 5
  D) 8
Answer: C
Explanation: NIST has standardized or selected 5 PQC algorithms: ML-KEM (FIPS 203), ML-DSA (FIPS 204), and SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) published August 2024; FN-DSA (FIPS 206) as a draft standard August 2025; and HQC selected March 2025 with a draft expected in 2026.

Q: What is NIST CMVP and why is it a bottleneck for PQC adoption?
  A) A quantum computing simulator
  B) The Cryptographic Module Validation Program — PQC implementations must be certified before federal use
  C) A database of deprecated algorithms
  D) A testing tool for algorithm performance
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST's Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP) validates cryptographic implementations under FIPS 140-3. PQC algorithm modules (FIPS 203/204/205) must pass CMVP certification before deployment in federal systems, creating a multi-year bottleneck in migration timelines.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: What is the timeline difference between NIST's "deprecated" (2030) and "disallowed" (2035) milestones?
  A) 1 year
  B) 3 years
  C) 5 years
  D) 10 years
Answer: C
Explanation: NIST IR 8547 establishes a 5-year gap: classical algorithms are deprecated in 2030 (discouraged but still permitted for legacy) and disallowed in 2035 (prohibited entirely). This gives organizations 5 years of transition between soft and hard deadlines.

Q: True or False: NIST has defined 5 security levels for PQC algorithms, with Level 1 being the weakest and Level 5 the strongest.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. NIST defines 5 security levels: Level 1 (AES-128), Level 2 (SHA-256 collision), Level 3 (AES-192), Level 4 (SHA-384 collision), and Level 5 (AES-256). Levels 1, 3, and 5 are the most commonly used for PQC algorithm parameter sets.

Q: When was the NIST HQC selection announced, and what milestone does it represent?
  A) August 2024 — the 4th standard
  B) March 11, 2025 — the 5th PQC standard
  C) January 2026 — the 6th standard
  D) December 2025 — a backup to SLH-DSA
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST announced HQC as the 5th PQC standard on March 11, 2025. As a code-based KEM, it provides critical algorithmic diversity from the lattice-based ML-KEM, serving as insurance against potential future vulnerabilities in lattice-based cryptography.

Q: Which NIST Special Publication provides guidance on Key Encapsulation Mechanisms published in September 2025?
  A) SP 800-131A
  B) SP 800-208
  C) SP 800-227
  D) SP 800-57
Answer: C
Explanation: NIST SP 800-227, published in September 2025, provides recommendations for Key Encapsulation Mechanisms (KEMs) including guidance on ML-KEM parameter selection, hybrid key exchange approaches, and best practices for KEM deployment.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: Which classical algorithms does NIST IR 8547 target for deprecation by 2030? (Select all that apply)
  A) RSA
  B) AES-256
  C) ECDSA
  D) EdDSA
Answer: A|C|D
Explanation: NIST IR 8547 targets all asymmetric/public-key algorithms for deprecation by 2030: RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, EdDSA, DH, and DSA. AES-256 is a symmetric algorithm and is not targeted — it remains quantum-safe with sufficient key sizes.

Q: What is the expected timeline for FIPS 206 (FN-DSA) finalization, and when was the draft submitted?
  A) Draft August 2024, final 2025
  B) Draft November 2024, final late 2026 or early 2027
  C) Draft March 2025, final 2026
  D) Draft January 2026, final 2027
Answer: B
Explanation: FIPS 206 (FN-DSA, formerly Falcon) had its Initial Public Draft (IPD) released in November 2024. Finalization is expected in late 2026 or early 2027 due to intellectual property and internal clearance considerations.

Q: Which FIPS standard defines SLH-DSA (formerly SPHINCS+)?
  A) FIPS 203
  B) FIPS 204
  C) FIPS 205
  D) FIPS 206
Answer: C
Explanation: FIPS 205 defines SLH-DSA (Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm). FIPS 203 is ML-KEM, FIPS 204 is ML-DSA, and FIPS 206 (draft) is FN-DSA.

Q: What does NIST Security Level 1 correspond to in terms of symmetric cipher strength?
  A) AES-64
  B) AES-128
  C) AES-192
  D) AES-256
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST Security Level 1 corresponds to the difficulty of breaking AES-128. It is the minimum security level for PQC algorithms. ML-KEM-512 and SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s provide Level 1 security.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: When did NIST announce HQC as the fourth PQC standard, and what type of cryptographic algorithm is it?
  A) October 2022; a lattice-based KEM
  B) March 2025; a code-based KEM (Key Encapsulation Mechanism)
  C) November 2024; a hash-based digital signature
  D) January 2026; an isogeny-based key exchange
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST announced HQC (Hamming Quasi-Cyclic) as a fourth PQC standard in March 2025, selected as a code-based KEM to complement ML-KEM (lattice-based). The selection was motivated by algorithmic diversity: HQC's security relies on the hardness of decoding random linear codes — a different mathematical problem from ML-KEM's lattice assumptions. HQC is expected to receive a dedicated FIPS number in 2026–2027, following NIST's standard publication process.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: Why does HQC's selection as a fourth NIST standard NOT create urgency for organizations to immediately deploy it instead of ML-KEM?
  A) HQC is not quantum-safe; it was selected for classical security only
  B) HQC is not yet standardized as a FIPS document; it lacks the validation infrastructure (CAVP, CMVP) and has larger keys/ciphertexts — ML-KEM should be primary, with HQC as a future hedge
  C) HQC replaces ML-KEM and must be deployed before 2027
  D) HQC is identical to ML-KEM-768 with different parameter names
Answer: B
Explanation: HQC, though announced as a fourth NIST standard, is not yet published as a FIPS document and lacks CAVP test vectors and CMVP validation modules. Organizations requiring FIPS 140-3 validated cryptography cannot yet use HQC in validated boundaries. Additionally, HQC has 4–5× larger key and ciphertext sizes than ML-KEM, making it unsuitable as a direct replacement. The correct approach: deploy ML-KEM now (FIPS 203, validated modules available), and plan crypto-agile architecture to add HQC as a parallel hedge when its FIPS is published and validated implementations are available.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: What does FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance (IG) require for modules claiming support for FIPS 203 (ML-KEM), FIPS 204 (ML-DSA), or FIPS 205 (SLH-DSA)?
  A) Modules must implement all three PQC algorithms to receive FIPS 140-3 validation
  B) Modules must include algorithm-specific Known Answer Tests (KATs) and self-tests for each PQC algorithm during power-up
  C) PQC algorithms are exempt from FIPS 140-3 self-test requirements until 2028
  D) Only hardware modules require PQC self-tests; software modules are exempt
Answer: B
Explanation: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance (updated 2025 for PQC) requires that cryptographic modules claiming FIPS 203/204/205 support implement power-up self-tests: Known Answer Tests (KATs) for each PQC algorithm variant implemented. This is the same requirement applied to AES, SHA, and ECDSA. The NIST CAVP (Cryptographic Algorithm Validation Program) provides official KAT vectors for ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA. Module vendors must include these self-tests to receive FIPS 140-3 validation for PQC algorithm support.

chunks.title: Quiz: nist-standards
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: nist-standards

Q: A vendor's HSM currently holds FIPS 140-3 Level 3 validation for RSA-4096 and AES-256. They add ML-KEM-768 support via firmware update. What must happen for the HSM to claim FIPS 140-3 validated ML-KEM-768?
  A) The existing FIPS 140-3 certificate automatically extends to cover new algorithms added by firmware update
  B) The vendor must submit a new FIPS 140-3 validation or a delta change request, including CAVP-validated ML-KEM KAT vectors and updated security policy documentation
  C) The vendor only needs to pass NIST's online ACVTS tests — no formal CMVP resubmission is required
  D) FIPS 140-3 validation for PQC is waived for Level 3 HSMs until 2026
Answer: B
Explanation: Adding ML-KEM-768 to an existing FIPS 140-3 validated module requires a formal change to the validation: either a new certificate submission or a delta/modification request through the CMVP. The updated module must include CAVP-validated KAT vectors from NIST's ACVTS for ML-KEM, add ML-KEM to the Security Policy document, and pass all physical/operational testing for the new algorithm. Simply passing online ACVTS tests provides CAVP algorithm validation but does not constitute FIPS 140-3 module validation. Until resubmission is approved, the HSM cannot claim FIPS 140-3 validated ML-KEM.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: What is a Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM)?
  A) A list of cryptographic hardware prices
  B) An inventory of all cryptographic algorithms, protocols, and keys used in a system
  C) A government mandate for cryptographic compliance
  D) A quantum computer component specification
Answer: B
Explanation: A CBOM is a comprehensive inventory of cryptographic assets — algorithms, key lengths, certificates, protocols, and libraries — used across an organization. It is the essential first step in PQC migration planning, similar to a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for cryptography.

Q: In the 7-phase PQC migration framework, what is the recommended first step?
  A) Deploy hybrid certificates to production
  B) Assessment & Inventory — build a CBOM and identify vulnerable algorithms
  C) Purchase quantum-safe HSMs
  D) Notify regulators of migration plans
Answer: B
Explanation: The first phase is Assessment & Inventory (4-8 weeks): building a Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM), identifying quantum-vulnerable algorithms, assessing HNDL risk for long-lived data, and mapping certificate chains.

Q: During PQC migration, which systems should be prioritized first for protection?
  A) Internal development tools
  B) External-facing systems handling long-lived sensitive data
  C) Marketing websites
  D) Employee email (internal only)
Answer: B
Explanation: External-facing systems (TLS endpoints, VPNs) handling data with long confidentiality lifetimes should be prioritized because they are most vulnerable to HNDL attacks. Data intercepted today from these systems could be decrypted by future quantum computers.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: True or False: Organizations should wait until a CRQC exists before beginning their PQC migration.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. Due to HNDL attacks, organizations must begin migration now. Data intercepted today can be stored and decrypted later. Additionally, large-scale migrations take years to complete, so starting early is essential to meet 2030/2035 deprecation deadlines.

Q: What is the recommended approach for initial PQC deployment in production TLS?
  A) Replace all classical algorithms immediately with PQC-only
  B) Deploy hybrid key exchange (e.g., X25519 + ML-KEM-768)
  C) Disable TLS until PQC is fully mature
  D) Use quantum key distribution instead
Answer: B
Explanation: Hybrid key exchange (combining classical + PQC algorithms) is the recommended initial approach. It maintains security even if one algorithm is broken and ensures backward compatibility. X25519MLKEM768 is already deployed in major browsers like Chrome and Firefox.

Q: Why is re-encrypting archived data an important part of PQC migration?
  A) To reduce storage costs
  B) To counter HNDL threats against already-stored encrypted data
  C) To improve data compression ratios
  D) To comply with GDPR right-to-be-forgotten
Answer: B
Explanation: Archived data encrypted with quantum-vulnerable algorithms is susceptible to HNDL attacks — adversaries may have already captured copies. Re-encrypting with PQC algorithms ensures this data remains protected even when quantum computers become available.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: Which of the following are key activities in the "Testing & Validation" phase of PQC migration? (Select all that apply)
  A) Pilot hybrid TLS/SSH with ML-KEM + X25519
  B) Test VPN PQC tunnels with IKEv2
  C) Measure performance impact on production workloads
  D) Submit final compliance reports to regulators
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: The Testing & Validation phase (2-4 months) focuses on piloting hybrid TLS/SSH, testing VPN PQC tunnels, validating certificate chain interoperability, and measuring performance impact. Compliance reporting comes later during monitoring and optimization.

Q: Which framework document provides the formal PQC transition timeline with deprecation and disallowment dates?
  A) FIPS 203
  B) NIST IR 8547
  C) RFC 9629
  D) ETSI TR 103 619
Answer: B
Explanation: NIST IR 8547 (Transition to Post-Quantum Cryptography Standards) establishes the formal timeline: classical public-key algorithms deprecated by 2030 and disallowed by 2035.

Q: What is the estimated duration for the full "Hybrid Migration & Rollout" phase in a typical PQC migration?
  A) 1-2 weeks
  B) 1-2 months
  C) 6-12 months
  D) 3-5 years
Answer: C
Explanation: The Hybrid Migration & Rollout phase typically takes 6-12 months. This includes deploying hybrid certificates, migrating code signing to PQC, and rolling out hybrid key exchange to production TLS/SSH systems across the organization.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: What role do HSMs play in PQC migration planning?
  A) HSMs are unnecessary for PQC — software-only solutions are sufficient
  B) HSM firmware must be upgraded to support PQC algorithms before they can generate or store PQC keys
  C) HSMs automatically detect and block quantum attacks
  D) HSMs only store classical keys and cannot support PQC
Answer: B
Explanation: Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) require firmware upgrades to support PQC key generation, storage, and operations. This is a critical dependency in migration planning, as many HSM vendors are still developing PQC-capable firmware.

Q: What is the recommended duration for the "Assessment & Inventory" phase of PQC migration?
  A) 1-2 days
  B) 4-8 weeks
  C) 6-12 months
  D) 2-3 years
Answer: B
Explanation: The Assessment & Inventory phase typically takes 4-8 weeks. It involves building a Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM), identifying quantum-vulnerable algorithms, assessing HNDL risk for data with long confidentiality requirements, and mapping certificate chains.

Q: According to CNSA 2.0, by what year must new networking equipment for NSS use PQC?
  A) 2025
  B) 2026
  C) 2030
  D) 2035
Answer: B
Explanation: CNSA 2.0 requires new networking equipment acquisitions for National Security Systems to support PQC by 2026. New software/firmware signing must use PQC by 2025, and all deployed systems must use PQC exclusively by 2030.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: Microsoft targets completing its full PQC transition by what year?
  A) 2025
  B) 2030
  C) 2033
  D) 2035
Answer: C
Explanation: Microsoft's Quantum Safe Program targets full PQC transition completion by 2033 — two years ahead of the US government's 2035 disallowment deadline. This demonstrates industry leadership and the recognition that large-scale migrations require years of planning.

Q: True or False: A Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM) is similar to a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) but focused on cryptographic assets.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. A CBOM inventories cryptographic assets — algorithms, key lengths, certificates, protocols, and libraries — across an organization, similar to how an SBOM catalogs software components. CBOM is the essential first step in PQC migration.

Q: What is the estimated total duration for a complete 7-phase PQC migration?
  A) 1-3 months
  B) 6-12 months
  C) 18-36 months
  D) 5-10 years
Answer: C
Explanation: A complete 7-phase PQC migration typically takes 18-36 months: Assessment & Inventory (4-8 weeks), Risk Prioritization & Planning (4-6 weeks), Preparation & Tooling (2-4 months), Testing & Validation (2-4 months), Hybrid Migration & Rollout (6-12 months), Production Deployment (6-12 months), and Monitoring & Optimization (ongoing).

Q: Which of the following are phases in the 7-phase PQC migration framework? (Select all that apply)
  A) Assessment & Inventory
  B) Preparation & Tooling
  C) Testing & Validation
  D) Budget Approval
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: The 7 phases are: Assessment & Inventory, Risk Prioritization & Planning, Preparation & Tooling, Testing & Validation, Hybrid Migration & Rollout, Production Deployment, and Monitoring & Optimization. Budget approval is a prerequisite, not a migration phase.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: Why is certificate chain interoperability testing critical during PQC migration?
  A) Certificates are not affected by PQC
  B) PQC certificate signatures are larger and may break existing chain validation logic
  C) Classical certificates automatically upgrade to PQC
  D) Certificate testing is only needed for government systems
Answer: B
Explanation: PQC signatures (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA) are significantly larger than classical signatures. This can break certificate chain validation in systems with size limits, require protocol-level adjustments, and cause TLS handshake failures if intermediate CAs or root CAs have not been updated.

Q: What unique migration challenge does the phrase "hybrid certificates" address?
  A) Combining certificates from two different CAs
  B) Including both classical and PQC signatures so both legacy and PQC-capable systems can verify
  C) Merging expired and active certificates
  D) Creating certificates valid in multiple countries
Answer: B
Explanation: Hybrid certificates contain both classical (e.g., ECDSA) and PQC (e.g., ML-DSA) signatures. This enables gradual rollout: PQC-capable systems verify the quantum-safe signature, while legacy systems use the classical one, avoiding a hard cutover.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-planning
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-planning

Q: Which type of system should be migrated to PQC last in the migration priority order?
  A) External-facing VPN gateways
  B) Internal development tools and non-sensitive applications
  C) Customer-facing payment processing
  D) Long-term classified data storage
Answer: B
Explanation: Internal development tools and non-sensitive applications have the lowest HNDL risk and should be migrated last. External-facing systems handling sensitive data (VPNs, payment processing, classified storage) should be prioritized first due to their higher exposure to harvesting attacks.

Q: What is the key challenge with HSM firmware upgrades for PQC?
  A) HSMs are inexpensive to replace
  B) HSM vendors need to develop and certify PQC-capable firmware through FIPS 140-3 CMVP validation
  C) HSMs already support all PQC algorithms
  D) HSM firmware upgrades happen automatically
Answer: B
Explanation: HSM firmware upgrades for PQC require development, testing, and FIPS 140-3 CMVP certification — a multi-year process. Many HSM vendors are still developing PQC-capable firmware, making this a critical dependency in migration timelines.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What does CNSA 2.0 stand for?
  A) Certified National Security Architecture
  B) Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0
  C) Cryptographic NIST Standard Algorithm
  D) Central Network Security Authority
Answer: B
Explanation: CNSA 2.0 is the NSA's Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0. It mandates the transition to quantum-resistant algorithms for National Security Systems (NSS), with a phased timeline from 2025 to 2035.

Q: By what year does CNSA 2.0 require NSS software/firmware signing and networking equipment to use PQC exclusively?
  A) 2025
  B) 2027
  C) 2030
  D) 2035
Answer: C
Explanation: CNSA 2.0 requires NSS software/firmware signing and networking equipment to use PQC exclusively by 2030. The phased timeline: new software signing by 2025, new networking equipment by 2026, signing/networking fully deployed by 2030, web/cloud/OS/PKI by 2033, and all legacy cryptography disallowed by 2035.

Q: Which country has set the most aggressive PQC transition deadline, prohibiting traditional asymmetric cryptography by 2030?
  A) United States
  B) United Kingdom
  C) Australia
  D) France
Answer: C
Explanation: Australia (ASD) has set a 2030 deadline for prohibiting traditional asymmetric cryptography — 5 years ahead of the US/UK full-transition deadline of 2035 (though aligned with US CNSA 2.0 interim milestones for software signing which also target 2030). This makes Australia's timeline one of the most aggressive globally.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is ANSSI's phased approach to PQC transition?
  A) Immediate PQC-only deployment with no transition period
  B) Phase 1: Optional PQC (2024-2025), Phase 2: Hybrid required (2025-2030), Phase 3: Standalone PQC optional (2030+)
  C) Full transition by 2025 with no hybrid period
  D) ANSSI has not issued any PQC guidance
Answer: B
Explanation: France's ANSSI (Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information) follows a three-phase approach: Phase 1 (2024-2025) where PQC is optional, Phase 2 (2025-2030) requiring hybrid cryptography, and Phase 3 (2030-2035) where standalone PQC becomes optional.

Q: True or False: PCI DSS 4.0.1 currently mandates the use of PQC algorithms for payment card systems.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. PCI DSS 4.0.1 requires "strong cryptography" but does not yet specifically mandate PQC algorithms. However, as NIST deprecation deadlines approach (2030), PCI DSS is expected to follow NIST guidance.

Q: What is eIDAS 2.0 and how does it relate to PQC?
  A) A quantum computing standard for European banks
  B) The EU Digital Identity framework mandating digital wallets by 2027+ that will eventually need quantum-safe cryptography
  C) An encryption standard for European military systems
  D) A testing framework for PQC algorithm performance
Answer: B
Explanation: eIDAS 2.0 is the updated EU regulation for electronic identification and trust services. It mandates European Digital Identity (EUDI) wallets with deployments starting in 2027+. While eIDAS 2.0 does not yet mandate PQC specifically, these wallets will need quantum-safe cryptography to maintain long-term trust as quantum threats materialize. ENISA has identified wallet providers as high-impact entities for early PQC adoption.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is FIPS 140-3 and why is it relevant to PQC migration?
  A) It defines PQC algorithm parameters
  B) It is the standard for validating cryptographic modules — PQC implementations need CMVP certification
  C) It mandates quantum key distribution for government systems
  D) It is a deprecated standard replaced by FIPS 203
Answer: B
Explanation: FIPS 140-3 is the government standard for Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP) certification. PQC algorithm implementations (FIPS 203/204/205) must undergo CMVP validation before use in federal systems. This certification process is a key dependency in migration timelines.

Q: Which of the following compliance frameworks have explicit PQC transition requirements or guidance? (Select all that apply)
  A) CNSA 2.0 (NSA)
  B) ANSSI (France)
  C) NIST IR 8547
  D) Common Criteria (ISO/IEC 15408)
Answer: A|B|C|D
Explanation: All four frameworks address PQC transition: CNSA 2.0 mandates PQC for National Security Systems, ANSSI provides phased PQC guidance for France, NIST IR 8547 sets deprecation/disallowment timelines, and Common Criteria is developing emerging PQC protection profiles for international security evaluation.

Q: By what year must Canadian federal departments submit initial PQC migration plans to CCCS?
  A) 2025
  B) 2026
  C) 2030
  D) 2035
Answer: B
Explanation: Canada's CCCS requires federal departments to submit initial PQC migration plans by April 2026, with annual progress reporting. New IT procurements must include PQC vendor support clauses. High-priority systems must be migrated by 2031, and all systems fully transitioned by 2035.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is the EU Coordinated Implementation Roadmap for PQC?
  A) A binding regulation requiring immediate PQC deployment
  B) A coordinated guidance document targeting high-risk systems secured by 2030 and full transition by 2035
  C) A funding program for quantum computer development
  D) An EU-only alternative to NIST PQC standards
Answer: B
Explanation: The EU Coordinated Implementation Roadmap (published June 2025) provides guidance for member states to secure high-risk systems by 2030 and complete full PQC transition by 2035, aligned with NIST timelines but adapted for European regulatory contexts.

Q: Which country's CCCS requires PQC migration plans by 2026, high-priority systems by 2031, and full transition by 2035?
  A) United States
  B) Canada
  C) Germany
  D) Japan
Answer: B
Explanation: Canada's CCCS (Canadian Centre for Cyber Security) has a three-phase timeline: federal departments must submit PQC migration plans by April 2026 (with PQC procurement clauses), high-priority systems migrated by 2031, and all systems fully transitioned by 2035.

Q: What is the UK's NCSC three-phase PQC migration roadmap?
  A) Phase 1: 2024-2025 (planning), Phase 2: 2026-2028 (deployment), Phase 3: 2029-2030 (completion)
  B) Phase 1: 2025-2028 (discovery), Phase 2: 2028-2031 (priority migration), Phase 3: 2031-2035 (full migration)
  C) Phase 1: 2025 (PQC mandatory), Phase 2: 2026-2028 (enforcement), Phase 3: 2029 (audit)
  D) Phase 1: 2030-2032, Phase 2: 2032-2034, Phase 3: 2035
Answer: B
Explanation: The UK NCSC roadmap has three phases: Phase 1 (2025-2028) for discovery and assessment, Phase 2 (2028-2031) for priority migration of high-risk systems, and Phase 3 (2031-2035) for full migration of all remaining systems.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: Which country was the first EU member state to set a specific 2027 encryption migration deadline?
  A) Germany
  B) France
  C) Czech Republic
  D) Netherlands
Answer: C
Explanation: The Czech Republic was the first EU country to set a specific 2027 encryption migration deadline, ahead of the broader EU coordinated roadmap timelines. This demonstrates proactive compliance by individual member states.

Q: True or False: The EU Coordinated Implementation Roadmap targets high-risk systems secured by 2030, aligned with NIST timelines.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. The EU Coordinated Implementation Roadmap (v1.1, published June 2025) targets high-risk systems secured by 2030 and full PQC transition by 2035, deliberately aligned with NIST IR 8547 timelines.

Q: What is Executive Order 14306 and how does it advance PQC adoption?
  A) A European encryption standard
  B) A presidential order (June 2025) sustaining PQC migration, with CISA procurement guidance issued January 2026
  C) A quantum computing research grant program
  D) A NIST algorithm competition announcement
Answer: B
Explanation: Executive Order 14306, issued by the President in June 2025, sustains cybersecurity efforts including PQC migration provisions. CISA subsequently issued federal buying guidance in January 2026 requiring PQC capabilities in new product and service acquisitions, driving market demand for PQC-capable solutions.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is Germany's QUANTITY initiative launched in March 2025?
  A) A quantum computer manufacturing program
  B) A quantum cryptanalysis initiative by BSI and DLR to validate PQC algorithm security
  C) A financial incentive program for crypto companies
  D) A quantum key distribution network project
Answer: B
Explanation: Germany's QUANTITY initiative, launched in March 2025 by BSI (Federal Office for Information Security) and DLR (German Aerospace Center), focuses on quantum cryptanalysis to strengthen algorithm selection and validate PQC security. Separately, BSI targets critical applications being PQC-protected by end of 2030.

Q: Which countries have set PQC transition deadlines of 2030 or earlier for high-priority systems? (Select all that apply)
  A) Australia (ASD)
  B) United States (CNSA 2.0)
  C) Taiwan
  D) Czech Republic
Answer: A|B|C|D
Explanation: All four have aggressive timelines: Australia prohibits traditional crypto by 2030, CNSA 2.0 requires exclusive PQC for NSS networking/signing by 2030, Taiwan targets PQC migration for critical semiconductor and technology sectors by 2027, and Czech Republic set a 2027 encryption migration deadline.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is the G7 Cyber Expert Group recommendation from January 2026 regarding PQC?
  A) PQC is unnecessary for financial institutions
  B) Financial sector completing PQC migration by 2034, with phased preparation starting 2025
  C) All G7 nations must use quantum key distribution by 2028
  D) PQC research should be funded but not mandated
Answer: B
Explanation: The G7 Cyber Expert Group (January 2026, chaired by US Treasury and Bank of England) recommended a coordinated financial sector roadmap with phased milestones: awareness and preparation (2025-2027), discovery and cryptographic inventory (2025-2028), risk assessment and migration planning (2026-2029), and migration execution completing by 2034.

Q: According to Israel's PQC guidance, what was the deadline for completing quantum threat assessments?
  A) End of 2024
  B) End of 2025
  C) End of 2026
  D) End of 2030
Answer: B
Explanation: Israel's cybersecurity guidance set an end-of-2025 deadline for organizations to complete quantum threat assessments. PQC is also required in new contracts, demonstrating early proactive compliance.

Q: What does Europol report about executive preparedness for quantum threats?
  A) 86% of executives are well-prepared
  B) 86% of executives are unprepared for quantum threats
  C) 50% of organizations have completed PQC migration
  D) Only government organizations are at risk
Answer: B
Explanation: Europol reported in February 2025 that 86% of executives are unprepared for quantum threats. This highlights a significant gap between the urgency of the threat and organizational readiness across industries.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: Which organization runs the Korean Post-Quantum Cryptography (KpqC) competition, and approximately when are the finalized Korean national PQC standards expected?
  A) Korean ETRI; standards expected in 2025
  B) Korean IITP / KISA; standards expected 2029 with a 2035 migration deadline
  C) Samsung Research; standards expected 2028
  D) Korean NIS; standards to align exactly with NIST FIPS 203/204/205
Answer: B
Explanation: The KpqC competition is run by the Korean Institute of Information Security and Cryptology (KISA) under IITP. South Korea is developing independent national PQC standards — selecting HAETAE (lattice-based signatures), AIMer (MPC-in-the-head signatures), SMAUG-T (lattice-based KEM), and NTRU+ (NTRU-based KEM). Final Korean national standards are targeted for 2029, with a national migration deadline of 2035. This parallel track gives South Korea domestic cryptographic sovereignty separate from US FIPS standards.

Q: Which algorithm won the KpqC signature category and what is its mathematical basis?
  A) SMAUG-T; based on NTRU lattices
  B) HAETAE; based on module lattices (similar to ML-DSA but with distinct design choices)
  C) AIMer; based on MPC-in-the-head and symmetric primitives
  D) NTRU+; based on NTRU problem variants
Answer: B
Explanation: HAETAE is the KpqC signature winner, based on module lattice problems — the same mathematical family as NIST's ML-DSA, but with distinct parameter choices and security analysis. AIMer (MPC-in-the-head, hash-based) is the runner-up signature candidate. For KEMs, SMAUG-T (module lattices) and NTRU+ (NTRU lattices) were selected. The KpqC portfolio deliberately includes diverse mathematical foundations to hedge against potential algorithmic breakthroughs.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is the primary stated reason South Korea and China are developing national PQC standards independent of NIST's FIPS 203/204/205?
  A) NIST standards have known mathematical weaknesses that only Asian researchers discovered
  B) Domestic cryptographic sovereignty — ensuring national algorithms are not dependent on foreign standards for critical infrastructure
  C) NIST standards are not performance-optimized for Asian hardware architectures
  D) Both countries plan to replace NIST standards globally by 2030
Answer: B
Explanation: Both South Korea (KpqC) and China (NGCC/ICCS) frame their national PQC programs primarily around cryptographic sovereignty: ensuring that algorithms protecting national critical infrastructure are designed, analyzed, and standardized domestically, without reliance on US government processes. This mirrors longstanding patterns (China's SM2/SM3/SM4 replacing RSA/SHA/AES for domestic use; South Korea's ARIA/SEED). These national standards typically coexist with FIPS standards in international contexts but are mandated for government systems.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: What is China's NGCC program (Next-Generation Commercial Cryptography), and which body oversees its PQC standardization?
  A) A NIST-aligned adoption program run by China's MIIT; standards mirror FIPS 203/204/205
  B) An independent national PQC standardization program run by OSCCA/ICCS; selecting domestic algorithms with a goal of 2030 national migration
  C) A joint research initiative between China and the EU for hybrid PQC standards
  D) A military-only cryptography program with no commercial implications
Answer: B
Explanation: China's Next-Generation Commercial Cryptography (NGCC) program is run by the Office of the State Commercial Cryptography Administration (OSCCA) in coordination with ICCS (Institute of Commercial Cryptography Standardization). The program issued a call for PQC algorithm proposals in 2024, targeting algorithms distinct from US NIST selections. China's existing commercial cryptography ecosystem (SM2/SM3/SM4) provides a template: separate domestic standards for government and regulated industries, with international standards permitted in some contexts. The NGCC targets national migration by approximately 2030.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: By what date must Canadian federal government departments submit their initial PQC migration plans under CCCS guidance ITSM.40.001?
  A) January 2025
  B) April 2026
  C) December 2027
  D) October 2030
Answer: B
Explanation: The Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS) published ITSM.40.001 (PQC Migration Roadmap) in June 2025, requiring federal departments to submit initial PQC migration plans by April 2026. The roadmap mandates that high-priority systems complete PQC migration by 2030 and all federal systems by 2033. This aligns with the broader Five Eyes coordination on PQC timelines, though Canada's April 2026 planning deadline is one of the most near-term compliance milestones globally.

Q: True or False: Canada's TBS Security Policy Implementation Notice (SPIN) on PQC became effective in October 2025, making PQC migration planning mandatory for federal institutions.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (TBS) issued a Security Policy Implementation Notice (SPIN) on PQC that became effective October 9, 2025. This SPIN makes PQC migration planning a formal security policy requirement for all federal institutions — not merely guidance. Combined with ITSM.40.001's April 2026 planning deadline, Canadian federal agencies face both policy mandates and concrete milestones for PQC transition.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: Australia's ASD Information Security Manual (ISM) December 2024 update sets requirements for critical systems. Which PQC algorithms are mandated for new systems by 2030?
  A) ML-KEM-512 and ML-DSA-44 (NIST Level 1)
  B) ML-KEM-1024 and ML-DSA-87 (NIST Level 5)
  C) Only SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s for signatures; ML-KEM-768 for key exchange
  D) FrodoKEM-1344 and FN-DSA-1024 for maximum security
Answer: B
Explanation: Australia's ASD ISM December 2024 update mandates that new systems protecting sensitive information must use ML-KEM-1024 (key encapsulation) and ML-DSA-87 (digital signatures) — both at NIST Security Level 5 — by 2030. This is among the most conservative algorithm selections globally, requiring the highest security tier rather than Level 3. Existing systems have until 2030 for critical infrastructure and 2033 for all government systems, consistent with Five Eyes coordination.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: Which Australian government body published the December 2024 ISM update mandating PQC requirements, and what is its relationship to the Five Eyes alliance?
  A) ASIO (Australian Security Intelligence Organisation); independent from Five Eyes
  B) ASD (Australian Signals Directorate); coordinates PQC guidance with NSA, GCHQ, NCSC-CA, and GCSB through Five Eyes
  C) ACSC (Australian Cyber Security Centre); operates independently of intelligence agencies
  D) AUSTRAC (financial intelligence); focused solely on financial sector PQC
Answer: B
Explanation: The Australian Signals Directorate (ASD), through its Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) arm, published the ISM update. ASD is Australia's national signals intelligence and cybersecurity agency and is a full member of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance (US NSA, UK GCHQ, Canadian CCCS/CSE, New Zealand GCSB, Australian ASD). Five Eyes members coordinate on PQC timelines and algorithm selections, which is why Australia's ML-KEM-1024/ML-DSA-87 mandate, Canada's ITSM.40.001, and US CNSA 2.0 are closely aligned in scope and timeline.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: Which of the following statements about KpqC algorithm selection are correct? (Select all that apply)
  A) HAETAE is the primary signature algorithm — based on module lattices
  B) AIMer uses MPC-in-the-head (symmetric-key-based) design — different hardness from lattice
  C) SMAUG-T is the KEM winner — based on module lattices (MLWE/MLWR)
  D) NTRU+ replaces ML-KEM in all international contexts including TLS
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: Three statements are correct: (a) HAETAE is the KpqC signature winner, based on module lattices with distinct parameter choices from ML-DSA; (b) AIMer uses MPC-in-the-head design based on symmetric primitives — a mathematically distinct approach providing diversity; (c) SMAUG-T is the primary KpqC KEM, based on module learning with errors/rounding. Statement (d) is false: NTRU+ is a KpqC alternative KEM candidate, not a global replacement for ML-KEM. KpqC algorithms are mandated for Korean domestic systems, not international protocols.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance

Q: An organization operates in both South Korea and the United States, handling government contracts in both countries. What PQC deployment strategy is most appropriate for 2025–2030?
  A) Deploy only NIST FIPS 203/204/205 globally — Korean KpqC standards can be ignored since they are not yet finalized
  B) Deploy only KpqC algorithms in Korea and FIPS 203/204/205 in the US — using separate cryptographic infrastructure
  C) Deploy FIPS 203/204/205 as the primary PQC layer globally with crypto-agile architecture ready to add KpqC algorithms for Korean government systems when mandated in 2029
  D) Wait until 2028 when both standards are finalized before beginning any PQC transition
Answer: C
Explanation: The correct approach is crypto-agile deployment: implement FIPS 203/204/205 now (already standardized, required by US federal contracts) while building the infrastructure to layer in KpqC algorithms for Korean government systems when mandated (~2029). A crypto-agile architecture (abstract key negotiation, algorithm registries, CBOM tracking) makes this addition straightforward. Deploying entirely separate infrastructure per country is operationally costly. Waiting until 2028 violates US CNSA 2.0 requirements and misses the NIST-mandated 2030 deadline for many system types.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: Which hybrid key exchange combination is already deployed in major web browsers?
  A) RSA-4096 + ML-KEM-512
  B) X25519 + ML-KEM-768 (X25519MLKEM768)
  C) P-521 + FrodoKEM-1344
  D) DH-2048 + HQC-256
Answer: B
Explanation: X25519MLKEM768 combines classical X25519 (ECDH) with ML-KEM-768 (NIST Level 3) for hybrid key exchange. It is already deployed in Chrome, Firefox, and other major browsers for TLS 1.3 connections.

Q: Which RFC defines the use of multiple key exchanges in IKEv2 (IPsec)?
  A) RFC 8446
  B) RFC 9370
  C) RFC 9629
  D) RFC 8391
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9370 defines support for multiple key exchanges in IKEv2, enabling hybrid PQC key exchange (e.g., ML-KEM combined with classical ECDH) for IPsec VPN tunnels.

Q: Which RFC defines the use of KEMs in Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)?
  A) RFC 9370
  B) RFC 9629
  C) RFC 8391
  D) RFC 7748
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9629 defines the use of Key Encapsulation Mechanisms (KEMs) in CMS, enabling PQC key exchange for S/MIME email encryption and other CMS-based applications.

Q: How is PQC being integrated into 5G authentication?
  A) By replacing the entire 5G protocol stack
  B) Through PQC upgrades to 5G-AKA authentication and SUCI subscriber identity protection
  C) 5G already uses PQC by default
  D) PQC is not applicable to 5G networks
Answer: B
Explanation: 5G security relies on 3GPP authentication protocols (5G-AKA) and SUCI (Subscriber Concealed Identifier) for subscriber identity protection. These protocols use RSA/ECDSA today and are being upgraded to support PQC algorithms, particularly for protecting the subscriber concealment mechanism.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: True or False: The Signal messaging protocol has already adopted post-quantum cryptography.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Signal upgraded its protocol to include PQXDH (Post-Quantum Extended Diffie-Hellman) using CRYSTALS-Kyber (now standardized as ML-KEM) for quantum-resistant key agreement, making it one of the first major messaging platforms to adopt PQC.

Q: Which RFC defines the use of ML-DSA signatures in CMS for email and document signing?
  A) RFC 9629
  B) RFC 9882
  C) RFC 9370
  D) RFC 8032
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9882 defines the use of ML-DSA (Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm) in Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) for S/MIME email signing and other document signature applications.

Q: What is the primary challenge of integrating PQC into TLS compared to classical algorithms?
  A) PQC algorithms are too slow for real-time connections
  B) Larger key and ciphertext sizes increase handshake overhead
  C) TLS does not support algorithm negotiation
  D) PQC requires a completely new protocol version
Answer: B
Explanation: PQC algorithms generally have larger public keys, ciphertexts, and signatures than classical algorithms. For example, ML-KEM-768 public keys are ~1.2KB vs ~32 bytes for X25519. This increases TLS handshake size and can cause issues with network MTU limits and packet fragmentation.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: Which protocols have published RFCs or drafts for PQC integration? (Select all that apply)
  A) IKEv2 / IPsec (RFC 9370)
  B) CMS / S/MIME (RFC 9629, 9882)
  C) DNS (DNSSEC)
  D) OpenPGP
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: IKEv2 (RFC 9370), CMS/S/MIME (RFC 9629 for KEMs, RFC 9882 for ML-DSA), and OpenPGP all have published or draft specifications for PQC integration. DNSSEC PQC integration remains a significant challenge due to DNS packet size constraints.

Q: Which version of OpenSSL first included full PQC algorithm support (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA)?
  A) OpenSSL 1.1.1
  B) OpenSSL 3.0
  C) OpenSSL 3.5
  D) OpenSSL 4.0
Answer: C
Explanation: OpenSSL 3.5, released in April 2025, was the first version with full PQC support including ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, and the X25519MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange. Earlier versions required external providers (like the OQS provider) for PQC support.

Q: In the context of PQC protocol integration, what is a "composite" certificate?
  A) A certificate signed by multiple certificate authorities
  B) A certificate containing both classical and PQC public keys and signatures
  C) A certificate that expires and renews automatically
  D) A certificate used exclusively for testing
Answer: B
Explanation: A composite certificate contains both classical (e.g., RSA/ECDSA) and PQC (e.g., ML-DSA) public keys and signatures. This enables hybrid trust during the transition period — verifiers that support PQC use the quantum-safe signature, while legacy systems fall back to the classical one.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: What does SUCI protect in 5G networks?
  A) Base station locations
  B) Subscriber identity — preventing IMSI-catching attacks
  C) Network bandwidth
  D) Billing records
Answer: B
Explanation: SUCI (Subscriber Concealed Identifier) protects subscriber identity in 5G networks by encrypting the SUPI (Subscriber Permanent Identifier). This prevents IMSI-catching attacks where adversaries track users by intercepting their identity. SUCI currently uses ECIES with P-256.

Q: In TLS 1.3, which message carries the server's public key share during the handshake?
  A) ClientHello
  B) ServerHello
  C) Certificate
  D) EncryptedExtensions
Answer: B
Explanation: In TLS 1.3, the ServerHello message carries the server's key share (public key for key exchange). With PQC hybrid key exchange (X25519MLKEM768), this message is larger due to the ML-KEM-768 public key (~1.2KB).

Q: What is the GSMA Post-Quantum Telco Taskforce and when was it established?
  A) A 3GPP working group established in 2024
  B) An industry taskforce established September 2022 to guide telecom PQC migration
  C) A government mandate from the EU for telecom companies
  D) A quantum computing vendor consortium
Answer: B
Explanation: The GSMA Post-Quantum Telco Taskforce was established in September 2022 to guide the telecom industry's PQC migration. It published PQ.03 v2.0 (telecom use cases) in October 2024 and provides guidance for 5G/6G quantum-safe security.

Q: True or False: Chrome and Firefox have already shipped support for hybrid PQC key exchange (X25519+ML-KEM-768) in TLS.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Both Chrome and Firefox have shipped X25519MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange for TLS 1.3 connections. This is one of the earliest large-scale PQC deployments, protecting billions of web connections.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: Why is DNSSEC PQC integration particularly challenging compared to other protocols?
  A) DNS servers cannot be upgraded
  B) PQC signatures are too large for DNS UDP packet size limits (512 bytes / 4096 bytes with EDNS)
  C) DNS does not use cryptography
  D) DNSSEC is being deprecated in favor of DNS-over-HTTPS
Answer: B
Explanation: DNSSEC PQC integration faces severe packet size constraints. DNS traditionally uses UDP with 512-byte limits (4096 with EDNS). PQC signatures (ML-DSA: 2.4-4.6KB, SLH-DSA: 7.8-16KB) can exceed these limits, requiring protocol-level solutions like DNS over TCP or signature compression.

Q: What protocol does RFC 9370 add PQC support to?
  A) TLS 1.3
  B) IKEv2 (IPsec VPN)
  C) SSH
  D) SMTP
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9370 defines support for multiple key exchanges in IKEv2, the key management protocol for IPsec VPN tunnels. This enables hybrid PQC key exchange combining classical ECDH with ML-KEM for quantum-safe VPN connections.

Q: Which 5G authentication protocol is being upgraded for PQC resistance?
  A) GSM A5 cipher
  B) 5G-AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement)
  C) SMS-based OTP
  D) Wi-Fi WPA3
Answer: B
Explanation: 5G-AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement) is the primary authentication protocol in 5G networks (3GPP TS 33.501). It currently uses ECDSA/RSA and is being upgraded to support PQC algorithms for quantum-resistant network authentication.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: Which of the following 5G security components use quantum-vulnerable cryptography? (Select all that apply)
  A) SUCI subscriber identity protection (ECIES P-256)
  B) N2/N3 interface IPsec tunnels
  C) Physical cell tower hardware
  D) SIM/eSIM PKI authentication (RSA-2048/ECDSA P-256)
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: SUCI uses ECIES with P-256, N2/N3 interfaces use IPsec with quantum-vulnerable key exchange, and SIM/eSIM cards rely on RSA-2048/ECDSA P-256 PKI. Physical cell tower hardware itself is not a cryptographic component vulnerable to quantum attacks.

Q: What does OpenSSL 3.5 (released April 2025) include for PQC?
  A) Only ML-KEM support
  B) Full PQC support: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, and X25519MLKEM768 hybrid
  C) Only experimental PQC via external providers
  D) No PQC support — PQC requires OpenSSL 4.0
Answer: B
Explanation: OpenSSL 3.5 was a landmark release with full built-in PQC support: ML-KEM for key exchange, ML-DSA and SLH-DSA for signatures, and the X25519MLKEM768 hybrid key exchange as the default TLS negotiation option.

Q: What is the Samsung/Thales ML-KEM embedded secure element breakthrough from January 2026?
  A) A quantum computer chip for smartphones
  B) The first commercial PQC implementation for IoT embedded secure elements
  C) A new PQC algorithm proposal
  D) A quantum key distribution system for mobile devices
Answer: B
Explanation: Samsung and Thales achieved the first commercial PQC implementation using ML-KEM in an embedded secure element (January 2026). This breakthrough enables quantum-safe security for IoT devices, smart cards, and constrained environments.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: What does RFC 9882 standardize?
  A) ML-KEM key encapsulation in TLS 1.3
  B) ML-DSA signatures in the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)
  C) A new certificate format for PQC public keys
  D) SLH-DSA signatures for code signing
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9882 (published 2025) defines how to use ML-DSA (FIPS 204) digital signatures within CMS (Cryptographic Message Syntax, RFC 5652). CMS is the underlying structure for S/MIME email signing, document signing (PDF, XML), timestamping responses, and PKCS#7/PKCS#12 containers. RFC 9882 enables these widely-deployed applications to use NIST's standardized lattice signature scheme, complementing RFC 9629 which covers ML-KEM in CMS for key encapsulation.

Q: An organization needs to send PQC-signed S/MIME emails. Which RFC combination provides the complete CMS-based PQC signing and encryption capability?
  A) RFC 9882 (ML-DSA signing) + RFC 9629 (ML-KEM encryption) in CMS
  B) RFC 9794 (hybrid terminology) + RFC 9802 (HSS/XMSS in X.509)
  C) FIPS 203 alone is sufficient for both signing and encryption in S/MIME
  D) RFC 9814 (SLH-DSA in CMS) is the only approved S/MIME PQC option
Answer: A
Explanation: Full PQC S/MIME capability requires two RFCs: RFC 9882 (ML-DSA in CMS) for digital signatures and RFC 9629 (ML-KEM in CMS) for key encapsulation/encryption. RFC 9629 was published in 2024 and covers ML-KEM-512/768/1024 in CMS EnvelopedData. RFC 9882 covers ML-DSA-44/65/87 in CMS SignedData. Together they enable PQC-protected email that can be signed (integrity/authentication) and encrypted (confidentiality) using NIST-standardized algorithms within existing S/MIME infrastructure.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: What does RFC 9794 standardize for PQC hybrid cryptography?
  A) A new hybrid encryption algorithm combining ML-KEM and ML-DSA
  B) Terminology and naming conventions for post-quantum/traditional (PQ/T) hybrid cryptographic schemes
  C) A key derivation function for combining classical and PQC shared secrets
  D) Certificate extensions for indicating hybrid algorithm support
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9794 (published 2025) standardizes the terminology for hybrid cryptographic schemes that combine post-quantum (PQ) algorithms with traditional (T) classical algorithms. It defines terms like 'PQ/T hybrid scheme,' 'composite,' 'parallel,' and 'non-composite' combinations. This shared vocabulary is essential for interoperability: when RFCs, FIPS documents, and vendor documentation use the same terms consistently, organizations can correctly evaluate whether they have deployed the hybrid approach their security policy requires.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: According to RFC 9794 terminology, what is the difference between a 'composite' PQ/T hybrid scheme and a 'non-composite parallel' scheme?
  A) Composite schemes use a single algorithm object containing both PQ and classical components; non-composite parallel schemes keep them as separate independent objects
  B) Composite schemes are slower; non-composite parallel schemes are always faster
  C) Composite schemes are only for KEMs; non-composite parallel schemes are only for signatures
  D) RFC 9794 does not distinguish between composite and parallel — these terms are synonymous
Answer: A
Explanation: RFC 9794 defines: a 'composite' scheme is one where the PQ and classical algorithms are bundled into a single cryptographic object (one key, one ciphertext/signature) that combines both — neither component is independently meaningful for security. A 'non-composite parallel' scheme transmits both components independently (e.g., separate PQ and classical signatures in a TLS extension) but requires both to verify. X25519MLKEM768 (used in TLS 1.3 hybrid key exchange) is a non-composite parallel KEM: the classical X25519 shared secret and the ML-KEM-768 shared secret are combined via a KDF, but transmitted as separate values.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: What does RFC 9810 standardize for PQC key transport?
  A) ML-KEM in TLS 1.3 certificate-based key exchange
  B) Updates to CMP (Certificate Management Protocol) to support KEM-based key transport for certificate enrollment
  C) A new OID registry for all NIST PQC algorithms
  D) SLH-DSA signing of OCSP responses
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9810 (published 2025) updates the Certificate Management Protocol (CMP, RFC 4210) to support KEM-based key transport. CMP is widely used by enterprise PKI for automated certificate enrollment, renewal, and revocation (used by many CA software products). Before RFC 9810, CMP key transport assumed RSA or Diffie-Hellman; RFC 9810 adds support for ML-KEM to protect the key transport phase of certificate issuance — closing a critical gap in PQC-safe PKI automation.

Q: What does RFC 9814 standardize?
  A) ML-DSA signatures in TLS 1.3 certificate authentication
  B) SLH-DSA (FIPS 205 / SPHINCS+) signatures in the Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)
  C) HQC key encapsulation in CMS
  D) FN-DSA (FALCON) signatures in S/MIME
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 9814 (published 2025) defines how to use SLH-DSA (Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm, FIPS 205) within CMS. SLH-DSA is the hash-based alternative to lattice signatures: its security relies only on hash function properties, providing a maximally conservative option where lattice assumptions are unacceptable. RFC 9814 enables SLH-DSA in document signing, timestamping, and S/MIME contexts — complementing RFC 9882 (ML-DSA in CMS) and giving implementers algorithm choice within the same CMS framework.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: True or False: RFC 9794 hybrid terminology applies exclusively to KEMs — PQC signature schemes do not have 'composite' or 'parallel' hybrid variants.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. RFC 9794 terminology applies to both KEMs and signature schemes. Hybrid signature schemes can be 'composite' (a single combined signature object verifiable only as a unit) or 'non-composite parallel' (two independent signatures — e.g., both ML-DSA and RSA — transmitted together, each independently verifiable). Composite signature OIDs are being standardized through IETF LAMPS working group drafts. Non-composite parallel signatures appear in protocols like TLS where the PQ and classical signatures are sent in separate extensions.

chunks.title: Quiz: protocol-integration
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: protocol-integration

Q: A PKI engineer must design certificate enrollment for a new enterprise CA that will be PQC-safe. Which combination of RFCs covers the complete enrollment and signing stack?
  A) RFC 9882 (ML-DSA in CMS) + RFC 9794 (hybrid terminology)
  B) RFC 9810 (CMP KEM transport for enrollment) + RFC 9882 (ML-DSA in CMS for signed certificates) + RFC 9802 (HSS/XMSS in X.509 for long-lived CA keys)
  C) RFC 9629 (ML-KEM in CMS) + RFC 9814 (SLH-DSA in CMS) alone are sufficient
  D) Only FIPS 203/204/205 are required — no IETF RFCs are needed
Answer: B
Explanation: A complete PQC-safe PKI enrollment stack requires: RFC 9810 for CMP-based automated certificate enrollment using ML-KEM key transport (replacing RSA key transport); RFC 9882 for ML-DSA signatures on issued end-entity certificates and CA certificates; RFC 9802 for long-lived CA root/intermediate keys using HSS or XMSS (stateful hash-based, recommended by CNSA 2.0 for long-lived CA keys). FIPS documents define the algorithms but not the protocol integration — the RFCs are needed to specify interoperable use in PKI protocols. RFC 9794 terminology is useful for documentation but not a protocol specification.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: Which industry is most vulnerable to HNDL attacks due to lifetime data sensitivity (e.g., genomic data)?
  A) Retail
  B) Healthcare
  C) Entertainment
  D) Hospitality
Answer: B
Explanation: Healthcare data — especially genomic data, mental health records, and patient histories — has lifetime sensitivity. This makes healthcare particularly vulnerable to HNDL attacks, where encrypted data intercepted today could be decrypted decades later.

Q: Approximately how many EMV payment cards worldwide use RSA-based offline authentication?
  A) ~500 million
  B) ~2 billion
  C) ~14.7 billion
  D) ~50 billion
Answer: C
Explanation: Approximately 14.7 billion EMV cards worldwide use RSA-based offline authentication (RSA-1024 or RSA-2048). Migrating this massive installed base to PQC represents one of the largest cryptographic transitions in history.

Q: Why are aviation and aerospace systems particularly challenging for PQC migration?
  A) They use quantum computers already
  B) They have 30-40 year service lives with limited crypto-agility
  C) They do not use any cryptography currently
  D) International aviation law prohibits cryptographic changes
Answer: B
Explanation: Aircraft and avionics systems have extremely long service lives (30-40 years) and are governed by strict certification standards (RTCA DO-326A). Updating cryptographic systems in certified avionics requires extensive re-certification, making rapid migration very difficult.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: True or False: Bitcoin is vulnerable to quantum attacks because it uses ECDSA (secp256k1) for transaction signing.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Bitcoin uses ECDSA with the secp256k1 curve for transaction signing. Shor's Algorithm can break ECDSA, potentially allowing quantum attackers to forge transactions. Approximately $718 billion in quantum-vulnerable P2PK addresses exist, including ~1.1 million BTC attributed to Satoshi Nakamoto.

Q: What unique quantum threat does blockchain immutability create?
  A) Blockchains can be deleted by quantum computers
  B) On-chain encrypted data is permanently exposed — it cannot be re-encrypted after the fact
  C) Quantum computers can reverse blockchain consensus
  D) Mining becomes impossible with quantum hardware
Answer: B
Explanation: Blockchain immutability means encrypted data stored on-chain remains permanently accessible. Unlike centralized systems where archived data can be re-encrypted, blockchain data cannot be modified. Any HNDL-harvested on-chain encrypted data remains vulnerable indefinitely.

Q: Which critical infrastructure sector faces unique challenges due to 20-30 year equipment lifecycles and IEC 62443 standards?
  A) Retail
  B) Energy and utilities (SCADA systems)
  C) Media and entertainment
  D) Education
Answer: B
Explanation: Energy and utility systems (power grids, SCADA, nuclear facilities) have equipment lifecycles of 20-30 years governed by IEC 62443 standards. Nuclear plants are licensed for 60+ years (with 80-year extensions). These extremely long lifecycles make PQC migration particularly complex.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: Why did the Ethereum Foundation establish a dedicated PQC team with $2M in research prizes in January 2026?
  A) To develop a quantum computer
  B) To research quantum-safe upgrades for Ethereum, as its secp256k1/BLS12-381 cryptography is vulnerable
  C) To create a competing PQC standard
  D) To build quantum mining hardware
Answer: B
Explanation: Ethereum uses secp256k1 ECDSA and BLS12-381 cryptography, both vulnerable to quantum attacks. The Foundation established a dedicated PQC team to research quantum-safe account abstraction, signature scheme upgrades, and consensus mechanism protections.

Q: Which automotive systems are vulnerable to quantum attacks? (Select all that apply)
  A) V2X (vehicle-to-everything) PKI communications
  B) OTA (over-the-air) firmware update code signing
  C) Windshield wiper controls
  D) In-vehicle network ECU authentication
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: V2X communications use ECDSA P-256 certificates (IEEE 1609.2), OTA firmware updates use RSA/ECDSA code signing, and ECU authentication uses cryptographic protocols — all quantum-vulnerable. Windshield wipers do not involve cryptographic operations.

Q: According to Thales 2025 data, what percentage of healthcare operators have encrypted 80% or more of their cloud data?
  A) 4%
  B) 25%
  C) 57%
  D) 75%
Answer: A
Explanation: Only 4% of healthcare operators have encrypted 80% or more of their cloud data (Thales 2025). This is alarming given that 677 healthcare breaches in 2024 exposed over 182 million individuals and healthcare data has lifetime sensitivity.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: How many healthcare breaches occurred in 2024, and how many individuals were affected?
  A) 200 breaches, 50 million individuals
  B) 677 breaches, 182+ million individuals
  C) 1000 breaches, 500 million individuals
  D) 50 breaches, 10 million individuals
Answer: B
Explanation: 677 healthcare breaches in 2024 exposed over 182 million individuals. Combined with only 4% of healthcare operators encrypting most cloud data, this represents a critical quantum vulnerability given the lifetime sensitivity of genomic and medical data.

Q: What percentage of global trade by volume is carried by maritime shipping, making maritime cryptography a critical concern?
  A) 30%
  B) 50%
  C) 80%
  D) 95%
Answer: C
Explanation: Maritime shipping carries approximately 80% of world trade by volume. Maritime systems use AIS (Automatic Identification System) and digital signatures (RSA/ECDSA) for vessel identification and electronic bills of lading, making them vulnerable to quantum-enabled forgery.

Q: What is the BIS Project Leap Phase 2 testing for quantum-safe financial infrastructure?
  A) Quantum computing for trading algorithms
  B) Hybrid PQC on the TARGET2 settlement system processing trillions daily
  C) Quantum key distribution for ATM networks
  D) Blockchain-based central bank digital currencies
Answer: B
Explanation: BIS Project Leap Phase 2 (July 2025) tests hybrid PQC implementations on the TARGET2 settlement system, which processes trillions of euros in daily interbank settlements. This is the first large-scale test of quantum-safe financial market infrastructure.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: True or False: Nuclear power plants have licensing periods of 60+ years with approved 80-year extensions.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Nuclear plants operate under NRC 10 CFR 73.54 with 60+ year licenses and approved 80-year extensions. These extremely long lifecycles make nuclear facilities some of the most challenging systems for PQC migration, as cryptographic systems must remain secure for decades.

Q: How many SIM cards globally use RSA-2048 or ECDSA P-256, creating a massive quantum-vulnerable surface?
  A) Millions
  B) Hundreds of millions
  C) Billions
  D) Tens of thousands
Answer: C
Explanation: Billions of SIM cards globally use RSA-2048 or ECDSA P-256 for authentication. The eSIM standard (SGP.22) also relies on quantum-vulnerable PKI. Replacing this massive installed base is one of the largest PQC migration challenges in the telecom industry.

Q: What does FS-ISAC warn about the financial sector's PQC readiness?
  A) The financial sector is well ahead of schedule
  B) Financial sector is unprepared and risks roadmap collapse by 2030 (September 2025 warning)
  C) PQC migration is unnecessary for financial institutions
  D) Only cryptocurrency firms are at risk
Answer: B
Explanation: FS-ISAC (Financial Services Information Sharing and Analysis Center) issued a September 2025 warning that the financial sector is unprepared for quantum threats and risks roadmap collapse by 2030 unless immediate action is taken.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: What is the quantum threat to global streaming DRM systems (Widevine/PlayReady/FairPlay)?
  A) No threat — streaming is not encrypted
  B) RSA key hierarchies protecting content could be broken, exposing >$100B annual streaming revenue
  C) Only live streaming is affected
  D) DRM will be replaced by blockchain before quantum computers arrive
Answer: B
Explanation: Widevine, PlayReady, and FairPlay DRM systems rely on RSA key hierarchies. Quantum computers could break these hierarchies, potentially exposing the entire global streaming content catalog worth over $100 billion in annual revenue.

Q: Which of the following Ethereum cryptographic components are quantum-vulnerable? (Select all that apply)
  A) secp256k1 ECDSA (transaction signing)
  B) BLS12-381 (consensus signatures)
  C) SHA-256 (block hashing)
  D) Keccak-256 (address derivation)
Answer: A|B
Explanation: Ethereum's secp256k1 ECDSA (transaction signing) and BLS12-381 (consensus/beacon chain signatures) are both vulnerable to Shor's Algorithm. SHA-256 and Keccak-256 are hash functions only weakened (not broken) by Grover's Algorithm.

Q: What is the estimated value of quantum-vulnerable Bitcoin in P2PK addresses, according to blockchain analysis?
  A) ~$10 million
  B) ~$100 million
  C) ~$718 billion
  D) ~$5 trillion
Answer: C
Explanation: Approximately $718 billion in quantum-vulnerable P2PK (Pay-to-Public-Key) addresses exist on Bitcoin, including approximately 1.1 million BTC attributed to Satoshi Nakamoto. These addresses permanently expose their public keys, making them prime targets for future quantum attacks.

chunks.title: Quiz: industry-threats
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: industry-threats

Q: According to Forescout 2025, by how much have average device risk scores increased across the top 10 countries year-over-year?
  A) 5%
  B) 15%
  C) 33%
  D) 50%
Answer: C
Explanation: Forescout 2025 reports average device risk scores across the top 10 countries increased 33% year-over-year (from 6.53 to 9.1), with overall device risk up 15%. Billions of IoT devices use ECDSA P-256 or RSA-2048 with no practical firmware update path, making IoT one of the most challenging sectors for PQC migration.

Q: What unique quantum threat do electronic bills of lading (eBL) in maritime shipping face?
  A) Data theft during transit
  B) Quantum forgery could enable cargo theft by forging digital proof of ownership
  C) Physical cargo damage
  D) Insurance fraud unrelated to cryptography
Answer: B
Explanation: DCSA electronic bills of lading (eBL) use digital signatures to prove cargo ownership. Quantum forgery of these signatures could enable cargo theft at a global scale, as forged eBLs would appear legitimate to all parties in the supply chain.

chunks.title: Quiz: crypto-operations
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: crypto-operations

Q: What is a Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM)?
  A) A method for digitally signing documents
  B) A mechanism for securely establishing a shared secret key between two parties
  C) A technique for compressing encryption keys
  D) A hardware device for storing private keys
Answer: B
Explanation: A KEM securely establishes a shared secret key between two parties. Unlike Diffie-Hellman key exchange, a KEM works by having one party generate a shared secret and encapsulate it using the other party's public key. The recipient then decapsulates to recover the shared secret.

Q: What is the approximate public key size of ML-KEM-768 compared to X25519?
  A) About the same (~32 bytes each)
  B) ML-KEM-768 is ~37x larger (~1184 bytes vs ~32 bytes)
  C) ML-KEM-768 is ~1000x larger (~32KB vs ~32 bytes)
  D) X25519 is actually larger than ML-KEM-768
Answer: B
Explanation: ML-KEM-768 public keys are approximately 1184 bytes, compared to 32 bytes for X25519. While significantly larger, this is still manageable for most applications. The trade-off is worthwhile for quantum resistance, and ML-KEM-768 is still very fast (~80x faster keygen than RSA-2048).

Q: What are the three core operations of a KEM?
  A) Encrypt, Decrypt, Verify
  B) KeyGen, Encapsulate, Decapsulate
  C) Sign, Verify, Exchange
  D) Generate, Distribute, Revoke
Answer: B
Explanation: The three operations of a KEM are: KeyGen (generate a public/private key pair), Encapsulate (use the public key to produce a ciphertext and shared secret), and Decapsulate (use the private key to recover the shared secret from the ciphertext).

chunks.title: Quiz: crypto-operations
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: crypto-operations

Q: True or False: ML-KEM key generation is approximately 80x faster than RSA-2048 key generation.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. ML-KEM key generation is dramatically faster than RSA-2048 because it uses simple linear algebra operations over polynomial rings, rather than RSA's computationally expensive prime number generation.

Q: How does ML-DSA signature size compare to RSA-2048 signature size?
  A) ML-DSA-65 signatures are smaller than RSA-2048 signatures
  B) They are approximately the same size (~256 bytes)
  C) ML-DSA-65 signatures are larger (~3.3KB vs ~256 bytes for RSA-2048)
  D) RSA-2048 does not produce signatures
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-DSA-65 produces signatures of approximately 3,309 bytes compared to RSA-2048's ~256 bytes. This larger size is a key trade-off for quantum resistance and impacts applications with bandwidth constraints, such as TLS handshakes and certificate chains.

Q: Why does FN-DSA require careful implementation to resist side-channel attacks?
  A) It uses extremely large key sizes
  B) Its signing algorithm relies on floating-point arithmetic that can leak timing information
  C) It requires internet connectivity during signing
  D) It stores private keys in plaintext
Answer: B
Explanation: FN-DSA (formerly Falcon) uses fast Fourier transforms over NTRU lattices, which require floating-point arithmetic. Variations in floating-point computation timing can leak information about the private key through side-channel attacks, requiring constant-time implementation techniques.

chunks.title: Quiz: crypto-operations
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: crypto-operations

Q: For multi-select questions about correctness: in ML-KEM, what does the encapsulation operation produce?
  A) A digital signature and verification key
  B) A ciphertext and a shared secret
  C) An encrypted message and decryption key
  D) A hash digest and salt
Answer: B
Explanation: ML-KEM encapsulation takes a public key and produces two outputs: a ciphertext (sent to the recipient) and a shared secret (kept by the sender). The recipient uses their private key to decapsulate the ciphertext and recover the same shared secret.

Q: Which of the following are valid hybrid key exchange combinations deployed or specified in standards? (Select all that apply)
  A) X25519MLKEM768
  B) SecP256r1MLKEM768
  C) SecP384r1MLKEM1024
  D) RSA2048MLKEM512
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: X25519MLKEM768, SecP256r1MLKEM768, and SecP384r1MLKEM1024 are all standardized or deployed hybrid key exchange combinations. RSA-based hybrid KEMs are not standard — hybrid approaches pair ECDH variants with ML-KEM variants.

Q: What is the fundamental difference between a KEM and a digital signature algorithm?
  A) KEMs are faster; signatures are slower
  B) KEMs establish shared secrets for encryption; signatures prove authenticity and integrity
  C) KEMs are quantum-safe; signatures are not
  D) There is no difference — they are interchangeable
Answer: B
