Explanation: HNDL attacks, signature forgery, and TLS handshake attacks are all valid quantum threat vectors involving public-key cryptography. Brute-forcing AES-256 is not practical even with quantum computers (Grover's only halves the effective key length to 128 bits).

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-risk-management
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-risk-management

Q: What is the HNDL exposure window?
  A) The time between algorithm selection and NIST standardization
  B) The period during which intercepted data remains vulnerable before quantum decryption becomes possible
  C) The window for applying security patches after a vulnerability disclosure
  D) The compliance deadline minus the current date
Answer: B
Explanation: The HNDL exposure window is the gap between when data is intercepted and when a CRQC can decrypt it. If your data must remain confidential for 15 years and a CRQC arrives in 10 years, your HNDL window has already opened — meaning data intercepted today is at risk.

Q: What is the recommended first step in a PQC risk management program?
  A) Deploy PQC algorithms to production immediately
  B) Conduct a cryptographic inventory of all systems and algorithms
  C) Wait for all vendors to announce PQC support
  D) Hire a quantum computing team
Answer: B
Explanation: A cryptographic inventory is the essential first step — you cannot assess or mitigate risks you haven't identified. This includes cataloging all cryptographic algorithms, key lengths, certificate lifecycles, and protocol dependencies across the organization.

Q: True or False: A risk heatmap for PQC should only consider technical impact and ignore business and regulatory consequences.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. A comprehensive PQC risk heatmap must consider multiple impact dimensions: technical (system compromise), business (revenue loss, reputational damage), regulatory (compliance penalties, audit failures), and operational (service disruption, recovery costs).

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-risk-management
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-risk-management

Q: When estimating CRQC timeline scenarios for risk planning, what is the generally recommended approach?
  A) Use only the most optimistic estimate to create urgency
  B) Use only the most pessimistic estimate to avoid unnecessary spending
  C) Model multiple scenarios (optimistic, moderate, pessimistic) and plan for the moderate case
  D) Ignore timeline estimates and focus only on compliance deadlines
Answer: C
Explanation: Best practice is scenario-based planning with multiple CRQC timelines. An optimistic case (e.g., 2030) reveals urgent risks, a moderate case (e.g., 2035) guides primary planning, and a pessimistic case (e.g., 2045) provides a lower bound. This approach balances urgency with resource allocation.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-business-case
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-business-case

Q: What is the primary financial justification for PQC migration?
  A) Reducing current IT headcount
  B) Avoiding catastrophic future breach costs and compliance penalties
  C) Eliminating the need for encryption entirely
  D) Reducing cloud computing costs
Answer: B
Explanation: The core business case for PQC migration centers on risk avoidance: preventing potentially devastating quantum-enabled breaches and associated costs (data loss, regulatory fines, reputational damage) that would far exceed the migration investment.

Q: Which cost category typically represents the largest portion of a PQC migration budget?
  A) Hardware upgrades for quantum computers
  B) Software and infrastructure updates across the technology stack
  C) Regulatory filing fees
  D) Training materials and documentation
Answer: B
Explanation: Software and infrastructure updates — including library upgrades, protocol changes, certificate system modifications, and testing — represent the bulk of migration costs. PQC runs on classical hardware, so quantum computer purchases are not needed.

Q: True or False: A quantum-enabled data breach is expected to cost significantly more than a classical breach of similar scope.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Quantum-enabled breaches can decrypt years of previously intercepted data simultaneously (HNDL), expose far more records, face heightened regulatory scrutiny, and cause greater reputational damage — all multiplying costs well beyond classical breach scenarios.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-business-case
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-business-case

Q: What metric best demonstrates PQC migration ROI to a board of directors?
  A) Number of algorithms replaced
  B) Risk-adjusted cost avoidance compared to migration investment
  C) Number of FIPS certifications obtained
  D) Lines of code changed
Answer: B
Explanation: Boards understand risk-adjusted financial metrics. Presenting the total migration investment against the expected cost of a quantum-enabled breach (weighted by probability) provides a clear ROI that resonates with business decision-makers.

Q: Which of the following should be included in a PQC business case cost model? (Select all that apply)
  A) Cryptographic library and infrastructure upgrade costs
  B) Staff training and skill development
  C) Cost of quantum computer procurement
  D) Compliance penalty avoidance estimates
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: A complete PQC business case includes infrastructure upgrade costs, training investments, and compliance penalty avoidance. Quantum computer procurement is NOT a cost — PQC algorithms run on classical hardware. This is a common misconception to address with executives.

Q: What does TCO stand for in PQC migration planning?
  A) Total Crypto Operations
  B) Total Cost of Ownership
  C) Technical Change Order
  D) Transition Compliance Objective
Answer: B
Explanation: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) encompasses all direct and indirect costs of PQC migration over its full lifecycle: initial implementation, testing, deployment, training, ongoing maintenance, and operational overhead changes.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-business-case
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-business-case

Q: When presenting a PQC business case to the board, which communication approach is most effective?
  A) Lead with detailed algorithm specifications and NIST standard numbers
  B) Frame the discussion around business risk, financial impact, and competitive positioning
  C) Present only the technical architecture changes needed
  D) Focus exclusively on compliance deadlines without mentioning costs
Answer: B
Explanation: Board members respond to business framing: risk quantification, financial impact modeling, competitive implications, and strategic positioning. Technical details should be available as backup but shouldn't lead the narrative. Connect PQC to outcomes the board already cares about.

Q: True or False: PQC migration can be positioned as a competitive advantage, not just a cost center.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Early PQC adoption can differentiate an organization: winning contracts requiring quantum-ready security, building customer trust, meeting compliance ahead of deadlines, and reducing future emergency migration costs that competitors will face.

Q: What is the payback period concept in PQC migration ROI calculations?
  A) The time until quantum computers become commercially available
  B) The time until the migration investment is recovered through avoided costs and benefits
  C) The NIST standardization timeline
  D) The time between starting and completing the migration
Answer: B
Explanation: Payback period measures how long until cumulative benefits (breach cost avoidance, compliance penalty avoidance, operational savings, competitive wins) exceed the total migration investment. Shorter payback periods indicate stronger business cases.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-business-case
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-business-case

Q: Which factor most increases the urgency of the PQC business case for a specific organization?
  A) Having a large IT budget
  B) Operating in a heavily regulated industry with long data retention requirements
  C) Using only open-source software
  D) Having completed a recent IT modernization
Answer: B
Explanation: Heavily regulated industries (finance, healthcare, government) with long data retention face the highest urgency: their data is most valuable to adversaries, regulatory penalties for breaches are severe, and long retention periods maximize HNDL exposure.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-governance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-governance

Q: What does RACI stand for in governance frameworks?
  A) Risk Assessment and Compliance Index
  B) Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed
  C) Regulatory Audit and Certification Inventory
  D) Resource Allocation and Control Integration
Answer: B
Explanation: RACI is a responsibility assignment matrix: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome), Consulted (provides input before decisions), Informed (kept updated after decisions). It ensures clear ownership across PQC migration activities.

Q: In a PQC RACI matrix, who should typically be Accountable for the overall migration program?
  A) The development team lead
  B) The CISO or CTO
  C) The compliance officer
  D) The procurement manager
Answer: B
Explanation: The CISO or CTO should be Accountable for the overall PQC migration because it spans security strategy, technology architecture, and organizational change. While many roles are Responsible for specific tasks, single accountability at the C-level ensures executive ownership and resource allocation.

Q: True or False: A PQC cryptographic policy should specify which algorithms are approved and which are prohibited.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. An effective PQC cryptographic policy must explicitly list approved PQC algorithms (e.g., ML-KEM-768, ML-DSA-65) and prohibited classical algorithms (e.g., RSA < 3072 bits, ECDSA without hybrid mode), along with transition timelines and exception processes.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-governance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-governance

Q: What is the primary benefit of centralized PQC governance compared to a distributed approach?
  A) It eliminates the need for technical expertise in business units
  B) It ensures consistent standards, policies, and timelines across the organization
  C) It reduces the total cost of migration by half
  D) It removes the need for a cryptographic inventory
Answer: B
Explanation: Centralized governance ensures consistency: unified algorithm policies, coordinated timelines, standard testing procedures, and consolidated vendor management. Distributed governance risks fragmented approaches, incompatible implementations, and gaps in coverage.

Q: Which elements should a PQC governance policy include? (Select all that apply)
  A) Approved and prohibited algorithm lists
  B) Exception and waiver processes
  C) Quantum computer purchase specifications
  D) Migration timeline with milestones
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: A PQC governance policy should include approved/prohibited algorithms, exception processes for systems that cannot immediately migrate, and a migration timeline. Quantum computer purchases are irrelevant — PQC runs on classical hardware.

Q: What governance model best supports PQC migration in a large enterprise?
  A) Fully centralized with no local autonomy
  B) Fully distributed with no central coordination
  C) Federated model with central policies and decentralized execution
  D) Outsourced entirely to a managed security provider
Answer: C
Explanation: A federated governance model provides the best balance: central teams set policies, standards, and timelines while business units and development teams execute within their domains. This respects organizational complexity while maintaining coherent standards.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-governance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-governance

Q: How often should PQC governance KPIs be reported to executive leadership?
  A) Only when migration is complete
  B) Annually at budget planning
  C) Monthly or quarterly with dashboard-style metrics
  D) Only when problems arise
Answer: C
Explanation: Regular monthly or quarterly KPI reporting keeps leadership informed of migration progress, surfaces blockers early, and maintains executive attention. Dashboard-style reporting with key metrics (% migrated, compliance gaps, vendor readiness, budget utilization) is most effective.

Q: True or False: PQC governance should include a formal exception process for systems that cannot immediately migrate to PQC algorithms.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Not all systems can migrate immediately — legacy systems, vendor dependencies, and contractual constraints create legitimate exceptions. A formal process ensures exceptions are documented, risk-accepted by appropriate authority, time-limited, and tracked for eventual resolution.

Q: Which KPI is most critical for tracking PQC governance effectiveness?
  A) Number of meetings held by the governance committee
  B) Percentage of cryptographic assets migrated to PQC-approved algorithms
  C) Total governance budget spent
  D) Number of policy documents published
Answer: B
Explanation: The percentage of cryptographic assets migrated to PQC-approved algorithms is the most meaningful outcome KPI — it directly measures progress toward the governance objective. Process metrics (meetings, documents, budget) are useful but don't capture actual migration progress.

chunks.title: Quiz: pqc-governance
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: pqc-governance

Q: What is the purpose of a cryptographic policy in PQC governance?
  A) To list all employees who work on encryption
  B) To define which cryptographic algorithms and practices are approved for use
  C) To document the organization's quantum computing research
  D) To track vendor pricing for security products
Answer: B
Explanation: A cryptographic policy establishes the organization's approved algorithms, key lengths, protocols, and practices. In the PQC context, it defines the transition path from classical to quantum-resistant cryptography and sets compliance expectations.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance-strategy
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance-strategy

Q: Which US government directive requires transition to PQC algorithms for national security systems?
  A) GDPR Article 32
  B) CNSA 2.0 (Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite)
  C) PCI DSS 4.0
  D) SOC 2 Type II
Answer: B
Explanation: CNSA 2.0, published by NSA, mandates the transition to quantum-resistant algorithms for all National Security Systems (NSS). It sets specific timelines: software/firmware using ML-KEM by 2025 and full transition to PQC by 2033.

Q: What is the primary challenge of multi-jurisdiction PQC compliance?
  A) Different countries use different programming languages
  B) Varying timelines, approved algorithms, and requirements across jurisdictions
  C) Only one country has PQC regulations
  D) All jurisdictions have identical requirements
Answer: B
Explanation: Multi-jurisdiction compliance is challenging because different countries and frameworks have varying timelines (CNSA 2.0 vs ANSSI vs BSI), different approved algorithm lists, and sometimes conflicting requirements. Organizations operating globally must navigate these overlaps and gaps.

Q: True or False: Organizations in the financial sector face PQC compliance pressure from multiple overlapping frameworks.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Financial institutions face PQC requirements from CNSA 2.0 (for government-adjacent operations), PCI DSS (payment security), SOX (financial reporting), industry-specific regulators (OCC, ECB), and potentially DORA (EU). Managing these overlapping requirements requires a coordinated compliance strategy.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance-strategy
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance-strategy

Q: What is a compliance-first approach to PQC migration?
  A) Ignoring compliance and focusing only on technical security
  B) Prioritizing migration activities based on regulatory deadlines and framework requirements
  C) Waiting until regulators issue fines before taking action
  D) Delegating all compliance work to external auditors
Answer: B
Explanation: A compliance-first approach uses regulatory deadlines as the primary driver for migration prioritization. Systems subject to the earliest or most stringent requirements are migrated first. This contrasts with a risk-first approach that prioritizes based on threat exposure.

Q: Which elements are essential for PQC audit readiness? (Select all that apply)
  A) Complete cryptographic inventory documentation
  B) Evidence of algorithm migration testing
  C) Quantum computer performance benchmarks
  D) Documented exception and waiver processes
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: Audit readiness requires documented proof of progress: a comprehensive cryptographic inventory, evidence that PQC algorithms have been tested and validated, and documented exception processes for systems not yet migrated. Quantum computer benchmarks are irrelevant to PQC compliance.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance-strategy
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance-strategy

Q: How do CNSA 2.0 and ANSSI recommendations differ in their approach to PQC transition?
  A) CNSA 2.0 requires PQC only; ANSSI requires hybrid (classical + PQC) during transition
  B) They are identical in all requirements
  C) ANSSI does not address PQC at all
  D) CNSA 2.0 applies only to civilian systems
Answer: A
Explanation: CNSA 2.0 (NSA) mandates direct transition to PQC algorithms for national security systems. ANSSI (France) recommends a phased hybrid approach where classical and PQC algorithms are combined during the transition period. This difference can create compliance challenges for multinational organizations.

Q: What is a compliance gap analysis in PQC migration?
  A) Measuring the speed difference between classical and PQC algorithms
  B) Identifying where current cryptographic practices fall short of regulatory requirements
  C) Counting the number of compliance officers in the organization
  D) Comparing vendor prices for compliance tools
Answer: B
Explanation: A compliance gap analysis maps current cryptographic practices against each applicable framework's requirements, identifying where the organization falls short. This drives prioritization: the largest gaps in the most critical frameworks should be addressed first.

Q: True or False: FIPS 140-3 validation is a binary requirement — a product either has it or doesn't.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. FIPS 140-3 validation exists on a spectrum: full CMVP validation (gold standard), modules in process (submitted but not yet validated), FIPS-mode operation (uses validated algorithms but module not separately validated), and partial compliance (FedRAMP, WebTrust). Different compliance frameworks accept different levels.

chunks.title: Quiz: compliance-strategy
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: compliance-strategy

Q: When building a PQC compliance timeline, what is the most critical risk to identify?
  A) Budget overruns in the compliance department
  B) Framework deadlines that fall before projected migration completion dates
  C) The number of compliance consultants hired
  D) Whether competitors have started their migration
Answer: B
Explanation: The most critical risk is a timing gap: when a compliance deadline arrives before migration is complete, creating regulatory exposure. A compliance timeline must overlay framework deadlines with realistic migration milestones to identify and address these gaps proactively.

Q: What role does evidence collection play in PQC compliance?
  A) It is only needed if the organization is audited
  B) It provides documented proof of compliance activities for regulators and auditors
  C) It replaces the need for actual PQC migration
  D) It is only relevant for government organizations
Answer: B
Explanation: Evidence collection — test results, migration logs, policy documents, inventory records — must be ongoing and systematic. Regulators and auditors require documented proof that the organization is actively and effectively transitioning to PQC, not just planning to do so.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-program
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-program

Q: How many phases does the NIST IR 8547 PQC migration framework define?
  A) 3 phases
  B) 5 phases
  C) 7 phases
  D) 10 phases
Answer: C
Explanation: NIST IR 8547 defines a 7-phase migration framework: Discovery, Inventory, Prioritization, Planning, Pilot/Testing, Deployment/Migration, and Validation/Monitoring. Each phase has specific activities, deliverables, and success criteria.

Q: What is the primary risk of skipping the pilot/testing phase in PQC migration?
  A) Increased vendor costs
  B) Performance regressions and interoperability failures in production
  C) Delayed compliance reporting
  D) Higher training costs
Answer: B
Explanation: PQC algorithms have different performance characteristics (larger keys, different latency profiles) than classical algorithms. Skipping piloting can lead to production failures: broken TLS handshakes, certificate chain issues, performance degradation, and interoperability problems with partners.

Q: True or False: PQC migration is primarily a technology project that doesn't require organizational change management.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. PQC migration requires significant organizational change: cross-functional coordination, updated policies, new skills, vendor negotiations, stakeholder communication, and budget allocation. Technical implementation is only one dimension of a successful migration program.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-program
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-program

Q: What is the critical path in a PQC migration program?
  A) The sequence of activities that determines the minimum migration timeline
  B) The most expensive activities in the budget
  C) The path data takes through the network
  D) The approval chain for policy documents
Answer: A
Explanation: The critical path is the longest sequence of dependent activities that determines the earliest possible completion date. In PQC migration, it often runs through: vendor readiness → library updates → application integration → testing → deployment. Delays on the critical path directly delay the program.

Q: Which stakeholders should receive regular PQC migration status updates? (Select all that apply)
  A) Board of Directors / Executive Committee
  B) Technical development teams
  C) External regulatory bodies
  D) Vendor and partner organizations
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: Internal stakeholders (board, technical teams) and external partners (vendors) need regular updates at appropriate levels of detail. Regulatory bodies typically receive updates during formal audits or scheduled reporting periods, not routine status updates.

Q: What is a migration roadmap in PQC program management?
  A) A geographic map of data center locations
  B) A timeline showing milestones, dependencies, and deadlines for the PQC transition
  C) A list of all quantum computing research papers
  D) A vendor product catalog
Answer: B
Explanation: A migration roadmap is a visual timeline showing key milestones, dependencies, resource needs, and regulatory deadlines. It helps stakeholders understand the overall plan, identify conflicts, and track progress against both internal goals and external deadlines.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-program
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-program

Q: What is the recommended approach for resource estimation in PQC migration programs?
  A) Estimate once at the beginning and never revise
  B) Use phased estimation with increasing precision as the program progresses
  C) Only estimate costs for the final deployment phase
  D) Base estimates solely on vendor quotes
Answer: B
Explanation: Phased estimation (rolling-wave planning) starts with rough order-of-magnitude estimates for the full program and refines them as each phase completes and more is known. Early phases like discovery and inventory inform more accurate estimates for later phases like deployment.

Q: True or False: A phased PQC migration approach (prioritizing high-risk systems first) is generally preferred over a big-bang migration.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Phased migration reduces risk by allowing the organization to learn from early deployments, catch integration issues before they affect critical systems, and demonstrate value incrementally. Big-bang approaches concentrate risk and provide no opportunity for course correction.

Q: What is the biggest risk to PQC migration program timelines?
  A) Insufficient documentation
  B) Vendor dependency — waiting for third-party software and hardware to support PQC
  C) Too many project meetings
  D) Excessive testing
Answer: B
Explanation: Vendor dependency is the most common schedule risk: organizations cannot migrate systems until their vendors provide PQC-compatible updates. This is especially impactful for COTS (commercial off-the-shelf) products, HSMs, and cloud services. Early vendor engagement and contractual requirements are critical mitigations.

chunks.title: Quiz: migration-program
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: migration-program

Q: What communication approach works best for different stakeholder groups in PQC migration?
  A) The same technical briefing for all audiences
  B) Tailored messages: business impact for executives, technical details for engineers, timeline impacts for partners
  C) Only communicate when milestones are completed
  D) Communicate exclusively through email
Answer: B
Explanation: Effective stakeholder communication requires tailored messaging: executives need business risk and financial framing, technical teams need architecture and implementation details, and partners need timeline and interoperability information. One-size-fits-all communication fails to engage any audience effectively.

chunks.title: Quiz: vendor-risk
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: vendor-risk

Q: What is a vendor PQC readiness scorecard?
  A) A quantum computing benchmark test for vendor hardware
  B) A structured assessment of a vendor's preparedness for PQC transition
  C) A vendor's financial credit rating
  D) A scorecard for quantum algorithm performance
Answer: B
Explanation: A vendor PQC readiness scorecard evaluates key dimensions: PQC algorithm support, FIPS validation status, published roadmap, crypto agility, SBOM/CBOM delivery, and hybrid mode support. It provides a standardized way to compare vendors and identify risk.

Q: What does CBOM stand for and why is it important for PQC vendor assessment?
  A) Certified Business Operations Manual — tracks vendor certifications
  B) Cryptographic Bill of Materials — inventories all cryptographic components in a product
  C) Corporate Budget Operations Model — tracks vendor spending
  D) Compliance Board Oversight Matrix — documents governance roles
Answer: B
Explanation: A Cryptographic Bill of Materials (CBOM) lists all cryptographic algorithms, libraries, and protocols used in a product. It is essential for PQC assessment because it reveals which vendor components use quantum-vulnerable algorithms and need migration.

Q: True or False: If a vendor has FIPS 140-3 validation for their current products, they automatically support PQC algorithms.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. FIPS 140-3 validation certifies that a cryptographic module meets security requirements for its current algorithms. It does not guarantee PQC support. Vendors must separately implement, test, and obtain validation for PQC algorithms like ML-KEM and ML-DSA.

chunks.title: Quiz: vendor-risk
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: vendor-risk

Q: Which contract clauses should be included in vendor agreements regarding PQC? (Select all that apply)
  A) PQC migration timeline commitments
  B) CBOM delivery requirements
  C) Exclusive use of vendor's quantum computers
  D) Crypto change notification obligations
Answer: A|B|D
Explanation: Key PQC contract clauses include: migration timeline commitments (when PQC support will be available), CBOM delivery (for visibility into cryptographic components), and crypto change notifications (advance notice of algorithm changes). Quantum computer use is irrelevant to PQC.

Q: What is the primary supply chain risk in the PQC transition?
  A) Vendors raising prices for PQC products
  B) Critical infrastructure depending on vendors who lack PQC migration plans
  C) Too many vendors offering PQC solutions
  D) Open-source libraries being too secure
Answer: B
Explanation: The primary supply chain risk is dependency on vendors without PQC roadmaps — if critical infrastructure relies on products that won't support PQC in time, the organization cannot complete its migration regardless of internal readiness.

chunks.title: Quiz: vendor-risk
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: vendor-risk

Q: How should a vendor's FIPS validation status be categorized for PQC risk assessment?
  A) Two tiers: Validated or Not Validated (binary pass/fail)
  B) Three tiers: Validated (CMVP), Partial (FIPS-mode/FedRAMP/WebTrust), No validation
  C) Four tiers: FIPS 140-3 Validated, FIPS 140-3 Submitted, FIPS 140-2 Legacy, FIPS Mode/Self-Claim
  D) FIPS validation is irrelevant to PQC vendor risk
Answer: C
Explanation: The vendor risk module distinguishes four tiers: FIPS 140-3 Validated = CMVP-certified (highest assurance); FIPS 140-3 Submitted = in CMVP testing queue (commitment demonstrated but not yet certified); FIPS 140-2 Validated = legacy validation (NIST stopped accepting new 140-2 submissions September 2021, migration to 140-3 required); FIPS Mode/Self-Claim = vendor asserts FIPS-compatible operation with no CMVP certificate (lowest assurance). Each tier carries different procurement risk implications.

Q: What is crypto agility in the context of vendor assessment?
  A) How fast a vendor's encryption runs
  B) A vendor's ability to swap cryptographic algorithms in their products without major rework
  C) The agility of the vendor's sales team
  D) How quickly a vendor responds to support tickets
Answer: B
Explanation: Crypto agility in vendor products means the ability to update or replace cryptographic algorithms through configuration or minor updates rather than major product overhauls. Vendors with high crypto agility can adopt PQC algorithms faster with less disruption.

chunks.title: Quiz: vendor-risk
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: vendor-risk

Q: True or False: Organizations should assess their entire vendor ecosystem for PQC readiness, not just security product vendors.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: True. Cryptography is embedded throughout the technology stack — databases, messaging systems, cloud services, IoT devices, and more. Any vendor product that uses public-key cryptography for encryption, authentication, or integrity needs PQC assessment.

Q: What should an organization do if a critical vendor has no published PQC roadmap?
  A) Immediately terminate the vendor relationship
  B) Ignore the risk since the vendor will eventually update
  C) Engage the vendor formally, set contractual requirements, and develop contingency plans
  D) Wait for industry analysts to evaluate the vendor
Answer: C
Explanation: The best approach combines proactive engagement (request formal PQC roadmap, include requirements in contract renewals), risk mitigation (develop contingency plans, evaluate alternative vendors), and monitoring (track vendor progress). Abrupt termination or passive waiting both create unnecessary risk.

Q: What is a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) and why is it relevant to PQC vendor risk?
  A) A vendor's revenue report
  B) An inventory of all software components and dependencies in a product
  C) A benchmark of software performance metrics
  D) A document listing the vendor's employees
Answer: B
Explanation: An SBOM lists all software components, libraries, and dependencies in a product. For PQC, it helps identify which cryptographic libraries are in use, their algorithm support, and whether they include quantum-vulnerable implementations that need migration.

chunks.title: Quiz: Code Signing
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Code Signing

Q: Which supply chain attack compromised approximately 18,000 organizations by distributing malicious code inside a legitimately signed software update?
  A) Codecov (2021)
  B) 3CX (2023)
  C) SolarWinds (2020)
  D) Log4Shell (2021)
Answer: C
Explanation: The SolarWinds attack (2020) injected malicious code into the Orion platform build pipeline. The trojanized update was signed with SolarWinds' legitimate code-signing certificate, allowing it to bypass signature verification and propagate to ~18,000 customer networks before detection.

Q: What is the primary security advantage of Sigstore keyless signing compared to traditional certificate-based code signing?
  A) Signatures are smaller than classical ECDSA
  B) Ephemeral keys tied to OIDC identity eliminate long-lived private key management
  C) The signing process requires no cryptographic hash
  D) Keyless signing does not use certificates
Answer: B
Explanation: Sigstore issues short-lived ephemeral keys (~20 minute validity) bound to an OIDC identity. After signing, the private key is destroyed. The certificate and signing event are recorded in the Rekor transparency log. This eliminates the risk of private key theft or misuse that plagues traditional long-lived code signing certificates.

Q: True or False: CNSA 2.0 mandates ML-DSA-65 as the required algorithm for firmware signing in national security systems (NSS).
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. CNSA 2.0 mandates LMS/HSS (RFC 8554, NIST SP 800-208) for firmware and software signing in NSS — not ML-DSA. LMS offers a 56-byte public key and ~0.1 ms verification time, making it ideal for constrained firmware verification. ML-DSA is stateless and acceptable where LMS state management is impractical, but it is not the CNSA 2.0 mandate.

chunks.title: Quiz: Code Signing
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Code Signing

Q: How does an ML-DSA-65 signature compare in size to an ECDSA P-256 signature?
  A) Roughly the same size (~64 bytes)
  B) About 3x larger (~192 bytes)
  C) About 52x larger (~3,309 bytes)
  D) About 2x larger (~128 bytes)
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-DSA-65 (FIPS 204) produces 3,309-byte signatures versus ECDSA P-256's 64 bytes — approximately 52x larger. This has significant implications for certificate chain sizes, CI/CD pipeline bandwidth, and systems that embed signatures in package metadata. PQC signatures are 38-123x larger than classical ECDSA depending on the parameter set.

Q: Which package manager ecosystem adopted ML-DSA-87 + Ed448 hybrid composite signing as of RHEL 10?
  A) npm (via Sigstore)
  B) PyPI (via Sigstore)
  C) RPM (Red Hat)
  D) Maven Central
Answer: C
Explanation: Red Hat adopted ML-DSA-87+Ed448 hybrid composite signing in RHEL 10's RPM package manager. npm and PyPI integrate PQC via Sigstore keyless signing. Maven Central and APK (Android) have no published PQC roadmap as of 2025.

Q: What role does the Rekor transparency log play in the Sigstore signing ecosystem?
  A) It encrypts the signing private key for storage
  B) It is an immutable append-only log of signing events enabling auditability and retrospective verification
  C) It generates quantum-safe hash functions for artifact digests
  D) It automatically revokes expired code signing certificates
Answer: B
Explanation: Rekor is an immutable, append-only transparency log that records all Sigstore signing events — including certificates, signer identities, artifact digests, and timestamps. It enables any party to verify that a signature was produced at a specific time by a specific identity, and to detect whether certificates were issued or used after compromise.

chunks.title: Quiz: Code Signing
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Code Signing

Q: True or False: LMS/HSS is a stateless signature scheme, meaning private key state does not need to be tracked between signatures.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. LMS/HSS (NIST SP 800-208, RFC 8554) is stateful — each one-time key in the Merkle tree can only be used once. The signer must maintain a counter tracking which one-time keys have been consumed. If state is lost or the same key is reused, security is catastrophically compromised. State management typically uses an HSM or TPM with a monotonic counter.

Q: Which of the following are steps in a Sigstore keyless signing flow? (Select all that apply)
  A) OIDC identity verification with an identity provider
  B) Ephemeral ML-DSA key generation (valid ~20 minutes)
  C) Rekor transparency log entry recording the signing event
  D) HSM-bound persistent private key storage
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: Sigstore keyless signing: (1) OIDC authentication to prove identity, (2) ephemeral key generation (~20 minute validity), (3) Fulcio CA issues a short-lived certificate binding the key to the identity, (4) artifact is signed, (5) signing event is logged to Rekor, (6) private key is destroyed. There is deliberately NO persistent HSM storage — eliminating long-lived key compromise is the core security property.

chunks.title: Quiz: Code Signing
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Code Signing

Q: In a firmware signing certificate chain (Root CA → Intermediate CA → Code Signing cert), which ML-DSA parameter set should be used for the Root CA?
  A) ML-DSA-44 (NIST Level 2, 2,420 B signature)
  B) ML-DSA-65 (NIST Level 3, 3,309 B signature)
  C) ML-DSA-87 (NIST Level 5, 4,627 B signature)
  D) SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s (7,856 B signature)
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-DSA-87 (NIST Level 5) is used for Root CA certificates because Root CAs have 20-year validity periods requiring maximum security margin. Intermediate CAs use ML-DSA-65 (10-year validity). End-entity code signing certificates use ML-DSA-44 (1-year validity). Security level is matched to validity period: longer-lived certificates face longer exposure windows and need higher security parameters.

Q: What Extended Key Usage (EKU) OID designates a certificate for code signing?
  A) 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.1 (serverAuth)
  B) 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.2 (clientAuth)
  C) 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.3 (codeSigning)
  D) 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.4 (emailProtection)
Answer: C
Explanation: OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.3 designates a certificate for code signing. This EKU is required for certificates used to sign software packages, firmware, and executables. The same OID applies whether the certificate uses classical ECDSA or post-quantum ML-DSA — the EKU identifies the certificate's purpose, not the algorithm.

chunks.title: Quiz: API Security & JWT
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: API Security & JWT

Q: What are the three dot-separated sections of a JWT?
  A) Certificate, payload, signature
  B) Header, payload, signature
  C) Header, algorithm, payload
  D) Nonce, claims, timestamp
Answer: B
Explanation: A JWT consists of three base64url-encoded sections: Header (metadata including the algorithm, e.g., {"alg":"ES256","typ":"JWT"}), Payload (claims: sub, iss, exp, custom claims), and Signature (cryptographic proof). Format: BASE64URL(Header).BASE64URL(Payload).BASE64URL(Signature). The header's alg field must be updated when migrating to ML-DSA.

Q: A JWT signed with ML-DSA-65 is approximately how much larger than an equivalent ES256 (ECDSA P-256) JWT?
  A) 2x larger (~600 bytes)
  B) 5x larger (~1.5 KB)
  C) 16x larger (~4.7 KB)
  D) The same size (~300 bytes)
Answer: C
Explanation: An ML-DSA-65 JWT is approximately 4.7 KB versus ~300 bytes for ES256 — roughly 16x larger. ML-DSA-65 alone contributes a 1,952-byte public key and a 3,309-byte signature, dwarfing ECDSA P-256's 32-byte key and 64-byte signature. This size increase must be factored into HTTP header budgets, token storage, and API bandwidth planning.

Q: True or False: Ed25519 (EdDSA) used in JWTs is quantum-resistant and does not need to be migrated to PQC algorithms.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. Ed25519 is based on elliptic curve cryptography (Edwards curves) and is vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on a cryptographically-relevant quantum computer. All current JWT signing algorithms — RS256, ES256, and EdDSA — rely on RSA or elliptic curve mathematics and must be migrated to ML-DSA or equivalent post-quantum algorithms.

chunks.title: Quiz: API Security & JWT
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: API Security & JWT

Q: Which PQC algorithm replaces ECDH-ES for key agreement in PQC-enhanced JWE (JSON Web Encryption)?
  A) ML-DSA-65 (a signature algorithm, not key agreement)
  B) SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s
  C) ML-KEM-768
  D) XMSS
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-KEM-768 (FIPS 203, NIST Level 3) replaces ECDH-ES for key encapsulation in JWE. It produces a 1,184-byte public key and 1,088-byte ciphertext. The established shared secret is then used to encrypt content with AES-GCM. Key distinction: ML-DSA is for signatures (JWS), ML-KEM is for key encapsulation/encryption (JWE).

Q: What is the primary challenge PQC-signed JWTs create for OAuth 2.0 access tokens delivered in HTTP headers?
  A) Tokens expire too quickly with PQC
  B) Token refresh becomes computationally expensive
  C) PQC JWT sizes can exceed the 8 KB HTTP header limit enforced by many servers and proxies
  D) OAuth 2.0 does not support JWT-based access tokens
Answer: C
Explanation: An ML-DSA-65 access token JWT is approximately 4.7 KB. When access tokens, refresh tokens, and DPoP proofs are combined in HTTP headers, total size can approach or exceed the 8 KB header limit enforced by nginx, Apache, and most proxies. Mitigations include opaque tokens (introspection-based), smaller ML-DSA-44 parameters, or increasing server header size limits.

Q: In a JWT JOSE header, which field specifies the signing algorithm?
  A) typ
  B) kid
  C) alg
  D) iss
Answer: C
Explanation: The `alg` field in the JOSE header specifies the cryptographic algorithm used to sign the JWT — for example, "ES256" (ECDSA P-256), "EdDSA" (Ed25519), or "ML-DSA-65" for post-quantum. The `kid` (Key ID) identifies which key to use for verification, `typ` specifies the token type ("JWT"), and `iss` is a payload claim (issuer), not a header field.

chunks.title: Quiz: API Security & JWT
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: API Security & JWT

Q: True or False: DPoP proof tokens bound to ML-DSA keys add negligible overhead per API request because they are short-lived.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. Each DPoP proof (RFC 9449) is a fully signed JWT. With ML-DSA-65, each DPoP proof is approximately 4 KB. At high API call volumes (thousands of requests per second), this multiplies into substantial bandwidth and compute overhead compared to ~300-byte classical DPoP proofs. Optimizations include using the smaller ML-DSA-44 parameter set or hybrid signing strategies.

Q: Which OAuth 2.0 / OIDC components are significantly impacted by PQC signature sizes? (Select all that apply)
  A) Access tokens (JWS-signed JWTs)
  B) ID tokens (JWS-signed JWTs)
  C) JWKS public key endpoints (publish signing keys)
  D) Authorization codes (short-lived opaque values)
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: Access tokens, ID tokens (all JWS-signed JWTs), and JWKS endpoints (which publish signing public keys — each ML-DSA-65 key is ~1.9 KB) all grow significantly. Authorization codes are short-lived opaque random strings — they are not JWTs, contain no cryptographic material, and are completely unaffected by PQC algorithm sizes.

chunks.title: Quiz: API Security & JWT
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: API Security & JWT

Q: What is the recommended migration strategy for deploying PQC JWT signatures in a service mesh with mixed classical/PQC API consumers?
  A) Replace all tokens immediately with ML-DSA-65 (breaking change)
  B) Issue dual classical+PQC hybrid JWTs signed by both ES256 and ML-DSA-65
  C) Stop using JWTs entirely and switch to opaque tokens only
  D) Wait for IETF JOSE PQC RFC finalization before any migration
Answer: B
Explanation: Hybrid JWTs carry both a classical signature (ES256 or EdDSA for legacy consumers) and a PQC signature (ML-DSA-65 for quantum-safe consumers). This enables graceful transition: legacy clients verify the classical signature, PQC-aware clients verify both. This mirrors hybrid TLS key exchange — providing backward compatibility while establishing quantum security. Immediate cutover creates service outages; waiting indefinitely leaves HNDL exposure unaddressed.

Q: Which RFC defines JSON Web Signature (JWS) — the standard governing how JWT digital signatures are structured?
  A) RFC 7519 (JWT format)
  B) RFC 7515 (JWS)
  C) RFC 7516 (JWE)
  D) RFC 7518 (JWA algorithms)
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 7515 defines JSON Web Signature (JWS), specifying how digital signatures are applied to JWT payloads. RFC 7519 defines the JWT token format itself, RFC 7516 defines JSON Web Encryption (JWE) for encrypted tokens, and RFC 7518 defines the JSON Web Algorithms (JWA) registry — the catalog of alg values including "RS256", "ES256", and future "ML-DSA-65" entries.

chunks.title: Quiz: iot-ot-pqc
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: iot-ot-pqc

Q: According to RFC 7228, which constrained-device class is defined by approximately 10 KB RAM and 100 KB flash — examples being sensor tags and RFID devices?
  A) Class 1
  B) Class 0
  C) Class 2
  D) Class 3
Answer: B
Explanation: RFC 7228 Class 0 devices have ~10 KB RAM and ~100 KB flash — the most constrained category. Examples include RFID tags, environmental sensors, and asset trackers. Full TLS and most PQC algorithms are infeasible; communication typically occurs via gateway-mediated protocols or pre-shared keys established during device provisioning.

Q: Which PQC KEM is the most feasible option for Class 1-2 IoT devices with ~3-6 KB available RAM?
  A) ML-KEM-1024 (~10 KB RAM required)
  B) FrodoKEM-640 (~180 KB RAM required)
  C) ML-KEM-512 (~3 KB RAM required)
  D) ML-KEM-768 (~6 KB RAM required)
Answer: C
Explanation: ML-KEM-512 (NIST Level 1) requires approximately 3 KB RAM with 800-byte public keys and 768-byte ciphertext — the smallest ML-KEM variant and the only one feasible on Class 1 devices. FrodoKEM-640 requires 180 KB RAM, completely infeasible for IoT. ML-KEM-768 at ~6 KB RAM is borderline for Class 2 but exceeds Class 1 capacity.

Q: True or False: A PQC certificate chain using ML-DSA-65 is similar in size to a classical ECDSA P-256 chain and presents no significant challenge for constrained IoT devices.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. A classical ECDSA P-256 certificate chain is approximately 3 KB, while a PQC ML-DSA-65 chain grows to ~22 KB — over 7x larger. For Class 1 devices with only 10 KB total RAM, a 22 KB cert chain exceeds the entire device memory. Mitigations include Merkle Tree Certificates (85% reduction), certificate compression via RFC 8879, and TLS session resumption (PSK).

chunks.title: Quiz: iot-ot-pqc
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: iot-ot-pqc

Q: Which IoT communication protocol makes PQC key exchange fundamentally impossible due to a maximum payload size of 222 bytes?
  A) CoAP + DTLS 1.3 (1,024 B max payload)
  B) MQTT 5.0 + TLS 1.3 (65,535 B max payload)
  C) LoRaWAN 1.1 (222 B max payload)
  D) Matter / Thread (1,280 B IPv6 MTU)
Answer: C
Explanation: LoRaWAN 1.1 has a maximum payload of 222 bytes. ML-KEM-512's ciphertext alone is 768 bytes — more than 3x the maximum. PQC key exchange is impossible over LoRaWAN. IoT devices on LoRaWAN must use pre-shared keys (PSK) provisioned through a secure out-of-band channel, with QKD or PQC-protected key establishment occurring at the provisioning stage.

Q: In the Purdue Model for ICS/SCADA environments, which layers have the highest PQC migration priority due to internet-facing exposure and short asset lifecycles?
  A) Level 0 (Physical Process — 25-year lifecycle, air-gapped)
  B) Level 1-2 (Basic Control and Area Supervisory — isolated, 15-20 years)
  C) Level 3.5 / 4 / 5 (DMZ, Enterprise IT, Enterprise Network — internet-facing, 3-5 years)
  D) All Purdue Model levels have equal PQC priority
Answer: C
Explanation: Purdue Model levels 3.5 (DMZ), 4 (Enterprise IT), and 5 (Enterprise Network) are all internet-facing with 3-5 year asset lifecycles — the highest PQC migration priority. Levels 0-2 are air-gapped or isolated with 15-25 year lifecycles; they have lower immediate quantum risk and more constrained upgrade paths due to operational criticality and hardware age.

chunks.title: Quiz: iot-ot-pqc
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: iot-ot-pqc

Q: Which PQC signature algorithm has the smallest public key size (56 bytes) and is CNSA 2.0's mandated choice for firmware signing in national security systems?
  A) ML-DSA-44 (1,312-byte public key)
  B) FN-DSA-512 (FALCON, 897-byte public key)
  C) XMSS (68-byte public key)
  D) LMS (56-byte public key)
Answer: D
Explanation: LMS (Leighton-Micali Signatures, RFC 8554 / NIST SP 800-208) has a 56-byte public key — the smallest among all PQC signature schemes. Fast verification (~0.1 ms) and the tiny public key make it ideal for firmware verification on constrained devices. CNSA 2.0 mandates LMS/HSS for NSS firmware/software signing with a 2025 preference and 2030 mandate.

Q: True or False: FN-DSA (FALCON) produces the most compact PQC signatures among stateless schemes and is safe for deployment on Class 0 devices with 10 KB RAM.
Answer: FALSE
Explanation: False. FN-DSA-512 (FALCON) does produce the most compact stateless PQC signatures (~666 bytes vs ML-DSA-44's 2,420 bytes) and requires only ~1.5 KB RAM — suitable for Class 1-3. However, FALCON is NOT recommended for Class 0 devices because its Gaussian sampling algorithm requires careful floating-point or high-precision integer arithmetic, creating implementation complexity and timing side-channel risks on simple microcontrollers.

chunks.title: Quiz: iot-ot-pqc
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: iot-ot-pqc

Q: Which techniques can reduce PQC certificate chain sizes for constrained IoT devices? (Select all that apply)
  A) Merkle Tree Certificates (replace PQC sigs with ~300 B Merkle proofs, ~85% reduction)
  B) Certificate compression via RFC 8879 (zlib/Brotli, 25-35% reduction)
  C) TLS session resumption with PSK (RFC 8446, eliminates cert exchange on reconnect)
  D) Using larger RSA-8192 certificates for stronger forward secrecy
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: Three viable mitigations exist: (1) Merkle Tree Certificates replace PQC signatures with ~300-byte Merkle inclusion proofs (85% reduction); (2) RFC 8879 certificate compression uses zlib/Brotli on the TLS handshake (25-35% reduction); (3) TLS PSK session resumption (RFC 8446) eliminates the full certificate exchange on subsequent connections (90% reduction). Larger RSA certificates make the problem dramatically worse.

chunks.title: Quiz: iot-ot-pqc
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: iot-ot-pqc

Q: A Class 1 smart meter (10 KB RAM, NB-IoT narrow-band) needs to authenticate to a grid operator via DTLS 1.3. What is the recommended PQC approach?
  A) Full ML-KEM-768 + ML-DSA-65 DTLS handshake on every connection
  B) Pre-shared key (PSK) provisioned at manufacturing with PQC-derived keys and session resumption on reconnect
  C) No PQC is needed since smart meters communicate over private cellular networks
  D) Use FrodoKEM-640 for maximum security against quantum attacks
Answer: B
Explanation: For Class 1 devices on NB-IoT (low bandwidth, low power), a full PQC DTLS handshake (~22 KB exchange, 15+ IPv6 fragments) is impractical. PSK provisioned at manufacturing (using PQC key derivation) with DTLS session resumption on subsequent connections eliminates the heavyweight handshake. Smart meters are NOT isolated — they use cellular networks exposed to HNDL attacks. FrodoKEM-640 requires 180 KB RAM, completely exceeding Class 1 capacity.

Q: What does the SUIT Manifest (RFC 9019) provide for IoT firmware security?
  A) A quantum-safe encryption algorithm for firmware images
  B) A signed metadata wrapper enabling secure over-the-air (OTA) firmware delivery for constrained devices
  C) A new certificate format replacing X.509 for IoT
  D) A network bootstrap protocol for provisioning IoT devices
Answer: B
Explanation: The SUIT (Software Updates for Internet of Things) Manifest (RFC 9019) wraps firmware with cryptographically signed metadata: version, component identity, payload digest, size, and conditions (vendor ID, class ID). It enables constrained devices to securely receive OTA firmware updates, verifying authenticity and integrity before installation — without requiring full TLS infrastructure. SUIT manifests are signed with LMS or ML-DSA depending on device class.

chunks.title: Quiz: Data & Asset Sensitivity
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Data & Asset Sensitivity

Q: In the four-tier data sensitivity classification model, which tier requires the highest level of PQC migration urgency?
  A) Low — publicly available data with no confidentiality requirements
  B) Medium — internal data protected by standard controls
  C) High — sensitive regulated data under GDPR, HIPAA, or PCI DSS
  D) Critical — national security, classified data, long-lived cryptographic key material
Answer: D
Explanation: The Critical tier encompasses national security data, classified information, and long-lived cryptographic key material. These assets face the highest HNDL (Harvest Now, Decrypt Later) risk and require immediate PQC migration prioritization.

Q: True or False: HNDL (Harvest Now, Decrypt Later) means adversaries can collect encrypted data today and decrypt it later once a CRQC becomes available.
Answer: TRUE
Explanation: HNDL is a real and active threat. Nation-state adversaries are already harvesting encrypted network traffic with the intent to decrypt it once a cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC) exists. Data with long retention periods or high sensitivity value is especially at risk.

Q: In Mosca's Theorem (X + Y > Z), what does Y represent?
  A) The number of years until a CRQC is available
  B) The time required to migrate cryptographic systems to PQC
  C) The retention period of sensitive data
  D) The security lifetime of the current cryptographic algorithm
Answer: B
Explanation: In Mosca's Theorem, X = data shelf life (how long data must remain confidential), Y = migration time (how long your PQC migration will take), and Z = time until CRQC arrival. If X + Y > Z, you are at risk and must begin migration immediately.

chunks.title: Quiz: Data & Asset Sensitivity
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Data & Asset Sensitivity

Q: How many steps are in the NIST Risk Management Framework (SP 800-37 Rev. 2), and what is the first step?
  A) 5 steps, starting with Identify
  B) 6 steps, starting with Categorize
  C) 7 steps, starting with Prepare
  D) 8 steps, starting with Assess
Answer: C
Explanation: The NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2) has 7 steps: Prepare → Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor. The Prepare step (added in Rev. 2) establishes organizational context and risk tolerance before categorization begins.

Q: Which EU regulation specifically targets the operational resilience of financial entities and includes ICT risk management requirements relevant to PQC migration?
  A) GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)
  B) NIS2 Directive
  C) DORA (Digital Operational Resilience Act)
  D) eIDAS 2.0
Answer: C
Explanation: DORA (Digital Operational Resilience Act) applies specifically to financial entities in the EU and requires robust ICT risk management including cryptographic resilience planning. It defines 5 ICT risk management pillars. NIS2 covers a broader range of critical sectors but DORA is the primary instrument for financial sector PQC compliance in the EU.

chunks.title: Quiz: Data & Asset Sensitivity
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Data & Asset Sensitivity

Q: An organization stores health records that must be retained for 20 years. If PQC migration will take 5 years and a CRQC is estimated to arrive in 10 years, what does Mosca's Theorem indicate?
  A) No immediate action needed — Z (10 years) exceeds Y (5 years)
  B) Urgent risk — X + Y (25 years) exceeds Z (10 years), migration must start now
  C) Low risk — X (20 years) and Y (5 years) are independent variables
  D) Moderate risk — only data created after CRQC arrival needs protection
Answer: B
Explanation: X (data shelf life) = 20 years, Y (migration time) = 5 years, Z (time to CRQC) = 10 years. Since X + Y = 25 > Z = 10, the organization is already in a risk window and must begin migration immediately. HNDL attacks can harvest today's data for decryption post-CRQC.

Q: For a Critical-tier asset requiring both confidentiality and authentication, which PQC algorithm combination is most appropriate?
  A) ML-KEM-512 for key encapsulation; ML-DSA-44 for digital signatures
  B) ML-KEM-1024 for key encapsulation; ML-DSA-87 for digital signatures
  C) SLH-DSA for key encapsulation; ML-KEM-768 for digital signatures
  D) FrodoKEM for key encapsulation; FN-DSA-512 for digital signatures
Answer: B
Explanation: Critical-tier assets warrant the highest security levels: ML-KEM-1024 (NIST security level 5, equivalent to AES-256) for key encapsulation and ML-DSA-87 (NIST security level 5) for signatures. ML-KEM-512/ML-DSA-44 are security level 1 and appropriate for lower sensitivity tiers. SLH-DSA is a signature scheme, not a KEM.

chunks.title: Quiz: Data & Asset Sensitivity
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Data & Asset Sensitivity

Q: In the FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) model, what does Loss Event Frequency (LEF) depend on?
  A) Asset value multiplied by threat actor capability
  B) Threat Event Frequency (TEF) and Vulnerability (the probability that a threat event results in loss)
  C) Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO) divided by Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)
  D) Primary loss magnitude plus secondary risk magnitude
Answer: B
Explanation: In FAIR v3.0, Loss Event Frequency (LEF) = Threat Event Frequency (TEF) × Vulnerability. TEF is how often a threat actor acts against an asset; Vulnerability is the conditional probability that the contact results in a loss event given the threat actor's capability vs. the asset's resistance strength. LEF is then combined with Loss Magnitude to calculate Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) in USD.

Q: ISO 27005:2022 introduces a dual risk assessment approach. Which two methods does it combine?
  A) Quantitative (monetary) and qualitative (color-coded) risk assessment
  B) Event-based and asset-based risk assessment approaches
  C) Top-down (executive) and bottom-up (technical) threat modeling
  D) Inherent risk and residual risk scoring models
Answer: B
Explanation: ISO 27005:2022 introduces a dual approach combining event-based risk assessment (focused on threat scenarios and their potential consequences) with asset-based risk assessment (focused on asset value and vulnerability). This allows organizations to model risks from both attacker-centric and asset-centric perspectives, producing a more complete 5×5 likelihood × consequence risk matrix.

chunks.title: Quiz: Data & Asset Sensitivity
chunks.content:
Quiz Category: Data & Asset Sensitivity

Q: Which of the following factors are included in the composite PQC migration priority scoring model? (Select all that apply)
  A) Data sensitivity tier (Low / Medium / High / Critical)
  B) Data retention period and HNDL risk window
  C) Applicable compliance mandates and their enforcement deadlines
  D) Physical location of data center servers
Answer: A|B|C
Explanation: The composite scoring model weighs four dimensions: (1) sensitivity tier, (2) data retention period and resulting HNDL risk window, (3) applicable compliance mandates with their deadlines. Physical data center location does not directly factor into PQC migration priority scoring — what matters is the sensitivity of the data and the regulatory obligations that apply to it, not where it is physically stored.

chunks.title: Assessment: retention
chunks.content:
Assessment Category: retention

- Less than 1 year: Data that needs to stay confidential for under a year

- 1-5 years: Data with a short to medium confidentiality window

- 5-10 years: Data requiring a medium to long-term confidentiality window
  Retention: 10 years

- 10-25 years: Data requiring long-term confidentiality protection
  Retention: 25 years

- 25+ years: Data with very long or archival confidentiality requirements

- Indefinite: Data with no defined expiration of confidentiality

chunks.title: Assessment: Industry Selection
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 1: Industry Selection

The Industry Selection step determines which compliance frameworks, threat scenarios, and migration priorities are relevant to your organization. Different industries face varying levels of quantum risk — for example, Financial Services and Government/Defense face the highest urgency due to long-lived data and regulatory mandates.

chunks.title: Assessment: Country Selection
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 2: Country Selection

The Country Selection step identifies which national PQC mandates and deadlines apply. Countries like the United States (CNSA 2.0), France (ANSSI), and Germany (BSI) have specific PQC migration timelines. This selection also filters compliance frameworks to show only relevant regulations.

chunks.title: Assessment: Current Cryptographic Usage
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 3: Current Cryptographic Usage

The Current Cryptographic Usage step identifies which classical algorithms your organization uses (RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, AES, SHA-2, etc.). This determines which PQC replacements are needed — RSA/ECDSA require ML-DSA or SLH-DSA for signatures, while ECDH requires ML-KEM for key exchange.

chunks.title: Assessment: Data Sensitivity
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 4: Data Sensitivity

Data Sensitivity drives urgency assessment. Organizations handling Top Secret, classified, financial, or health data face higher HNDL (Harvest Now, Decrypt Later) risk because adversaries may already be collecting encrypted data for future quantum decryption. Multiple sensitivity levels can be selected — the highest level determines the risk score.

chunks.title: Assessment: Compliance Frameworks
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 5: Compliance Frameworks

The Compliance step identifies which regulatory frameworks apply to your organization (CNSA 2.0, NIST guidelines, ANSSI requirements, BSI recommendations, etc.). Frameworks are filtered by your selected industry and country. Each framework has different PQC adoption deadlines and requirements.

chunks.title: Assessment: Migration Status
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 6: Migration Status

The Migration Status step assesses how far along your organization is in the PQC transition: Not Started, Planning, Pilot/Testing, Partial Deployment, or Fully Migrated. Organizations in earlier stages receive higher urgency scores to encourage action.

chunks.title: Assessment: Use Cases
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 7: Use Cases

The Use Cases step identifies specific cryptographic applications in your organization: TLS/HTTPS, VPN/IPsec, email signing, code signing, PKI/certificates, IoT device authentication, database encryption, etc. Each use case maps to specific PQC algorithms and migration complexity.

chunks.title: Assessment: Data Retention
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 8: Data Retention

Data Retention periods directly impact HNDL risk exposure. Data that must remain confidential for 10+ years (e.g., health records, state secrets, financial archives) faces the highest quantum threat since quantum computers could decrypt it within its retention window. Multiple retention levels can be selected.

chunks.title: Assessment: Organization Scale
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 9: Organization Scale

Organization Scale affects migration complexity and timeline. Large enterprises with thousands of endpoints, multiple data centers, and complex supply chains require longer migration timelines and more comprehensive crypto-agility frameworks than smaller organizations.

chunks.title: Assessment: Crypto Agility
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 10: Crypto Agility

Crypto Agility measures your organization's ability to quickly swap cryptographic algorithms. Organizations with centralized key management, automated certificate rotation, and modular crypto libraries can migrate faster. Low agility increases migration risk and timeline.

chunks.title: Assessment: Infrastructure Assessment
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 11: Infrastructure Assessment

The Infrastructure step evaluates which systems need PQC upgrades: HSMs, load balancers, firewalls, certificate authorities, databases, cloud services, IoT devices. Hardware-bound systems (HSMs, embedded devices) require longer migration timelines due to firmware/hardware replacement cycles.

chunks.title: Assessment: Vendor Dependencies
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 12: Vendor Dependencies

Vendor Dependencies identifies third-party products and services in your cryptographic supply chain. Organizations dependent on vendor timelines for PQC support face additional risk. The Migrate Catalog can help identify PQC-ready alternatives.

chunks.title: Assessment: Target Timeline
chunks.content:
PQC Assessment Wizard — Step 13: Target Timeline

The Target Timeline step sets your organization's PQC migration deadline based on regulatory requirements, risk tolerance, and industry benchmarks. Country-aligned options show relevant national deadlines (e.g., CNSA 2.0 2030/2033 milestones, ANSSI 2025 hybrid requirement).

chunks.title: Getting Started for Developers
chunks.content:
Getting Started with PQC for Developers

Start with the PQC 101 module to understand the quantum threat and why migration matters. Then explore ML-KEM key generation in the Playground — you can generate real PQC keypairs in your browser. The TLS Basics module shows how ML-KEM integrates with TLS 1.3 handshakes. For hands-on practice, OpenSSL Studio provides a full WASM-based OpenSSL 3.6 terminal for generating PQC keys and certificates. The Algorithm Reference page compares all NIST-standardized algorithms with performance benchmarks.

chunks.title: Getting Started for Organizations
chunks.content:
Getting Started with PQC for Organizations

Begin with the Assessment wizard to evaluate your organization's quantum risk posture — it analyzes industry, data sensitivity, compliance requirements, and infrastructure to generate a prioritized migration plan. Review the Compliance page for regulatory frameworks (CNSA 2.0, ANSSI, BSI guidelines) and their deadlines. The Migrate Catalog lists PQC-ready products across 7 infrastructure layers, including HSMs, TLS libraries, and certificate authorities. The Threat Landscape page shows industry-specific quantum risks to help build the business case.

chunks.title: Getting Started for Learners
chunks.content:
Getting Started with PQC for Learners

The Learn section has 27 modules covering PQC fundamentals to advanced topics. Start with PQC 101 for an overview, then Quantum Threats to understand Shor's and Grover's algorithms. Key modules include: Hybrid Cryptography (transition strategy), Crypto Agility (algorithm flexibility), TLS Basics (web security), KMS & PQC, HSM & PQC Operations, Data & Asset Sensitivity, 5G Security, and an executive track (Governance, Business Case, Risk Management, Compliance Strategy, Migration Program). Each module includes interactive demonstrations and a Workshop tab for hands-on exercises. Test your knowledge with the Quiz covering 530 questions across 33 categories. The Glossary provides definitions for 100+ PQC terms.

chunks.title: PQC Playground — Interactive Crypto Demos
chunks.content:
PQC Playground Overview

The PQC Playground is an interactive browser-based tool for generating real post-quantum cryptographic keys, encrypting data, and signing messages. All operations run locally in the browser using WebAssembly (WASM) — no data leaves your machine. Available operations: Key Generation, Encryption/Decryption (KEM), and Digital Signatures.

Supported algorithms: ML-KEM-512/768/1024 (key encapsulation), ML-DSA-44/65/87 (digital signatures), SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s/192s/256s (hash-based signatures), X25519 (classical ECDH), P-256 (classical ECDSA), RSA-2048/4096 (classical). All generated keys are for educational purposes only — not for production use.

chunks.title: Playground — Key Generation
chunks.content:
Key Generation in the PQC Playground

Select any algorithm to generate a keypair instantly in your browser. The playground shows public key size, private key size, and generation time for each algorithm. Compare PQC key sizes with classical equivalents — ML-KEM-768 public keys are 1,184 bytes vs RSA-2048 at 256 bytes, while ML-DSA-65 public keys are 1,952 bytes vs ECDSA P-256 at 64 bytes. Use the algorithm selector dropdown to switch between algorithms, or use the URL parameter: /playground?algo=ML-KEM.

chunks.title: Playground — Key Encapsulation (KEM)
chunks.content:
Key Encapsulation in the PQC Playground

ML-KEM (FIPS 203) key encapsulation generates a shared secret between two parties. The playground demonstrates: 1) Generate a keypair, 2) Encapsulate — create a ciphertext + shared secret using the public key, 3) Decapsulate — recover the shared secret using the private key. Compare ciphertext sizes: ML-KEM-768 produces 1,088-byte ciphertexts vs X25519 at 32 bytes. The shared secret is always 32 bytes regardless of parameter set.

chunks.title: Playground — Digital Signatures
chunks.content:
Digital Signatures in the PQC Playground

ML-DSA (FIPS 204) and SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) digital signatures can be generated and verified in the playground. Enter a message, sign it with a private key, then verify the signature with the public key. Compare signature sizes: ML-DSA-65 signatures are 3,309 bytes, SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s signatures are 7,856 bytes, vs ECDSA P-256 at 64 bytes. ML-DSA is faster but SLH-DSA is based on hash functions only (conservative security assumption).

chunks.title: OpenSSL Studio — Browser-Based WASM Terminal
chunks.content:
OpenSSL Studio Overview

OpenSSL Studio provides a full OpenSSL 3.6.0 terminal running in the browser via WebAssembly (WASM). It supports PQC algorithms through the OQS provider, enabling hands-on practice with post-quantum key generation, certificate creation, and cryptographic operations without installing anything. All operations execute locally in the browser with SharedArrayBuffer support.

chunks.title: OpenSSL Studio — PQC Key Generation Commands
chunks.content:
PQC Key Generation with OpenSSL Studio

Generate PQC keys using modern OpenSSL 3.x commands:
- ML-KEM: openssl genpkey -algorithm mlkem768 -out mlkem768_key.pem
- ML-DSA: openssl genpkey -algorithm mldsa65 -out mldsa65_key.pem
- SLH-DSA: openssl genpkey -algorithm slhdsa-sha2-128s -out slhdsa_key.pem
- Extract public key: openssl pkey -in key.pem -pubout -out pub.pem

Use genpkey (not genrsa/ecparam) — modern OpenSSL commands support all PQC algorithms through the OQS provider.

chunks.title: OpenSSL Studio — PQC Certificate Operations
chunks.content:
PQC Certificate Operations with OpenSSL Studio

Create PQC certificates and CSRs:
- Self-signed cert: openssl req -x509 -new -key mldsa65_key.pem -out cert.pem -days 365 -subj "/CN=PQC Test"
- CSR: openssl req -new -key mldsa65_key.pem -out csr.pem -subj "/CN=PQC Test"
- Verify cert: openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout
- Sign data: openssl pkeyutl -sign -inkey mldsa65_key.pem -in data.txt -out sig.bin
- Verify signature: openssl pkeyutl -verify -pubin -inkey pub.pem -in data.txt -sigfile sig.bin

All certificates use ML-DSA-65 or other PQC algorithms for signing, demonstrating post-quantum PKI workflows.

chunks.title: Related Certificates for Use in Multiple Authentications within a Protocol — Document Analysis
chunks.content:
Title: Related Certificates for Use in Multiple Authentications within a Protocol
Authors: IETF LAMPS; Alison Becker (NSA); Rebecca Guthrie (NSA); Michael Jenkins (NSA)
Publication Date: 2022-03-21
Last Updated: 2025-06-13
Document Status: Proposed Standard
Main Topic: Enabling non-composite hybrid authentication using separate traditional and post-quantum cryptography (PQC) certificates
Protocols Covered: X.509
Infrastructure Layers: PKI
Standardization Bodies: IETF

chunks.title: A Mechanism for Encoding Differences in Paired Certificates (Chameleon Certs) — Document Analysis
chunks.content:
Title: A Mechanism for Encoding Differences in Paired Certificates (Chameleon Certs)
Authors: IETF LAMPS; Corey Bonnell (DigiCert); John Gray (Entrust); D. Hook (KeyFactor); Tomofumi Okubo (DigiCert); Mike Ounsworth (Entrust)
Publication Date: 2023-05-31
Last Updated: 2025-10-18
Document Status: Active Internet-Draft
Main Topic: An X.509 v3 certificate extension that allows a relying party to construct both paired certificates with a single certificate.
PQC Algorithms Covered: ML-DSA-65
Protocols Covered: X.509, PKCS #10
Infrastructure Layers: PKI
Standardization Bodies: IETF

chunks.title: Profile for Use of SLH-DSA in the Internet X.509 PKI — Document Analysis
chunks.content:
Title: Profile for Use of SLH-DSA in the Internet X.509 PKI
Authors: IETF LAMPS; Jake Massimo (AWS); Scott Fluhrer (Cisco)
Publication Date: 2025-10-01
Last Updated: 2025-10-01

Main Topic: E-commerce payment flow quantum vulnerability: PCI DSS 4.0.1 requires strong cryptography for online payment processing but TLS protecting checkout flows uses RSA/ECDSA key exchange vulnerable to quantum attacks.
